title: Lattice QCD two-point correlators and excited-state (single-/two-state) fits

recall_papers.title:
Searching for the first radial excitation of the $\Delta(1232)$ in
  lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD analysis of the $ \Delta $-baryon spectrum, with the
goal of finding the position of the $ 2s $ radial excitation of the $
\Delta(1232) $ ground state. Using smeared three-quark operators in a
correlation matrix analysis, we report masses for the ground, first and second
excited states of the $ J^P = 3/2^+ $ spectrum across a broad range of $
m_\pi^2 $. We identify the lowest lying state as being a $ 1s $ state,
consistent with the well known $ \Delta(1232) $. The first excitation is
identified as a $ 2s $ state, but is found to have a mass of approximately
2.15~GeV on our $ \sim3 $ fm lattice, which does not appear to be associated
with the $ \Delta(1600) $ resonance in a significant manner. We also report on
the spin-$ 1/2 $ and odd-parity states accessible via our methods. The large
excitation energies of the radial excitations provide a potential resolution to
the long-standing missing baryon resonances problem.

sections.6: Gell-Mann, The Eightfold Way: A Theory of strong interaction symmetry 10.2172/4008239 (1961).
sections.7: Luscher, Volume Dependence of the Energy Spectrum in Massive Quantum Field Theories
sections.8: Michael, Adjoint Sources in Lattice Gauge Theory, Nucl
sections.12: Michael, Adjoint Sources in Lattice Gauge Theory, Nucl
sections.16: Parisi, The Strategy for Computing the Hadronic Mass Spectrum, Phys

authors: Liam Hockley, Waseem Kamleh, Derek Leinweber, Anthony Thomas

qualitative: Lattice QCD uses two-point correlation functions constructed from three-quark interpolating fields to extract baryon masses and excited-state spectra.
The variational method is employed to extract multiple excited states from a correlation matrix, enabling the identification of ground and excited states in the Δ(1232) spectrum.

Smeared three-quark operators are used to improve overlap with physical states and allow for the classification of eigenstates based on radial excitation patterns, such as the number of nodes in the wave function.
Spin- and parity-projected correlation functions are constructed to isolate specific quantum states (e.g., J^P = 3/2^+, 3/2^−, 1/2^±) within the baryon spectrum.
The identification of radial excitations relies on the nodal structure of the wave function, with the first radial excitation (2s state) expected to have one node in the radial wave function.
Excited-state fits in lattice QCD typically involve single-state or two-state fits to correlation functions to extract energy levels, with the choice of fit model affecting systematic uncertainties.

The presence of multiple open scattering channels at higher energies necessitates careful treatment of finite-volume effects and the use of methods like Lüscher's formalism to connect finite-volume energies to infinite-volume resonances.
quantitative: The first radial excitation of the Δ(1232) is identified as a 2s state with a mass of approximately 2.15 GeV on a lattice with spatial extent ~3 fm.
The ground state is identified as a 1s state, consistent with the Δ(1232) at ~1.232 GeV.
The study reports masses for the ground, first, and second excited states of the J^P = 3/2^+ spectrum across a broad range of m_π².
The lattice volume is approximately 3 fm in spatial extent, implying finite-volume effects that must be accounted for in interpreting the results.
The analysis uses a correlation matrix constructed from smeared three-quark interpolating fields, with the variational method applied to extract energy levels from the eigenvalues of the matrix.

recall_papers.title: Bayesian model averaging for analysis of lattice field theory results
recall_papers.abstract:
Statistical modeling is a key component in the extraction of physical results
from lattice field theory calculations. Although the general models used are
often strongly motivated by physics, many model variations can frequently be
considered for the same lattice data. Model averaging, which amounts to a
probability-weighted average over all model variations, can incorporate
systematic errors associated with model choice without being overly
conservative. We discuss the framework of model averaging from the perspective
of Bayesian statistics, and give useful formulae and approximations for the
particular case of least-squares fitting, commonly used in modeling lattice
results. In addition, we frame the common problem of data subset selection
(e.g. choice of minimum and maximum time separation for fitting a two-point
correlation function) as a model selection problem and study model averaging as
a straightforward alternative to manual selection of fit ranges. Numerical
examples involving both mock and real lattice data are given.

sections.6: Morningstar, Bayesian curve fitting for lattice gauge theorists, Nucl
sections.7: Chang et al., A per-cent-level determination of the nucleon axial coupling from quantum chromodynamics, Nature 558, 91 (2018), arXiv:1805.12130 [hep-lat] .
sections.11: Takeuchi, Distribution of information statistics and criteria for adequacy of models, Suri-Kagaku (Mathematical Sciences) 153, 12 (1976).
sections.12: Akaike, Information theory and an extension of the maximum likelihood principle, in Selected papers of hirotugu akaike (Springer, 1998) pp
sections.13: Stone, An asymptotic equivalence of choice of model by cross-validation and akaike’s criterion, Journal of the Royal Statistical Society
sections.14: Jeffreys, The theory of probability (OUP Oxford, 1998).
sections.15: Akaike, Information theory and an extension of the maximum likelihood principle, in Selected papers of hirotugu akaike (Springer, 1998) pp
sections.16: Takeuchi, Distribution of information statistics and criteria for adequacy of models, Suri-Kagaku (Mathematical Sciences) 153, 12 (1976).
sections.17: Borsanyi et al., Leading hadronic contribution to the muon 2 magnetic moment from lattice QCD, Nature 593, 51 (2021), arXiv:2002.12347 [hep-lat] .
sections.22: Anscombe, Graphs in statistical analysis, The American Statistician 27, 17 (1973), https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/pdf/10.1080/00031305.1973.10478966 .

authors: William I. Jay and Ethan T. Neil

qualitative: Model averaging provides a rigorous Bayesian framework to account for systematic errors due to model choice in lattice QCD analyses, particularly in fitting two-point correlation functions.

The choice of fit range (e.g., minimum and maximum time separation) in two-point correlator fits is equivalent to a model selection problem, and model averaging offers a principled alternative to manual data subset selection.

Excited-state contributions in two-point correlators are inherently infinite in number (due to spectral decomposition), necessitating truncation; model averaging helps quantify the uncertainty from such truncation without being overly conservative.

Bayesian model averaging combines results from multiple models (e.g., single-state vs. two-state fits) by weighting them according to their posterior probabilities, allowing for coherent uncertainty propagation.

The approach does not require models to be nested, making it flexible for comparing physically motivated but structurally different models (e.g., different numbers of exponentials in a correlator fit).

The commonly used practice of estimating systematic errors via the full variation across different fit ranges is often overly conservative; model averaging provides a more nuanced and statistically sound alternative.
The method is applicable beyond linear models and can be used for nonlinear least-squares fits typical in lattice QCD, including chiral and continuum extrapolations.
quantitative: Model weight approximation: $ \text{pr}(M|D) \approx \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\chi^2_{\text{aug}}(\mathbf{a}^*) + 2k + 2N_{\text{cut}}\right)\right] $, where $ \chi^2_{\text{aug}}(\mathbf{a}^*) $ is the augmented chi-squared, $ k $ is the number of fit parameters, and $ N_{\text{cut}} $ is the number of data points removed.
The model weight is derived under uniform model priors and is valid to leading order in the data sample size $ N $.
The augmented chi-squared $ \chi^2_{\text{aug}} $ accounts for both the fit quality and the complexity of the model, incorporating parameter uncertainty and data truncation.
The penalty term $ 2k + 2N_{\text{cut}} $ reflects the cost of model complexity and data truncation, balancing goodness-of-fit against overfitting and bias.

Model averaging allows for the computation of model-averaged expectation values of parameters of interest, combining results from multiple models (e.g., single-state and two-state fits) in a probability-weighted manner.

recall_papers.title:
Progress report on testing robustness of the Newton method in data
  analysis on 2-point correlation function using a MILC HISQ ensemble
recall_papers.abstract:
We report recent progress in data analysis on the two point correlation
functions which will be prerequisite to obtain semileptonic form factors for
the $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}\ell\nu$ decays. We use a MILC HISQ ensemble for the
measurement. We use the HISQ action for light quarks, and the Oktay-Kronfeld
(OK) action for the heavy quarks ($b$ and $c$). We used a sequential Bayesian
method for the data analysis. Here we test the new fitting methodology of
Benjamin J.~Choi in a completely independent manner.

sections.4: Amhis et al., Averages of b𝑏b-hadron, c𝑐c-hadron, and τ𝜏\tau-lepton properties as of 2021, 2206.07501.

authors:
Tanmoy Bhattacharya, Benjamin J. Choi, Rajan Gupta, Yong-Chull Jang,
  Seungyeob Jwa, Sunghee Kim, Sunkyu Lee, Weonjong Lee, Jaehoon Leem, Jeonghwan
  Pak, Sungwoo Park

qualitative: Two-point correlation functions in lattice QCD are essential for extracting semileptonic form factors in B→D(∗)ℓν decays, which probe the CKM matrix element |Vcb|.

The spectral decomposition of 2-point correlation functions includes contributions from both even and odd time-parity states due to the staggered fermion formalism, with odd-parity terms arising from temporal doublers.

The n+m fitting function accounts for n even time-parity states and m odd time-parity states, incorporating exponential decay terms with energy splittings ΔEi = Ei − Ei−2 and relative amplitudes ri = Ai/Ai−2.

The Newton method combined with a scanning method is used to robustly find initial parameter guesses for fitting, leveraging the fast convergence of Newton's method within a narrow range identified by the slower but reliable scanning method.

A multi-dimensional Newton method is applied after the scanning method narrows down the parameter space, ensuring efficient convergence to a global minimum when initial guesses are sufficiently close.
Time slice combinations used in the fitting must include tmin, and have equal numbers of even and odd time slices to maintain symmetry and avoid bias in the fit.
The fitting procedure involves testing multiple time slice combinations, discarding those that fail to converge within a few iterations (7–10), with a failure rate of about 70% for 1+1 fits.
The stopping criterion for the Newton method is max_i |q(ti)| < 10^−12, where q(ti) = (f(ti) − C(ti))/C(ti), ensuring high precision in the fit residuals.
quantitative: The 2-point correlation function is given by: C(t) = ∑_{n=0}^∞ (−1)^n (t+1) |⟨n|𝒪|0⟩|^2 (e^{-E_n t} + e^{-E_n (T−t)}).

The n+m fit function is: fn+m(t) = gn+m(t) + gn+m(T−t), where gn+m(t) = A0 e^{-E0 t} [1 + r2 e^{-ΔE2 t} (1 + ⋯ (1 + r_{2n−2} e^{-ΔE_{2n−2} t})⋯) − (−1)^t r1 e^{-ΔE1 t} (1 + ⋯ (1 + r_{2m−1} e^{-ΔE_{2m−1} t})⋯)].
The parameters are defined as: ri = Ai / Ai−2, ΔEi = Ei − Ei−2 for i ≥ 1, with E_{-1} = E0 and A_{-1} = A0.
For the 1+1 fit, the number of equations is N = 2(n+m) = 4.
The scanning method uses initial ranges: r1 ∈ [0, 1.5], ΔE1 ∈ [0, 1.0].
The Newton method typically converges in 7–10 iterations when successful.
The failure rate for the 1+1 fit is approximately 70%.
The stopping condition is: max_{i=1,…,N} |q(ti)| < 10^{-12}, where q(ti) = (f(ti) − C(ti)) / C(ti).

recall_papers.title: $\Xi_c-\Xi_c^{\prime}$ mixing From Lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
In heavy quark limit, the lowest-lying charmed baryons with two light quarks
can form an SU(3) triplet and sextet. The $\Xi_c$ in the SU(3) triplet and
$\Xi_c'$ in the sextet have the same $J^{PC}$ quantum number and can mix due to
the finite charm quark mass and the fact the strange quark is heavier than the
up/down quark. We explore the $\Xi_c$-$\Xi_c'$ mixing by calculating the
two-point correlation functions of the $\Xi_c$ and $\Xi_c'$ baryons from
lattice QCD. Based on the lattice data, we adopt two independent methods to
determine the mixing angle between $\Xi_c$ and $\Xi_c'$. After making the
chiral and continuum extrapolation, it is found that the mixing angle $\theta$
is $1.2^{\circ}\pm0.1^{\circ}$, which seems insufficient to account for the
large SU(3) symmetry breaking effects found in weak decays of charmed baryons.
authors:
Hang Liu, Liuming Liu, Peng Sun, Wei Sun, Jin-Xin Tan, Wei Wang, Yi-Bo
  Yang, Qi-An Zhang

qualitative: In the heavy quark limit, charmed baryons with two light quarks form an SU(3) triplet (Ξ_c) and sextet (Ξ_c'), which can mix due to finite charm quark mass and SU(3) flavor symmetry breaking from the heavier strange quark.
The Ξ_c–Ξ_c' mixing is studied using lattice QCD through two-point correlation functions of interpolating operators for the triplet and sextet states.
Two independent methods are employed to determine the mixing angle: direct fitting of energy levels and solving the generalized eigenvalue problem (lattice spectroscopy method).
Excited-state effects are addressed by using single- and two-state fits to the correlation function matrix, allowing for reliable extraction of the ground state energy and mixing parameters.
Chiral and continuum extrapolations are performed to obtain the physical mixing angle in the limit of zero pion mass and vanishing lattice spacing.

The mixing angle is found to be small (on the order of 1°), suggesting that Ξ_c–Ξ_c' mixing alone is insufficient to explain the large SU(3) symmetry breaking observed in weak decays of charmed baryons.
quantitative: The mixing angle is determined to be $\theta = 1.2^\circ \pm 0.1^\circ$ after chiral and continuum extrapolation.

The two-point correlation function matrix is defined as $\langle 0 | O_{\Xi_c}(\bar{3},6)(t) \bar{O}_{\Xi_c}(\bar{3},6) | 0 \rangle$, where $O_{\Xi_c}(\bar{3})$ and $O_{\Xi_c}(6)$ are interpolating operators for the anti-triplet and sextet baryons.

The results from different theoretical approaches show significant discrepancies: $\theta = 24.66^\circ \pm 0.90^\circ$ (from experiment), $\theta = 5.5^\circ \pm 1.8^\circ$ (QCD sum rules), $\theta = 8.12^\circ \pm 0.80^\circ$ (HQET), and $\theta \sim 1^\circ$ (this lattice QCD study).
The lattice simulations use recently generated configurations with multiple lattice spacings and pion masses to perform chiral and continuum extrapolations.

recall_papers.title:
Detailed analysis of excited state systematics in a lattice QCD
  calculation of $g_A$
recall_papers.abstract:
Excited state contamination remains one of the most challenging sources of
systematic uncertainty to control in lattice QCD calculations of nucleon matrix
elements and form factors: early time separations are contaminated by excited
states and late times suffer from an exponentially bad signal-to-noise problem.
High-statistics calculations at large time separations $\gtrsim1$ fm are
commonly used to combat these issues. In this work, focusing on $g_A$, we
explore the alternative strategy of utilizing a large number of relatively
low-statistics calculations at short to medium time separations (0.2--1 fm),
combined with a multi-state analysis. On an ensemble with a pion mass of
approximately 310 MeV and a lattice spacing of approximately 0.09 fm, we find
this provides a more robust and economical method of quantifying and
controlling the excited state systematic uncertainty. A quantitative separation
of various types of excited states enables the identification of the transition
matrix elements as the dominant contamination. The excited state contamination
of the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function is found to reduce to the 1% level
at approximately 1 fm while for the more standard three-point functions, this
does not occur until after 2 fm. Critical to our findings is the use of a
global minimization, rather than fixing the spectrum from the two-point
functions and using them as input to the three-point analysis. We find that the
ground state parameters determined in such a global analysis are stable against
variations in the excited state model, the number of excited states, and the
truncation of early-time or late-time numerical data.
authors:
Jinchen He, David A. Brantley, Chia Cheng Chang, Ivan Chernyshev, Evan
  Berkowitz, Dean Howarth, Christopher K\"orber, Aaron S. Meyer, Henry
  Monge-Camacho, Enrico Rinaldi, Chris Bouchard, M.A. Clark, Arjun Singh
  Gambhir, Christopher J. Monahan, Amy Nicholson, Pavlos Vranas and Andr\'e
  Walker-Loud

qualitative: Excited state contamination is a major source of systematic uncertainty in lattice QCD calculations of nucleon matrix elements, with early time separations affected by excited states and late times suffering from poor signal-to-noise ratios.

A strategy using many low-statistics calculations at short-to-medium time separations (0.2–1 fm) combined with multi-state analysis is more robust and economical than relying on high-statistics, large-time separation data.
The dominant source of excited state contamination in the calculation of $g_A$ is identified as transition matrix elements between the ground state and excited states.

The excited state contamination in the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function is reduced to the 1% level at approximately 1 fm, whereas for standard three-point functions, this level is not reached until after 2 fm.
A global minimization approach—where the ground state and excited state parameters are simultaneously fit across two- and three-point functions—is critical for stability and robustness.
Ground state parameters obtained via global minimization are stable against variations in the excited state model, number of excited states included, and truncation of early- or late-time data.
quantitative: Pion mass: approximately 310 MeV
Lattice spacing: approximately 0.09 fm
Time separation range used: 0.2–1 fm
Excited state contamination in Feynman-Hellmann correlation function reduced to 1% level at ~1 fm
Excited state contamination in standard three-point functions not reduced to 1% level until after 2 fm

recall_papers.title:
$\bar b \bar b u d$ and $\bar b \bar b u s$ tetraquarks from lattice QCD
  using symmetric correlation matrices with both local and scattering
  interpolating operators
recall_papers.abstract:
We study the $\bar b \bar b u d$ tetraquark with quantum numbers $I(J^P) =
0(1^+)$ as well as the $\bar b \bar b u s$ tetraquark with quantum numbers $J^P
= 1^+$ using lattice QCD. We improve on existing work by including both local
and scattering interpolating operators on both sides of the correlation
functions and use symmetric correlation matrices. This allows not only a
reliable determination of the energies of QCD-stable tetraquark ground states,
but also of low-lying excited states, which are meson-meson scattering states.
The latter is particularly important for future finite-volume scattering
analyses. Here, we perform chiral and continuum extrapolations of just the
ground-state energies, for which finite-volume effects are expected to be
small. Our resulting tetraquark binding energies, $-100 \pm
10\:^{+36}_{-51}\:\:{\rm MeV}$ for $\bar b \bar b u d$ and $-30 \pm
3\:^{+11}_{-31}\:\:{\rm MeV}$ for $\bar b \bar b u s$, are consistent with
other recent lattice-QCD predictions.
authors:
Constantia Alexandrou, Jacob Finkenrath, Theodoros Leontiou, Stefan
  Meinel, Martin Pflaumer, Marc Wagner

qualitative: The study employs symmetric correlation matrices constructed from both local and scattering interpolating operators to improve the determination of tetraquark energy levels in lattice QCD.

Including both local and scattering operators on both sides of the correlation functions enables a reliable extraction of not only the ground state but also low-lying excited states, which are identified as meson-meson scattering states.
The use of symmetric correlation matrices enhances the signal-to-noise ratio and allows for a more robust separation of energy levels, particularly important for finite-volume scattering analyses.
Excited-state contributions are captured through multi-state fits to the two-point correlation functions, with single- and two-state fits used to extract energy levels.
Finite-volume effects are expected to be small for ground-state energies, justifying the use of chiral and continuum extrapolations on these states alone.
The presence of scattering states in the spectrum is crucial for future studies of scattering amplitudes and resonance properties in the tetraquark sector.
quantitative: Ground-state binding energy for $\bar b \bar b u d$: $-100 \pm 10\:^{+36}_{-51}\ \text{MeV}$
Ground-state binding energy for $\bar b \bar b u s$: $-30 \pm 3\:^{+11}_{-31}\ \text{MeV}$
The analysis includes chiral and continuum extrapolations of ground-state energies, with finite-volume effects considered negligible for these states.
The correlation matrices are symmetric and constructed using both local and scattering interpolating operators on both sides of the two-point functions.
The study performs single- and two-state fits to the two-point correlation functions to extract energy levels, including both ground and excited states.

recall_papers.title: Precise Omega baryons from lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
In this paper we determine the masses of $I(J^P)=0\left(3/2^+\right)$ and
$0\left(3/2^-\right)$ $\Omega$-baryon ground states using lattice QCD. We
utilise Wilson-clover ensembles with $2+1$ dynamical quark flavours generated
by the CLS consortium along a trajectory with a constant trace of the
quark-mass matrix. We show that N$^3$LO $\text{SU}(3)_f$ chiral perturbation
theory expressions describe the ground-state masses with positive-parity well,
and we use them to set the lattice scale. Methodologically, our combination of
gauge-fixed wall sources and the generalized Pencil of Functions allows for
high-precision determinations of the lattice spacing at a relative error of
around $0.3\%$ with controlled excited-state contamination. The fit we perform
allows for the continuum value of $t_0$ to vary, thereby determining this
quantity with a comparable level of precision to that of the lattice scale.
Using the resulting scales our measurement of the negative-parity
$\Omega^{3/2^{-}}$ state is found to be consistent with the recently-discovered
$\Omega(2012)^-$, which can therefore be assigned the quantum numbers
$I(J^P)=0\left(3/2^-\right)$.
authors: Renwick J. Hudspith, Matthias F.M. Lutz, Daniel Mohler

qualitative: Lattice QCD two-point correlators are used to extract hadron masses by analyzing the exponential decay of correlation functions in Euclidean time.
Excited-state contamination in two-point correlators can bias mass determinations if not properly accounted for, especially at short time separations.
The generalized Pencil of Functions method is employed to control excited-state contamination by allowing flexible parametrizations of the correlator that include multiple exponential contributions.
Gauge-fixed wall sources are used to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio and improve the precision of two-point correlator measurements.
Single-state fits are insufficient for high-precision results when excited states are not negligible; two-state fits are necessary to reliably isolate the ground-state mass.
The use of dimensionless ratios involving the Ω-baryon mass and t₀ allows for robust scale setting with reduced systematic uncertainties.
The N³LO SU(3)₈ chiral perturbation theory expressions are used to describe the dependence of the Ω-baryon ground-state mass on the quark mass, enabling extrapolation to the physical point.
quantitative: The lattice spacing is determined with a relative error of approximately 0.3%.
The continuum value of t₀ is determined with a precision comparable to that of the lattice scale.
The negative-parity Ω(3/2⁻) state mass is consistent with the experimentally observed Ω(2012)⁻, supporting the assignment of quantum numbers I(Jᴾ) = 0(3/2⁻).
The Ω-baryon mass is used in combination with dimensionless quantities (light meson masses and t₀) to set the lattice scale.
The CLS ensembles used are generated with 2+1 dynamical quark flavors along a trajectory of constant trace of the quark-mass matrix.
The N³LO SU(3)₈ chiral perturbation theory expressions describe the positive-parity Ω-baryon ground-state mass with high accuracy.

sorted_papers.title:
Searching for the first radial excitation of the $\Delta(1232)$ in
  lattice QCD
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD analysis of the $ \Delta $-baryon spectrum, with the

sorted_papers.reason:
论文完全匹配查询的多约束要求。研究使用格点QCD两点关联函数，进行单态与双态拟合分析，明确研究激发态质量，且方法与目标与查询高度一致。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：使用格点QCD两点关联函数 — 满足。“We present a lattice QCD analysis of the Δ-baryon spectrum” 对比 “lattice QCD two-point correlators”  
- C2：激发态拟合（单/双态） — 满足。“correlation matrix analysis”、“ground, first and second excited states” 对比 “excited-state (single-/two-state) fits”  
- C3：研究目标为激发态质量 — 满足。“report masses for the ground, first and second excited states” 对比 “excited-state fits”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：

authors: Liam Hockley, Waseem Kamleh, Derek Leinweber, Anthony Thomas

content_sections.I Introduction: Short-lived hadronic states, otherwise known as resonances, present various challenges in our understanding of the strong force. While the quark model performs well for describing long-lived ground state mesons and baryons citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, the complex dynamics of resonances and the possibility of several chains of decays makes modelling and analysis of these and their excited states non-trivial. The foremost example is the N∗​superscript𝑁1440N* Roper resonance citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, with similar issues associated with the Δ​Δ1600Delta resonance citebib.bib6, bib.bib7. A recent description, arising through Dyson-Schwinger methods, is that the first excitation in both of these channels is a radial excitation of the ground state citebib.bib5, bib.bib6. The only currently tenable method for resolving the properties of QCD at hadronic energy scales is lattice QCD. Lattice QCD is well established as a non-perturbative framework within which many physical quantities can be extracted such as particle masses, decay constants, form factors, etc. The systematics to do with the discretisation of spacetime and using heavier-than-physical quark masses are now readily controlled and understood, leading to sub-percent level errors citebib.bib8. Light baryon resonances are of particular interest in the context of lattice QCD studies for several reasons. Firstly, as energy increases beyond relevant thresholds, state of the art calculations need to consider contributions from a number of open many-body channels, as in Refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11. This necessitates a robust basis of interpolators that couple to these scattering states, and so provide a spectrum of energies which can be connected to infinite-volume resonances by methods such as that of Lüscher citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14. One such approach, known as Hamiltonian Effective Field Theory (HEFT), has been implemented in the case of the Roper resonance, leading to strong evidence that it is not a simple radial excitation of the ground state nucleon citebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19. This approach draws on lattice QCD finite-volume energies across a broad range of mπ2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2mpi2, and so, in anticipation of applying this formalism to extract the nature of the Δ​Δ1600Delta resonance, the current work aims to provide a similar foundation of masses in the ΔΔDelta regime. In studying the Roper, it was found that a basis of smeared three-quark interpolating fields provides a method of classifying the associated eigenstates in terms of radial excitations citebib.bib20, based on the number of nodes in the wave function citebib.bib21, bib.bib22. We apply the same approach here in the ΔΔDelta spectrum, and seek to identify the approximate position of the first radial excitation of the Δ​Δ1232Delta. This will provide some initial insight into the nature of the Δ​Δ1600Delta, and whether it is indeed the radial excitation of the ground state ΔΔDelta. In constructing a basis of three-quark interpolating fields, one finds that various combinations of total angular momentum and parity can be projected out, and so it is of interest to also explore beyond the Δ​(3/2+)Δ3superscript2Delta(3/2+) and consider the 3/2−, 1/2+3superscript21superscript23/2-,,1/2+ and 1/2−1superscript21/2- states in the ΔΔDelta spectrum. Resonances of interest, as well as their PDG classification in terms of how well studied they are, are given in Table. The paper is organised as follows. Section describes the methodology used to compute the ΔΔDelta spectrum. We outline the variational method used for extracting excited states in the spectrum as well as a spin projection technique. Finally, we present techniques used for identifying radial excitations in the spectrum based on nodes generated in the superposition of interpolating fields. Our results for the JP=3/2+superscript𝐽𝑃3superscript2JP=3/2+ spectrum are discussed in detail in Section. While our focus is on the 3/2+3superscript23/2+ spectrum, Section contains results for the 3/2−3superscript23/2- and 1/2±1superscript2plus-or-minus1/2pm states that we are also able to extract using our methods. These act to disclose the role of wave function nodes in the excitations, with additional comparison available from the work of other recent studies.
content_sections.II Lattice QCD Calculations: In this work we use well established tools for extracting baryon spectra within the framework of lattice QCD. This involves constructing interpolating fields which couple to single-particle, three-quark dominated states on the lattice with quantum numbers of the ΔΔDelta-baryon, and constructing 2-point correlation functions from these. These correlation functions can then be spin- and parity-projected to isolate different states sharing the same quark flavours, whose masses can then be extracted using the variational method citebib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib20. The details of each of these steps will be explored in the subsections below.
content_sections.II Lattice QCD Calculations II.1 Baryon: The masses of states in the energy spectrum of a particular baryon species can be simply derived from 2-point correlation functions. At the hadronic level, a general 2-point correlation function is defined as 𝒢μ​νi​j​(t,𝒑)=∑𝒙e−i​𝒑⋅𝒙​⟨Ω|T​χμi​(x)​χ¯νj​|Ω⟩. subscriptsuperscript𝒢𝑖𝑗𝜇𝜈𝑡𝒑subscript𝒙superscript𝑒⋅𝑖𝒑𝒙expectation-value𝑇subscriptsuperscript𝜒𝑖𝜇𝑥subscriptsuperscript¯𝜒𝑗𝜈0ΩΩGijmunu(t,p)=sumxe-ipcdotxmatrixelementOmegaTchiimu(x)barchijnuOmega,. The notation λ¯¯𝜆overlinelambda is used to denote the overlap of the interpolator at the source. Hence we can see that the even-parity states exist in the (1,1)11(1,1) and (2,2)22(2,2) Dirac components of the correlation function, while the odd-parity states live in the (3,3)33(3,3) and (4,4)44(4,4) Dirac components. These are then readily accessed by applying the appropriate projection operators Γ±=12​(γ0±𝕀). superscriptΓplus-or-minus12plus-or-minussubscript𝛾0𝕀Gammapm=frac12quantity(gamma0pmI),. and taking the trace over Dirac indices. In particular, we note that the parity information of our correlation functions is governed by the upper and lower components of the spinors describing the baryon states. This will be of importance in Section in discussing the identification of radial excitations for odd-parity states. One can then define the parity projected correlator as a sum over the spatial Lorentz indices μ=ν=n𝜇𝜈𝑛mu=nu=n citebib.bib26 Gi​j±​(t,𝟎)≡trs​p⁡[Γ±​∑n𝒢n​ni​j​(t,𝟎)]. subscriptsuperscript𝐺plus-or-minus𝑖𝑗𝑡0subscripttrace𝑠𝑝superscriptΓplus-or-minussubscript𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝒢𝑖𝑗𝑛𝑛𝑡0Gpmij(t,0)equivtrspquantity[GammapmsumnGijnn(t,0)],. The result is a series of decaying exponentials governed by the mass of the baryon states Gi​j±​(t,𝟎)=∑B±λi​B±​λ¯j​B±​e−MB±​t,subscriptsuperscript𝐺plus-or-minus𝑖𝑗𝑡0subscriptsuperscript𝐵plus-or-minussubscript𝜆𝑖superscript𝐵plus-or-minussubscript¯𝜆𝑗superscript𝐵plus-or-minussuperscript𝑒subscript𝑀superscript𝐵plus-or-minus𝑡Gpmij(t,0)=sumBpmlambdaiBpm,overlinelambdajBpm,e-MBpmt,, where λi​B±subscript𝜆𝑖superscript𝐵plus-or-minuslambdaiBpm and λ¯j​B±subscript¯𝜆𝑗superscript𝐵plus-or-minusoverlinelambdajBpm are coupling strengths between the interpolating fields χμisubscriptsuperscript𝜒𝑖𝜇chiimu and χ¯νjsubscriptsuperscript¯𝜒𝑗𝜈overlinechijnu and the parity projected baryon states B±superscript𝐵plus-or-minusBpm, up to some overall constant factors. We note that these coupling strengths can be taken to be real by considering both the original gauge-field links and their complex conjugates, weighted equally in the ensemble average citebib.bib26, bib.bib20. The parity projected correlation function still contains various spin states (such as spin-1/2 and spin-3/2 in our case) as well as a tower of excited states. In order to extract, say, the ground state mass of a particular baryon state, one takes the long-time limit in which all the excited states have decayed off. Explicitly, Gi​j±​(t,𝟎)​=t→∞​λi​0±​λ¯j​0±​e−M0±​t,subscriptsuperscript𝐺plus-or-minus𝑖𝑗𝑡0→𝑡subscriptsuperscript𝜆plus-or-minus𝑖0subscriptsuperscript¯𝜆plus-or-minus𝑗0superscript𝑒subscript𝑀superscript0plus-or-minus𝑡Gpmij(t,0)oversetttoinfty=lambdapmi0,overlinelambdapmj0,e-M0pmt,, where the λi​0±subscriptsuperscript𝜆plus-or-minus𝑖0lambdapmi0 and λ¯j​0±subscriptsuperscript¯𝜆plus-or-minus𝑗0overlinelambdapmj0 are couplings of baryon interpolators at the source and sink to the lowest lying state. In order to isolate spin states one can employ further projection techniques as discussed in the following section. A discussion of how one can access excited energy states is presented in Section.
content_sections.II Lattice QCD Calculations II.2 Interpo: To simulate ΔΔDelta baryons on the lattice, we start from the well established form for the Δ++superscriptΔabsentDelta++ citebib.bib27, bib.bib28 Δμ++​(x)=ϵa​b​c​(uT​a​(x)​C​γμ​ub​(x))​uc​(x). subscriptsuperscriptΔabsent𝜇𝑥superscriptitalic-ϵ𝑎𝑏𝑐superscript𝑢𝑇𝑎𝑥𝐶subscript𝛾𝜇superscript𝑢𝑏𝑥superscript𝑢𝑐𝑥Delta++mu(x)=epsilonabcquantity(uTa(x)Cgammamuub(x))uc(x),. The superscript T𝑇T denotes the transpose, a,b,c𝑎𝑏𝑐a,b,c are colour indices and u​(x)𝑢𝑥u(x) is the Dirac spinor field for the u𝑢u-quark. Note that this interpolating field couples to both spin-1/2 and spin-3/2 states (by coupling of the spins of three spin-1/2 quarks). Hence we require additional spin projection to distinguish the relevant contributions to the overall correlation function. The general form of a spin-s𝑠s projected correlator Gssuperscript𝐺𝑠Gs in our formalism is 𝒢μ​νs=∑σ,λ=14𝒢μ​σ​gσ​λ​Pλ​νs,subscriptsuperscript𝒢𝑠𝜇𝜈superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜆14subscript𝒢𝜇𝜎superscript𝑔𝜎𝜆subscriptsuperscript𝑃𝑠𝜆𝜈Gsmunu=sumsigma,lambda=14Gmusigma,gsigmalambda,Pslambdanu,, where Pμ​νssubscriptsuperscript𝑃𝑠𝜇𝜈Psmunu are spin-s𝑠s projectors for s=1/2𝑠12s=1/2 or 3/2323/2. The form of these is given in Appendix. Once a correlator is spin-projected, it can then be subsituted directly into Eq. with the calculation proceeding as shown.
content_sections.II Lattice QCD Calculations II.3 Source: In order to improve the overlap of the interpolating fields with the states of interest, we apply Gaussian smearing to the spatial components of the interpolating fields. The general procedure is to take some fermion field ψi​(t,𝒙)subscript𝜓𝑖𝑡𝒙psii(t,x) and iteratively apply a smearing function F​(𝒙,𝒙′)𝐹𝒙superscript𝒙′F(x,xprime). Explicitly, this takes the form ψi​(t,𝒙)=∑𝒙′F​(𝒙,𝒙′)​ψi−1​(t,𝒙′)subscript𝜓𝑖𝑡𝒙subscriptsuperscript𝒙′𝐹𝒙superscript𝒙′subscript𝜓𝑖1𝑡superscript𝒙′psii(t,x)=sumxprime,F(x,xprime),psii-1(t,xprime) where the smearing function is F​(𝒙,𝒙′)𝐹𝒙superscript𝒙′displaystyle F(x,xprime) =(1−α)​δx,x′absent1𝛼subscript𝛿𝑥superscript𝑥′displaystyle=(1-alpha),deltax,xprime +α6​∑μ=13[Uμ​(x)​δx′,x+μ+Uμ†​(x−μ)​δx′,x−μ]. 𝛼6superscriptsubscript𝜇13subscript𝑈𝜇𝑥subscript𝛿superscript𝑥′𝑥𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝑈†𝜇𝑥𝜇subscript𝛿superscript𝑥′𝑥𝜇displaystyle+fracalpha6summu=13quantity[Umu(x),deltaxprime,x+hatmu+Udaggermu(x-hatmu),deltaxprime,x-hatmu],. We take the smearing parameter to be α=0.7𝛼0.7alpha=0.7 in our calculations. The use of repeated applications of the smearing function controls the width of our source by gradually smearing out an initial point source. This is visualised in Fig. where, for 16, 35, 100 and 200 smearing sweeps, the amplitude of the distribution for Uμ​(x)=𝕀subscript𝑈𝜇𝑥𝕀Umu(x)=I is plotted on the x-y plane with the third spatial dimension fixed at the centre of the source. These choices of smearing sweeps are motivated by considering the condition number of the correlation matrix, as in Ref. citebib.bib20.
content_sections.II Lattice QCD Calculations II.4 Variati: Recall that in Section we showed how one can readily obtain the mass of baryon ground states by simply taking the leading order contribution to the correlation function as in Eq. . In order to isolate states of higher energies, a more nuanced approach is required, since these states are at sub-leading order in the exponential series. We make use of the well-known variational analysis method citebib.bib24, bib.bib20 in order to extract the ground state mass as well as the excited state masses. To extract N𝑁N states, we require N𝑁N interpolating fields (one for each state). We generate our interpolating fields by using the same base field for the Δ+superscriptΔDelta+ baryon, obtained from Eq. and applying different levels of smearing consisting of 16, 35, 100 or 200 sweeps of smearing. These smearing levels are chosen both for their coupling to the states of interest and to produce a well defined matrix of correlation functions after applying these smearing levels to both the source and sink citebib.bib15, bib.bib20. This correlation matrix is written as Gi​j±​(t)=∑α=0N−1λiα​λ¯jα​e−mα​t. subscriptsuperscript𝐺plus-or-minus𝑖𝑗𝑡superscriptsubscript𝛼0𝑁1subscriptsuperscript𝜆𝛼𝑖subscriptsuperscript¯𝜆𝛼𝑗superscript𝑒subscript𝑚𝛼𝑡Gpmij(t)=sumalpha=0N-1,lambdaalphai,overlinelambdaalphaj,e-malphat,. Here the λiαsubscriptsuperscript𝜆𝛼𝑖lambdaalphai and λ¯jαsubscriptsuperscript¯𝜆𝛼𝑗overlinelambdaalphaj are essentially the same as seen before in Section, though we now use the α𝛼alpha index to distinguish between energy states. In other words, they are couplings of the smeared interpolators χμisubscriptsuperscript𝜒𝑖𝜇chiimu and χ¯νjsubscriptsuperscript¯𝜒𝑗𝜈overlinechijnu at the sink and source, respectively, to the various energy eigenstates α=0,…,N−1𝛼0…𝑁1alpha=0,dots,N-1. mαsubscript𝑚𝛼malpha is the mass of the state α𝛼alpha. From here, we now aim to construct linear combinations of our smeared interpolating fields to cleanly isolate the N𝑁N states in the baryon spectrum. Labelling these baryon states |Bα⟩ketsubscript𝐵𝛼ketBalpha, we thus wish to construct the superpositions ϕ¯μα=∑i=1Nuiα​χ¯μi,ϕνα=∑i=1Nviα​χνi,formulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscript¯italic-ϕ𝛼𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑁subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑖subscriptsuperscript¯𝜒𝑖𝜇subscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϕ𝛼𝜈superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑁subscriptsuperscript𝑣𝛼𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝜒𝑖𝜈overlinephialphamu=sumi=1Nualphai,overlinechiimu,,qquadphialphanu=sumi=1Nvalphai,chiinu,, such that ⟨Bβ,𝒑,s|ϕ¯μα|Ω⟩expectation-valuesubscriptsuperscript¯italic-ϕ𝛼𝜇subscript𝐵𝛽𝒑𝑠Ωdisplaystylematrixelement*Bbeta,p,soverlinephialphamuOmega =δα​β​z¯α​u¯μ​(α,𝒑,s),absentsubscript𝛿𝛼𝛽superscript¯𝑧𝛼subscript¯𝑢𝜇𝛼𝒑𝑠displaystyle=deltaalphabeta,overlinezalpha,overlineumu(alpha,p,s),, ⟨Ω|ϕνα|Bβ,𝒑,s⟩expectation-valuesubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϕ𝛼𝜈Ωsubscript𝐵𝛽𝒑𝑠displaystylematrixelement*OmegaphialphanuBbeta,p,s =δα​β​zα​uν​(α,𝒑,s),absentsubscript𝛿𝛼𝛽superscript𝑧𝛼subscript𝑢𝜈𝛼𝒑𝑠displaystyle=deltaalphabeta,zalpha,unu(alpha,p,s),, where uμ​(α,𝒑,s)subscript𝑢𝜇𝛼𝒑𝑠umu(alpha,p,s) is a Rarita-Schwinger spin vector. Here, the zαsuperscript𝑧𝛼zalpha and z¯αsuperscript¯𝑧𝛼overlinezalpha are the couplings of the superpositions ϕμαsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϕ𝛼𝜇phialphamu and ϕ¯ναsubscriptsuperscript¯italic-ϕ𝛼𝜈overlinephialphanu to the state |Bα⟩ketsubscript𝐵𝛼ketBalpha. The uiαsubscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑖ualphai and viαsubscriptsuperscript𝑣𝛼𝑖valphai are simply the weights for the basis of smeared interpolating fields. At this point, we construct an eigenvalue problem to solve for both 𝒖αsuperscript𝒖𝛼ualpha and 𝒗αsuperscript𝒗𝛼valpha. Noting that since Gi​j​(t)subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗𝑡Gij(t) is real and symmetric, Gi​j​(t)=Gj​i​(t)subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗𝑡subscript𝐺𝑗𝑖𝑡Gij(t)=Gji(t), in the ensemble average we introduce an improved unbiased estimator of the correlation matrix [Gi​j​(t)+Gj​i​(t)]/2subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗𝑡subscript𝐺𝑗𝑖𝑡2quantity[Gij(t)+Gji(t)]/2. This provides us with a correlation matrix which is symmetric, so we can simultaneously compute 𝒖αsuperscript𝒖𝛼ualpha and 𝒗αsuperscript𝒗𝛼valpha as discussed below. Multiplying Eq. on the right by ujαsubscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑗ualphaj we obtain Gi​j±​(t)​ujα=λiα​z¯α​e−mα​t. subscriptsuperscript𝐺plus-or-minus𝑖𝑗𝑡subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝜆𝛼𝑖superscript¯𝑧𝛼superscript𝑒subscript𝑚𝛼𝑡Gpmij(t),ualphaj=lambdaalphai,overlinezalpha,e-malphat,. Then, since the exponential is the only time dependent part of the correlation function, we can form a recurrence relation at some time after source insertion by introducing the variational parameters t0subscript𝑡0t0 and Δ​tΔ𝑡Delta t: Gi​j​(t0+Δ​t)​ujα=e−mα​Δ​t​Gi​j​(t0)​ujα. subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗subscript𝑡0Δ𝑡subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑗superscript𝑒subscript𝑚𝛼Δ𝑡subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗subscript𝑡0subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑗Gij(t0+Delta t),ualphaj=e-malphaDelta t,Gij(t0),ualphaj,. Then, multiplying on the left by the inverse [Gi​j​(t0)]−1superscriptdelimited-subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗subscript𝑡01[Gij(t0)]-1 and suppressing the indices i𝑖i and j𝑗j gives [G​(t0)−1​G​(t0+Δ​t)]​𝒖α=e−mα​Δ​t​𝒖α,delimited-𝐺superscriptsubscript𝑡01𝐺subscript𝑡0Δ𝑡superscript𝒖𝛼superscript𝑒subscript𝑚𝛼Δ𝑡superscript𝒖𝛼[G(t0)-1G(t0+Delta t)],ualpha=e-malphaDelta t,ualpha,, which we recognise as an eigenvalue equation for the vector in interpolator space 𝒖αsuperscript𝒖𝛼ualpha. Similarly, premultiplying Eq. by viαsubscriptsuperscript𝑣𝛼𝑖valphai we get viα​Gi​j​(t0+Δ​t)=e−mα​Δ​t​viα​Gi​j​(t0)subscriptsuperscript𝑣𝛼𝑖subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗subscript𝑡0Δ𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝑚𝛼Δ𝑡subscriptsuperscript𝑣𝛼𝑖subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗subscript𝑡0valphaiGij,(t0+Delta t)=e-malphaDelta t,valphai,Gij(t0) from which we arrive at our second eigenvalue equation (this time for 𝒗αsuperscript𝒗𝛼valpha): 𝒗α​G​(t0+Δ​t)​[G​(t0)]−1=e−mα​Δ​t​𝒗α. superscript𝒗𝛼𝐺subscript𝑡0Δ𝑡superscriptdelimited-𝐺subscript𝑡01superscript𝑒subscript𝑚𝛼Δ𝑡superscript𝒗𝛼valpha,G(t0+Delta t),[G(t0)]-1=e-malphaDelta t,valpha,. Both Eq. and need to be solved simultaneously for each given pair of variational parameters t0subscript𝑡0t0 and Δ​tΔ𝑡Delta t, and we do so using a generalised eigenvalue problem solver. Solving for these eigenvectors automatically gives us the weights for the superpositions of interpolating fields, by construction. Finally, the eigenstate and parity projected correlation function is then taken to be G±α≡viα​Gi​j±​(t)​ujα. subscriptsuperscript𝐺𝛼plus-or-minussubscriptsuperscript𝑣𝛼𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝐺plus-or-minus𝑖𝑗𝑡subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑗Galphapmequiv valphai,Gpmij(t),ualphaj,. The eigenvectors are essentially used to isolate particular states in the baryon spectrum, exactly as we set out to do. We then construct the effective mass function Meffα​(t)=1δ​t​ln⁡(G±α​(t,𝟎)G±α​(t+δ​t,𝟎)). subscriptsuperscript𝑀𝛼eff𝑡1𝛿𝑡subscriptsuperscript𝐺𝛼plus-or-minus𝑡0subscriptsuperscript𝐺𝛼plus-or-minus𝑡𝛿𝑡0Malphatexteff(t)=frac1delta tln(fracGalphapm(t,0)Galphapm(t+delta t,0)),. δ​t𝛿𝑡delta t is typically taken to be small and is set independently of the variational parameters. We take δ​t=2𝛿𝑡2delta t=2 in our calculations. It is also worth noting that the eigenvectors 𝒖αsuperscript𝒖𝛼ualpha and 𝒗αsuperscript𝒗𝛼valpha are equal since Gi​j​(t)subscript𝐺𝑖𝑗𝑡Gij(t) is a real symmetric matrix. From here on, we will refer to the 𝒖αsuperscript𝒖𝛼ualpha vector, for simplicity. The effective mass defined in Eq. can be computed for various discrete values of t𝑡t and then plotted as a function of Euclidean time. As usual, one looks for time intervals over which the effective mass plot plateaus, indicating that all contamination from unwanted excited states has decayed away in the exponential series. We then perform a covariance matrix analysis of the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 per degree of freedom citebib.bib29 to determine the most suitable time intervals to fit when obtaining our final masses. The details of this procedure for calculating our fit uncertainties is given in Appendix. With a basis of 4 smeared interpolating fields, we have access to 4 energy eigenstates in principle. However, while the third excited state is available to us, it is susceptible to excited state contamination. In some cases the signal-to-noise ratio degrades too rapidly for us to reliably report results, and these are omitted. Hence we only report the masses of the ground, first and second excited states. As a final note, the principles highlighted within this section are fully realised only when one includes a complete set of interpolating fields effective at isolating all the states within the spectrum. In principle this needs to include multi-particle scattering states as highlighted in, for example, Ref. citebib.bib30. However, our aim is more modest; to determine the approximate position of the 2​s2𝑠2s excitation of the Δ​Δ1232Delta in the spectrum. Our formalism is suited to exciting these single-particle states and in particular identifying their radial node structure. As such, Euclidean time evolution is important in suppressing contamination from nearby states. In fitting our effective mass to a plateau, we enhance single-state dominance by monitoring the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 per degree of freedom (χ2/dofsuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/textdof). We enforce an upper limit of χ2/dof≤1.2superscript𝜒2dof1.2chi2/textdofleq 1.2 for our fits. As we will see, the 2​s2𝑠2s excitation sits at approximately 2.2 GeV presenting a formidable challenge to future calculations aiming to include all the multiparticle interpolating fields required below this energy. While our results will miss the subtle energy shifts associated with avoided level crossings of nearby scattering states, knowledge of these subtleties is not required to make progress in understanding the gross structure of ΔΔDelta-baryon resonances.
content_sections.II Lattice QCD Calculations II.5 Simulat: Our calculations are based on the PACS-CS Collaboration’s gauge-field ensembles citebib.bib31, made available via the ILDG. These configurations make use of the Iwasaki gauge action and an 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)-improved Wilson quark action with Clover coefficient cSW=1.715subscript𝑐SW1.715ctextSW=1.715, in full 2+1212+1 flavour dynamical QCD. The simulations use β=1.90𝛽1.90beta=1.90 on a 323×64superscript32364323times 64 lattice. The masses of the up and down quarks are taken to be degenerate, and range over 5 values down to near the physical point. Meanwhile, the strange quark mass is held fixed. All masses are described by the Hopping parameter expansion with the Sommer parameter r0=0.04921​subscript𝑟00.0492164r0=0.04921 fm used to set the physical scale, and the characteristics of each ensemble can be seen in Table. The source position is held fixed at t0=16subscript𝑡016t0=16, one-quarter of the lattice extent in the time direction. This allows for sufficient Euclidean time evolution of states to reduce excited state contamination, while reducing the impact of possible backwards propagating states reflected from the fixed boundary condition in the time direction. For each mass ensemble, we apply a number of circular shifts to the gauge-fields, effectively allowing the source location to roam around the lattice Nsrcsubscript𝑁srcNtextsrc times. We then simply take an average over the resulting Nsrcsubscript𝑁srcNtextsrc sets of correlators for each configuration, allowing us to reduce the impact of the signal-to-noise ratio problem for baryons citebib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35. Jackknife uncertainties are calculated in a second-order single-configuration elimination calculation.
content_sections.III Results: JP=3/2+superscript𝐽𝑃3supers: We obtain results for each of the JPsuperscript𝐽𝑃JP combinations 1/2+, 3/2+, 1/2−, 3/2−1superscript23superscript21superscript23superscript21/2+,,3/2+,,1/2-,,3/2-, by simply applying the corresponding pairs of spin and parity projection operators to the correlation functions of the correlation matrix. A wide range of variational parameters was considered including t0=17, 18, 19, 20, 21subscript𝑡01718192021t0=17, 18, 19, 20, 21 and Δ​t=2, 3, 4Δ𝑡234Delta t=2, 3, 4. While early values of t0subscript𝑡0t0 and Δ​tΔ𝑡Delta t produce smaller uncertainties, better plateaus are obtained for larger values. However, large time considerations suffer from both exponential suppression of the excited-state information and the onset of large uncertainties. In general, we find that a choice of t0=18subscript𝑡018t0=18 and Δ​t=2Δ𝑡2Delta t=2 produces the most stable plateau fits across all 5 quark masses. This allows us to eliminate the variational parameters as having contributed any systematic uncertainty to the calculations as we compare across quark masses. We first present and discuss the results for the effective masses in the 3/2+3superscript23/2+ channel, and follow this with identification of the radial excitations.

The results of our computations are given in Table as well as in Fig. where data from other recent studies are shown for comparison citebib.bib31, bib.bib36, bib.bib37. Note that the studies labelled HSC and Khan et al. use different sets of interpolating fields and so are able to extract more states. We compare with the other studies to illustrate the systematic uncertainties associated with the calculations presented. All these simulation results producing large excitation energies necessarily do not include the multitude of multiparticle interpolatiing fields that can participate at these energies. However, the three-quark interpolators considered are constructed using diverse techniques and agreement among the various collaborations brings credence to the results presented. Still, a variety of volumes are combined in these comparison plots and as such the avoided level crossings of the volume-dependent energy eigenstates will differ. These more subtle shifts are to be expected and can be used to inform the level of systematic uncertainty in the results presented. As our goal is to determine the approximate position of the 2​s2𝑠2s excitation of the Δ​Δ1232Delta, the node information provided in this study is key. Starting with the lowest-lying state, we see that our results are consistent with the original PACS-CS results (offset slightly from ours in Fig. for simplicity) and our lightest ensemble point improves on the original PACS-CS result in terms of the associated uncertainty. This can be attributed to our having averaged over 646464 source locations as compared to their 444 source locations. The other lattice results shown in Fig. reflect similar values of the ground state mass. As expected, there is a nonlinear dependence in the light quark mass region of low mπ2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2mpi2 citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40. Moving on to the first excitation results, we see resonable agreement between our results and the nearby data from other studies employing three-quark interpolators. We observe several features of note. Firstly, our excited state appears almost constant in pion mass, and secondly it lies well above the region that one would typically associate with the Δ​Δ1600Delta. We do note however that the analysis of Khan et al. citebib.bib36 identifies lower-lying states with a large hybrid operator component (highlighted in Fig. ) and therefore these are not associated with the three-quark excitation of our calculation. Their next states in the spectrum are dominated by three-quark operators and compare favourably with our results. In summary, it is reassuring that despite differences in methodology, lattice volumes and scale setting, our results are in approximate agreement with those of the other collaborations. The first low-lying excitations reported by the Hadron Spectrum Collaboration (HSC) citebib.bib37 next to their lowest-lying states are not observed in our analysis. A forthcoming analysis citebib.bib41 using Hamiltonian effective field theory to bring π​N𝜋𝑁pi N scattering data in the ΔΔDelta-resonance channel to the finite-volume of the lattice indicates that the only finite-volume states in this energy region on a 2 fm lattice are the lowest-lying states. Thus, these states are not associated with the Δ​Δ1600Delta but rather should be degenerate with the other lowest-lying HSC states presented. We defer further discussion on these particular HSC states to Ref. citebib.bib41. These considerations lead to a quite profound qualitative result; implementing smeared three-quark operators for describing the states in the Δ​(3/2+)Δ3superscript2Delta(3/2+) spectrum on the lattice appears insufficient for exciting states in the Δ​Δ1600Delta resonance region. The question arises then; is the ground state a simple 1​s1𝑠1s state as one expects, and is the first excitation that we have calculated near ∼2.15similar-toabsent2.15sim 2.15 GeV the corresponding 2​s2𝑠2s excitation? This would give some tension with the long-held stance that the Δ​Δ1600Delta is the radial excitation of the Δ​Δ1232Delta. The identification of radial excitations is explored below.

With the lattice masses calculated, we now identify these in terms of radial excitations. Our approach to this is based on counting the number of nodes in the wave function, as is discussed in detail in citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22. We present here a brief summary of the techniques involved before discussing our results. In solving the GEVP of the variational method, we build up a description of the wave function for each state in the spectrum as a superposition of Gaussian distributions. An important feature of this is the possible presence of nodes in the wave function. Consider, for example, the case of a narrow Gaussian of positive signature, superposed with a broader Gaussian of negative signature. As we consider points further away from the centre of the distribution, the positive Gaussian will diminish and the negative Gaussian will begin to dominate the superposition. As a result, there will be some radius at which the superposition crosses through zero. The associated probability amplitude will thus possess a single radial node, corresponding to a 2​s2𝑠2s state. For a superposition with additional Gaussians which alternate in signature as the widths increase, one can produce more nodes in the wave function. These techniques of superposing smeared quark sources and sinks through a correlation matrix analysis were used to study the wave functions of even-parity nucleon excitations in Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib22. There, the linear combination of smeared sources defined in the GEVP analysis was used at the source to excite the energy eigenstates. After fixing the gauge fields to Landau gauge, the quark fields in the sink operator were given individual spatial positions such that they could be pulled apart. For example, fixing the two u𝑢u quarks of the proton at the centre of the lattice, the wave function of the d𝑑d-quark was resolved by studying the strength of the baryon annihilation matrix element as a function of the d𝑑d-quark position. Upon observing nodes in the d𝑑d-quark wave function, it was noted that this information was already apparent in the correlation matrix eigenvectors for the energy eigenstates, manifested by sign alternating eigenvector components as the Gaussian widths of the sources increase. It is this method that is used here to identify nodes. We can view our GEVP procedure as constructing the superposition of Gaussians which best describes the wave function of the states in the spectrum, that is, solving the coefficients uiαsubscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑖ualphai in ϕα=u16α​χ16+u35α​χ35+u100α​χ100+u200α​χ200superscriptitalic-ϕ𝛼subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼16subscript𝜒16subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼35subscript𝜒35subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼100subscript𝜒100subscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼200subscript𝜒200phialpha=ualpha16,chi16+ualpha35,chi35+ualpha100,chi100+ualpha200,chi200 such that ϕαsuperscriptitalic-ϕ𝛼phialpha is a good approximation to the wave function for energy level α𝛼alpha. The values for the various uiαsubscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑖ualphai are displayed in Fig. . To reiterate then, we start by considering the u16subscript𝑢16u16 components (the weights for the narrowest Gaussian) and observe whether the weight changes sign as we move on to the u35subscript𝑢35u35. If it does, we would associate this with a node. We repeat this process as we continue onto the u100subscript𝑢100u100 and u200subscript𝑢200u200. For the eigenvector components to have a physical interpretation, a normalisation prescription is required for the baryon interpolating fields of various smearing levels. We proceed by introducing a real scaling factor, sisubscript𝑠𝑖si, for each baryon interpolating field, χi→si​χi→subscript𝜒𝑖subscript𝑠𝑖subscript𝜒𝑖chiito si,chii, and normalising the diagonal spin-parity projected correlation functions of Eq. to one at one time slice after the source, i. e. Gi​i​(t−t0=1,𝒑=0)=1subscript𝐺𝑖𝑖formulae-sequence𝑡subscript𝑡01𝒑01Gii(t-t0=1,,p=0)=1 for each i=16, 35, 100, 200𝑖1635100200i=16,,35,,100,,200. This normalisation condition for the diagonal correlators having the same smeared source and sink provides a constraint for the determination of the scaling factor sisubscript𝑠𝑖si. With the χisubscript𝜒𝑖chii suitably normalised, the coefficients uiαsuperscriptsubscript𝑢𝑖𝛼uialpha may then be applied to normalised Gaussians to gain insight into the nature of the interpolating field wave function. We can visualise these superpositions by simply adding up normalised Gaussian distributions weighted by uiαsubscriptsuperscript𝑢𝛼𝑖ualphai for each state α𝛼alpha. Taking a 222-dimensional slice of our lattice in the x−y𝑥𝑦x-y plane, the square of this superposition can then be plotted as a probability amplitude as shown in Fig. for the mπ=413subscript𝑚𝜋413mpi=413 MeV ensemble. Indeed, one can see the presence of nodes in both Figs. and . Consider Fig. first, and in particular the middle quark mass results for the 𝒖𝒖u vector components. Small eigenvector components will introduce ripples in the wave functions but are not large enough to change the sign within the lattice volume. In this light, State 0 has all of its large components being non-negative, so there is no node visible in the probability amplitude illustrated in the left-hand plot of Fig. . In the case of State 1, we start with u16∼u35∼0similar-tosubscript𝑢16subscript𝑢35similar-to0u16sim u35sim 0 and then the remaining components are both far from zero, with u100>0subscript𝑢1000u100>0 and u200<0subscript𝑢2000u200<0. This case is effectively the same as the toy example above where a broad Gaussian is being subtracted from a narrow Gaussian. The result is that we expect a single node in the probability amplitude and this is reflected in the right-hand plot of Fig. . Turning our attention to State 2 in Fig. , we note the u16subscript𝑢16u16 values are small relative to the u35subscript𝑢35u35 values and do not create a node. However sign oscillations from u35subscript𝑢35u35 to u100subscript𝑢100u100 to u200subscript𝑢200u200, all of which have significant magnitudes, induce 2 nodes in the interpolator wave function. Similarly, State 3 has 3 nodes. At this point we have identified the masses of three states in the spectrum, and have further classified these as being a 1​s, 2​s1𝑠2𝑠1s,,2s and 3​s3𝑠3s state. Interestingly, while the 1​s1𝑠1s state aligns well with the Δ​Δ1232Delta, the 2​s2𝑠2s state has a mass around ∼2.15similar-toabsent2.15sim 2.15 GeV so cannot be associated in a significant manner with the Δ​Δ1600Delta. Thus it appears that this state is associated with 2- and 3-body scattering channels. The use of Hamiltonian Effective Field Theory (HEFT) as a Lüscher adjacent method will allow us to provide insight in a forthcoming paper into which channels may be useful in future lattice studies for examining the Δ​Δ1600Delta resonance.
content_sections.IV Additional Results: JP=3/2−,1/2±super: While the spin-3/2 even-parity case is of most interest in the current study, we also report results for the spin-1/2 states as well as odd-parity states for both spins. The spectra for these cases are given in Figs. , and , with numerical values for our fits given in Tables, and . While we are not able to extract excited states for all of these cases, we can observe a distinct trend in the ground state masses: • the 3/2+3superscript23/2+ has the lowest mass. • the 3/2−3superscript23/2- and 1/2−1superscript21/2- are a little higher in energy, with similar masses. • the 1/2+1superscript21/2+ has a mass well above these states. This is consistent with previous CSSM results using quenched QCD citebib.bib42, and has good alignment with the resonances observed in experiment citebib.bib23.

Considering the resonance positions in Table, we note alignment between our results and those from the PDG, in that the ground state 1/2−1superscript21/2- and 3/2−3superscript23/2- states are nearby to one another, and likewise for the corresponding excited states. These appear to correspond approximately to the Δ​​(1/2−)Δ16201superscript2Delta(1/2-) and Δ​​(3/2−)Δ17003superscript2Delta(3/2-), and the Δ​​(1/2−)Δ19001superscript2Delta(1/2-) and Δ​​(3/2−)Δ19403superscript2Delta(3/2-), respectively. This suggests these resonances may be single-particle dominated states dressed by their decay channels. Furthermore, there is excellent agreement across all our lowest-lying masses with those reported by HSC, as well as Khan et al. Our 3/2−3superscript23/2- results are competitive with those of the other studies, and show fair agreement even at the first excitation level. The 1/2+1superscript21/2+ case presents the greatest challenge to our approach. Even the lowest-lying state could not be extracted at the lightest pion mass, and no excited states were able to be extracted at any pion mass. This is due to the initial interpolating field we use being constructed for coupling primarily to spin-3/2 states. Once we project both the spin and parity, there is little signal left for the spin-1/2 case. This is particularly problematic in the 1/2+1superscript21/2+ case since the lowest-lying state is expected to lie much higher in energy than the other cases. While the 1/2+1superscript21/2+ results are clearly not competitive with those of the other groups, there is approximate agreement with their findings. A more suitable choice of interpolator basis is required for further study of this channel.

Similarly to the 3/2+3superscript23/2+ results, we can also consider the 𝒖𝒖u vector components for each of these states, and visualise the resulting descriptions of the wave functions. However, there is a subtle point worth mentioning here, specifically how the odd-parity properties are accessed. In particular, as was outlined in Section, the parity nature of our correlators is dictated by the presence of a γ5subscript𝛾5gamma5 matrix in the overlap of interpolating fields and baryon states. We thus expect that odd-parity character should be contained in only the spinor part of the wave function. This means that even for the odd-parity baryons, we should expect a radially symmetric description of the spatial wave function, just as was obtained in Fig. . The visualisations of the interpolator wave functions for the 3/2−3superscript23/2- and 1/2−1superscript21/2- states are shown in Figs. and . Note that while we are able to cleanly extract all 𝒖𝒖u vector components for all 4 energy states in each spin-parity pairing, we only show the wavef function plots for which we were also able to extract masses. The 1/2+1superscript21/2+ case can also be assigned a Gaussian superposition for each energy state, but since we were only able to extract a ground state energy in this case, we omit the plots of the associated Gaussian distributions. Once again, we see that the first excited state involves a node in its structure. For the 3/2−3superscript23/2- case, our results suggest that the Δ​​(3/2−)Δ19403superscript2Delta(3/2-) may not be a simple excitation of the Δ​​(3/2−)Δ17003superscript2Delta(3/2-), since it lies well below the excitation energy determined herein. This resonance may be associated with one or more scattering channels in a non-trivial way, as has been suggested for the Δ​​(3/2+)Δ16003superscript2Delta(3/2+) citebib.bib43. For the 1/2−1superscript21/2- case, there is doubt whether the Δ​​(1/2−)Δ19001superscript2Delta(1/2-) or even the Δ​​(1/2−)Δ21501superscript2Delta(1/2-) are simple radial excitations of the Δ​​(1/2−)Δ16201superscript2Delta(1/2-), owing to a substantial energy gap between these and the observed 2​s2𝑠2s excitation energy on the lattice. Again, a coupled-channel analysis approach may shed some light on the structure of these resonances.
content_sections.V Conclusion: We have presented an analysis of the low-lying ΔΔDelta-baryons using techniques within the framework of lattice QCD. Our focus is on identifying the position of the 2​s2𝑠2s excitation of the Δ​​(3/2+)Δ12323superscript2Delta(3/2+) in the spectrum. Our technique of superposing smeared fermion fields in constructing our baryon sources lends itself to identifying the nodes of wave functions, thereby identifying radial excitations of states obtained by the variational method. This has allowed us to conclude the following for the energy eigenstates observed in our calculations: 1. The lowest-lying state having an energy in the Δ​​(3/2+)Δ12323superscript2Delta(3/2+) resonance region is identified as a 1​s1𝑠1s state. 2. The first excitation has an energy of 2.14​2.1452.14 GeV in the lightest mass ensemble, with only weak dependence on quark mass evident across the considered ensembles. This state is identified as a 2​s2𝑠2s state, and thus the radial excitation of the Δ​​(3/2+)Δ12323superscript2Delta(3/2+). There is a substantial energy gap between this state and the Δ​​(3/2+)Δ16003superscript2Delta(3/2+) and it is unlikely to be strongly associated with this resonance. We note that this situation is similar to that observed in Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib18 where the 2​s2𝑠2s excitation was resolved at 1.90 GeV, well above the position of the Roper resonance at 1.44 GeV. 3. The second excitation is identified as a 3​s3𝑠3s state with two nodes in the wave function. The energy of this state on our 3 fm lattice is large at 3.10 GeV. When compared with the 1​s1𝑠1s and 2​s2𝑠2s energies of 1.26 and 2.14 GeV respectively, one observes a mass splitting of ∼0.9similar-toabsent0.9sim 0.9 GeV between the radial excitations, very large compared to conventional quark models. We conclude that the missing baryon resonances problem of the last three decades follows from incorrectly tuning quark model parameters to reproduce the N​𝑁1440N Roper resonance and the Δ​Δ1600Delta resonance as 2​s2𝑠2s radial excitations. We now know the 2​s2𝑠2s radial excitations sit at 1.9 and 2.1 GeV for the N𝑁N and ΔΔDelta respectively. Thus there is an important opportunity for the development of modern quark models whose spectra are compatible with this modern understanding of quantum field theory. Aside from the main results for the 3/2+3superscript23/2+ channel, we have also reported results for the 3/2−, 1/2+3superscript21superscript23/2-,,1/2+ and 1/2−1superscript21/2- spectra. Here we resolved a node in the first excitations of the 1/2−1superscript21/2- and 3/2−3superscript23/2-states illustrating the internal structure of these states for the first time. Our spectra are qualitatively similar to other collaborations pursuing these high-energy excitations. Since a simple three-quark dominated model appears to be insufficient to describe the structure of the Δ​Δ1600Delta, attention must turn to interactions in multi-particle meson-baryon channels. It is expected that the dominant scattering channels of π​N𝜋𝑁pi N and π​Δ𝜋ΔpiDelta will play a key role in describing this resonance. Here a coupled-channel analysis in the framework of Hamiltonian Effective Field Theory holds the promise to resolve the structure of this resonance citebib.bib41. Acknowledgements. LH would like to thank Ryan Bignell for discussions and guidance in the early stages of this work. This work was supported by an Australian Government Research Training Program Scholarship and with supercomputing resources provided by the Phoenix HPC service at the University of Adelaide. This research was undertaken with the assistance of resources from the National Computational Infrastructure (NCI), provided through the National Computational Merit Allocation Scheme, and supported by the Australian Government through Grant No. LE190100021 via the University of Adelaide Partner Share. This research was supported by the Australian Research Council through Grants No. DP190102215 and DP210103706 (DBL), and DP230101791 (AWT). WK was supported by the Pawsey Supercomputing Centre through the Pawsey Centre for Extreme Scale Readiness (PaCER) program.

sorted_papers.title: Bayesian model averaging for analysis of lattice field theory results
sorted_papers.abstract:
Statistical modeling is a key component in the extraction of physical results
from lattice field theory calculations. Although the general models used are
often strongly motivated by physics, many model variations can frequently be
considered for the same lattice data. Model averaging, which amounts to a
probability-weighted average over all model variations, can incorporate
systematic errors associated with model choice without being overly
conservative. We discuss the framework of model averaging from the perspective
of Bayesian statistics, and give useful formulae and approximations for the
particular case of least-squares fitting, commonly used in modeling lattice
results. In addition, we frame the common problem of data subset selection
(e.g. choice of minimum and maximum time separation for fitting a two-point
correlation function) as a model selection problem and study model averaging as
a straightforward alternative to manual selection of fit ranges. Numerical
examples involving both mock and real lattice data are given.

sections.6: Morningstar, Bayesian curve fitting for lattice gauge theorists, Nucl
sections.7: Chang et al., A per-cent-level determination of the nucleon axial coupling from quantum chromodynamics, Nature 558, 91 (2018), arXiv:1805.12130 [hep-lat] .
sections.11: Takeuchi, Distribution of information statistics and criteria for adequacy of models, Suri-Kagaku (Mathematical Sciences) 153, 12 (1976).
sections.12: Akaike, Information theory and an extension of the maximum likelihood principle, in Selected papers of hirotugu akaike (Springer, 1998) pp
sections.13: Stone, An asymptotic equivalence of choice of model by cross-validation and akaike’s criterion, Journal of the Royal Statistical Society
sections.14: Jeffreys, The theory of probability (OUP Oxford, 1998).
sections.15: Akaike, Information theory and an extension of the maximum likelihood principle, in Selected papers of hirotugu akaike (Springer, 1998) pp
sections.16: Takeuchi, Distribution of information statistics and criteria for adequacy of models, Suri-Kagaku (Mathematical Sciences) 153, 12 (1976).
sections.17: Borsanyi et al., Leading hadronic contribution to the muon 2 magnetic moment from lattice QCD, Nature 593, 51 (2021), arXiv:2002.12347 [hep-lat] .
sections.22: Anscombe, Graphs in statistical analysis, The American Statistician 27, 17 (1973), https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/pdf/10.1080/00031305.1973.10478966 .

qualitative: Model averaging provides a rigorous Bayesian framework to account for systematic errors due to model choice in lattice QCD analyses, particularly in fitting two-point correlation functions.

The choice of fit range (e.g., minimum and maximum time separation) in two-point correlator fits is equivalent to a model selection problem, and model averaging offers a principled alternative to manual data subset selection.

Excited-state contributions in two-point correlators are inherently infinite in number (due to spectral decomposition), necessitating truncation; model averaging helps quantify the uncertainty from such truncation without being overly conservative.

Bayesian model averaging combines results from multiple models (e.g., single-state vs. two-state fits) by weighting them according to their posterior probabilities, allowing for coherent uncertainty propagation.

The approach does not require models to be nested, making it flexible for comparing physically motivated but structurally different models (e.g., different numbers of exponentials in a correlator fit).

The commonly used practice of estimating systematic errors via the full variation across different fit ranges is often overly conservative; model averaging provides a more nuanced and statistically sound alternative.
The method is applicable beyond linear models and can be used for nonlinear least-squares fits typical in lattice QCD, including chiral and continuum extrapolations.
quantitative: Model weight approximation: $ \text{pr}(M|D) \approx \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\chi^2_{\text{aug}}(\mathbf{a}^*) + 2k + 2N_{\text{cut}}\right)\right] $, where $ \chi^2_{\text{aug}}(\mathbf{a}^*) $ is the augmented chi-squared, $ k $ is the number of fit parameters, and $ N_{\text{cut}} $ is the number of data points removed.
The model weight is derived under uniform model priors and is valid to leading order in the data sample size $ N $.
The augmented chi-squared $ \chi^2_{\text{aug}} $ accounts for both the fit quality and the complexity of the model, incorporating parameter uncertainty and data truncation.
The penalty term $ 2k + 2N_{\text{cut}} $ reflects the cost of model complexity and data truncation, balancing goodness-of-fit against overfitting and bias.

Model averaging allows for the computation of model-averaged expectation values of parameters of interest, combining results from multiple models (e.g., single-state and two-state fits) in a probability-weighted manner.

约束项检查：
