authors: William I. Jay and Ethan T. Neil

content_sections.I Introduction: One of the central problems in lattice field theory is that of model fitting and parameter estimation. This problem appears repeatedly in analysis of lattice results, from single two-point correlators up to joint chiral and continuum extrapolations of results from many simulation streams. The functional forms which appear in these cases are often notoriously difficult to work with; the sum of exponentials which models the two-point correlator is in general quite numerically unstable, and chiral and continuum extrapolations can involve nonlinear dependence on large numbers of unknown variables. Making analysis of lattice simulations even more challenging, many of the models appearing require an ever-increasing number of parameters as simulations become more precise. For example, chiral perturbation theory is an effective theory which will break down at large masses or momentum scales, and contains (in principle) an infinite number of low-energy constants. The spectral decomposition of a single two-point correlator also contains (in principle) an infinite tower of excited states. The Symanzik effective theory describing the appearance of lattice artifacts similarly contains an organized but infinite number of terms. These contributions are typically dealt with by truncating the model, and often the data as well. However, this can lead to subtle dependence in the results on the analyst’s choice of fit range and number of terms included in the model. This is not to say that lattice theorists are unaware of these potential sources of systematic error. The effects of model truncation and data truncation can be estimated by studying the variation of quantities of interest as the range of data included is varied, or additional model terms are added. However, the often-adopted approach of taking the full difference between these variations as a systematic error is somewhat crude and likely to be overly conservative in many cases. In this paper, we describe the technique of Bayesian model averaging as an alternative approach to these systematic errors, and outline its potential applications in the analysis of lattice simulation results. This approach allows for fully rigorous estimation of probability distributions for parameters of interest by combining results from several models. All models must reduce to a common model containing the parameter(s) to be estimated, but there is no other requirement that they be nested models. (For a continuum extrapolation of a matrix element e. g. , the reduced model can be simply be a single constant parameter, the value of the matrix element in the continuum limit. ) Bayesian model averaging is somewhat well-known in the statistical literature citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, although it is most often used in the context of linear models. Here we place no such restriction, giving formulae which can be used for arbitrary nonlinear models.1 Appendix defines the distinction between linear and nonlinear models. In general, the model probability weights required for model averaging are complicated integrals, but we will give approximate formulae which may be used with sufficient sample sizes. As we will show, combining statistical results obtained with a set of models M𝑀M relies on the estimation of the model weight, pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷mboxpr(M|D). We will also argue that the commonly-used procedure of applying cuts to the data can also be understood as a model selection problem. Our key result is that, at leading order in the data sample size N𝑁N, the model weight can be estimated by the Akaike information criterion (or “AIC”), modified with a penalty term for cutting away data points: pr​(M|D)≈exp⁡[−12​(χaug2​(𝐚⋆)+2​k+2​Ncut)]prconditional𝑀𝐷12superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆2𝑘2subscript𝑁cutmboxpr(M|D)approxexpleft[-frac12left(chirm aug2(astar)+2k+2Nrm cutright)right] (this is , simplified for the case of uniform model priors). Here χaug2​(𝐚⋆)superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆chirm aug2(astar) is the standard best-fit augmented chi-squared citebib.bib6 (see below), k𝑘k is the number of fit parameters, and Ncutsubscript𝑁cutNrm cut is the number of removed data points as defined in . Our work is partially inspired by the Bayesian approach to effective field theory advocated by Schindler and Phillips citebib.bib7. Other examples of using weighted averages over models and Bayesian ideas in lattice analysis include citebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15. We believe that our treatment is the first attempt to lay out the procedure rigorously and in a fully Bayesian framework for a lattice field theory audience. An outline of the paper is as follows. In , we describe the basic Bayesian framework for model averaging and derive formal results for model-averaged expectation values. specializes to the case of least-squares fitting and derives an approximate formula for the model probabilities which are needed for model averaging. gives a numerical example of model averaging applied to mock data. In , we discuss the common problem of data subset selection, reframing it as a model variation problem in order to apply model averaging. shows an example application to the common task of fitting a two-point correlation function and demonstrates the effectiveness of model averaging as a replacement for choosing cuts on the data. We make some concluding remarks in . A detailed discussion of bias correction in the estimation of model probability is given in . and describe some technical details related to the chi-squared function and linear vs. nonlinear models.
content_sections.II Bayesian framework: The basic analysis problem is as follows: we wish to describe a dataset D𝐷D using a base model M0subscript𝑀0M0 in order to determine the value of one or more common parameters 𝐚csubscript𝐚𝑐ac. In the example of a continuum extrapolation, M0subscript𝑀0M0 could simply be the value of a specific matrix element in the continuum limit. In estimating the parameters 𝐚csubscript𝐚𝑐ac, we are often led to consider several extensions of the base model M0subscript𝑀0M0 which contain various unphysical or uninteresting terms, like lattice artifacts or undetermined excited states. This extends our study to a space of models M𝑀M, but our basic interest is still in estimation of the parameters of M0subscript𝑀0M0. All of the models in M𝑀M must contain M0subscript𝑀0M0, in the sense that marginalizing over additional parameters 𝐚msubscript𝐚𝑚am reduces them to M0subscript𝑀0M0. (Note that the set 𝐚msubscript𝐚𝑚am implicitly depends on the choice of model M𝑀M. ) It is important to note that the base model M0subscript𝑀0M0 itself does not necessarily have to be contained in the set M𝑀M of models that are actually fit to the data. As a simple example, in a continuum extrapolation it is certainly not necessary to include the continuum-only model M0subscript𝑀0M0 (without any lattice spacing dependence) in the set of fits. Indeed, a continuum-only model would surely describe the data poorly in this example. To obtain the marginal probabilities for the common parameters, we marginalize over both models and additional parameters citebib.bib7: pr​(𝐚c|D)=∑M∫𝑑𝐚m​pr​(D|𝐚,M)​pr​(𝐚|M)​pr​(M)pr​(D). prconditionalsubscript𝐚𝑐𝐷subscript𝑀differential-dsubscript𝐚𝑚prconditional𝐷𝐚𝑀prconditional𝐚𝑀pr𝑀pr𝐷mboxpr(ac|D)=sumMint damfracmboxpr(D|a,M)mboxpr(a|M)mboxpr(M)mboxpr(D). where “pr” denotes a probability distribution, and the set of all parameters 𝐚𝐚a is the union of 𝐚csubscript𝐚𝑐ac and 𝐚msubscript𝐚𝑚am. In principle, this formula assumes that all parameters 𝐚𝐚a are dimensionless. In practice, we will be interested in model weights which will depend only on ratios of probabilities, so any units will tend to cancel. If we can carry out the integrals and explicitly construct pr​(𝐚c|D)prconditionalsubscript𝐚𝑐𝐷mboxpr(ac|D), then expectation values for arbitrary functions of the common parameters 𝐚csubscript𝐚𝑐ac are immediately available: ⟨f​(ac)⟩=∫𝑑ac​f​(ac)​pr​(ac|D),delimited-⟨⟩𝑓subscripta𝑐differential-dsubscripta𝑐𝑓subscripta𝑐prconditionalsubscripta𝑐𝐷left f(ac)right=int dacf(ac)mboxpr(ac|D), from which we can construct the standard mean, variance, and so forth. However, evaluating the integrals in the “master formula” is generally quite difficult, especially in the context of the complicated non-linear models appearing in lattice analyses.2Direct Monte Carlo evaluation of such integrals is an intriguing option which deserves more attention in the context of lattice studies, in which much more complicated integrals are evaluated as a matter of course. This method seems to have been explored in Ref. citebib.bib16. However, we will not pursue this approach here. For our present purposes, it is more interesting to observe that we can reconstruct the combined estimate from the individual model fit results. Applying Bayes’ theorem and using elementary properties of conditional probability gives ⟨f​(𝐚c)⟩Msubscriptdelimited-⟨⟩𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐𝑀displaystyleleft f(ac)rightM =∫𝑑𝐚c​f​(𝐚c)​pr​(𝐚c|M,D)absentdifferential-dsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐prconditionalsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑀𝐷displaystyle=int dacf(ac)mboxpr(ac|M,D) =∫𝑑𝐚c​f​(𝐚c)​pr​(𝐚c,M,D)pr​(M,D)absentdifferential-dsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐prsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑀𝐷pr𝑀𝐷displaystyle=int dacf(ac)fracmboxpr(ac,M,D)mboxpr(M,D) =∫𝑑𝐚c​f​(𝐚c)​pr​(D|𝐚c,M)​pr​(𝐚c,M)pr​(M|D)​pr​(D)absentdifferential-dsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐prconditional𝐷subscript𝐚𝑐𝑀prsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑀prconditional𝑀𝐷pr𝐷displaystyle=int dacf(ac)fracmboxpr(D|ac,M)mboxpr(ac,M)mboxpr(M|D)mboxpr(D) =∫𝑑𝐚c​f​(𝐚c)​pr​(D|𝐚c,M)​pr​(𝐚c|M)​pr​(M)pr​(M|D)​pr​(D)absentdifferential-dsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐prconditional𝐷subscript𝐚𝑐𝑀prconditionalsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑀pr𝑀prconditional𝑀𝐷pr𝐷displaystyle=int dacf(ac)fracmboxpr(D|ac,M)mboxpr(ac|M)mboxpr(M)mboxpr(M|D)mboxpr(D) =1pr​(M|D)​∫𝑑𝐚c​f​(𝐚c)​pr​(𝐚c,M|D). absent1prconditional𝑀𝐷differential-dsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐prsubscript𝐚𝑐conditional𝑀𝐷displaystyle=frac1mboxpr(M|D)int dacf(ac)mboxpr(ac,M|D). But now if we marginalize the integral on the right-hand side over the space of models M𝑀M, we just obtain the total, model-independent expectation value for f𝑓f: ∑M∫𝑑𝐚c​f​(𝐚c)​pr​(𝐚c,M|D)=∫𝑑𝐚c​f​(𝐚c)​pr​(𝐚c|D)=⟨f​(𝐚c)⟩. subscript𝑀differential-dsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐prsubscript𝐚𝑐conditional𝑀𝐷differential-dsubscript𝐚𝑐𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐prconditionalsubscript𝐚𝑐𝐷delimited-⟨⟩𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐sumMint dacf(ac)mboxpr(ac,M|D)=int dacf(ac)mboxpr(ac|D)=left f(ac)right. Thus, we arrive at the relation ⟨f​(𝐚c)⟩=∑M⟨f​(𝐚c)⟩M​pr​(M|D). delimited-⟨⟩𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐subscript𝑀subscriptdelimited-⟨⟩𝑓subscript𝐚𝑐𝑀prconditional𝑀𝐷left f(ac)right=sumMleft f(ac)rightMmboxpr(M|D). This is the central formula of interest for purposes of model averaging. It shows that all of the moments of the fully combined probability distribution function can be obtained as a weighted average over individual model information, with the weight factors given by the posterior probability pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷mboxpr(M|D) for each individual model. Due to its role in model averaging, we will refer to pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷mboxpr(M|D) interchangeably as the “posterior probability” or as the “model weight. ” The model weight itself can be expressed as an integral over the parameter space, pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷displaystylemboxpr(M|D) =∫𝑑𝐚​pr​(M,𝐚|D)absentdifferential-d𝐚pr𝑀conditional𝐚𝐷displaystyle=int da mboxpr(M,a|D) =∫𝑑𝐚​pr​(D|𝐚,M)​pr​(𝐚,M)pr​(D)absentdifferential-d𝐚prconditional𝐷𝐚𝑀pr𝐚𝑀pr𝐷displaystyle=int da fracmboxpr(D|a,M)mboxpr(a,M)mboxpr(D) =∫𝑑𝐚​pr​(D|𝐚,M)​pr​(𝐚|M)​pr​(M)pr​(D). absentdifferential-d𝐚prconditional𝐷𝐚𝑀prconditional𝐚𝑀pr𝑀pr𝐷displaystyle=int da fracmboxpr(D|a,M)mboxpr(a|M)mboxpr(M)mboxpr(D). These probabilities are normalized to unity, ∑Mpr​(M|D)=1,subscript𝑀prconditional𝑀𝐷1sumMmboxpr(M|D)=1, which follows immediately from the definition of conditional probabilities and the marginalization formula, ∑Mpr​(M,D)=pr​(D)subscript𝑀pr𝑀𝐷pr𝐷sumMmboxpr(M,D)=mboxpr(D). Another (but perhaps more physical) argument to obtain the same result is that the expectation of the unit operator ⟨1⟩delimited-⟨⟩1left 1right should be unity independent of model choice. The formulae presented so far are completely general. However, certain common choices used for the estimators of the likelihood function and other quantities can introduce bias. Any such biases should be corrected to guarantee convergence to correct results. We discuss an important bias correction in detail in below.

content_sections.III Least-squares fitting III.1 Bias cor: Estimator bias occurs when a sample estimator differs from the “true” underlying population value it is approximating. A common example of such a bias occurs in the naive estimator of variance for data drawn from an underlying Gaussian distribution. In this example, the bias is of order 1/N1𝑁1/N, where N𝑁N is the sample size, which means that it will be automatically removed in the limit of large N𝑁N. However, there are also examples of asymptotic biases which do not vanish as N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty. The sample maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) of the log likelihood function, χaug2​(𝐚⋆)superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆chirm aug2(astar), suffers from such an asymptotic bias. Roughly speaking, because the MLE maximizes the sample log-likelihood, it tends to overshoot the true asymptotic value. Fundamentally, this bias arises not from the choice of the MLE but rather from finite-sample-size fluctuations in the data itself. To ensure convergence to the correct asymptotic model weight, we define the bias-corrected model weight to be citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21 pr​(M|D)B​C=exp⁡(−tr​[J−1​(𝐚⋆)​I​(𝐚⋆)])×∫𝑑𝐚​pr​(D|𝐚,M)​pr​(𝐚|M)​pr​(M)pr​(D)prsubscriptconditional𝑀𝐷𝐵𝐶trdelimited-superscript𝐽1superscript𝐚⋆𝐼superscript𝐚⋆differential-d𝐚prconditional𝐷𝐚𝑀prconditional𝐚𝑀pr𝑀pr𝐷mboxpr(M|D)BC=expleft(-texttr[J-1(astar)I(astar)]right)timesint dafracmboxpr(D|a,M)mboxpr(a|M)mboxpr(M)mboxpr(D) where I𝐼I and J𝐽J are the sample estimates of the log-likelihood Fisher information matrix and the (negative) Hessian matrix, respectively. These matrices are defined in , which also gives an informal derivation of the bias correction term, ∼tr​[J−1​(𝐚⋆)​I​(𝐚⋆)]similar-toabsenttrdelimited-superscript𝐽1superscript𝐚⋆𝐼superscript𝐚⋆simtexttr[J-1(astar)I(astar)]. When the model M𝑀M is correctly specified, i. e. assuming that the “true model” from which the data are drawn can be described by M𝑀M, it is straightforward to show (see citebib.bib19, bib.bib20 and ) that as N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty, the matrices I𝐼I and J𝐽J become identical, so that J−1​(𝐚⋆)​I​(𝐚⋆)→1→superscript𝐽1superscript𝐚⋆𝐼superscript𝐚⋆1J-1(astar)I(astar)rightarrow 1, the k×k𝑘𝑘ktimes k identity matrix. For lattice gauge theory applications where the theoretical model rests on a firm physical foundation, the assumption of correct specification is likely to hold, and the bias correction simply becomes exp⁡(−k)𝑘exp(-k), counting the total number of parameters in the model. The appearance of the structure tr​[J−1​I]trdelimited-superscript𝐽1𝐼texttr[J-1I] is closely related to the Akaike information criterion citebib.bib22, bib.bib23 and its generalization, the Takeuchi information criterion citebib.bib18. We discuss this connection more below.

(For linear models, as defined in , this approximation is of course exact, since the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 function is quadratic in parameters 𝐚𝐚a. ) Within this approximation, the integral for model weight becomes Gaussian and can be evaluated analytically: pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷displaystylemboxpr(M|D) =∫𝑑𝐚​pr​(D|𝐚,M)​pr​(𝐚|M)​pr​(M)pr​(D)absentdifferential-d𝐚prconditional𝐷𝐚𝑀prconditional𝐚𝑀pr𝑀pr𝐷displaystyle=int dafracmboxpr(D|a,M)mboxpr(a|M)mboxpr(M)mboxpr(D) ≈∫𝑑𝐚​1(2​π)k/2​(detΣ)1/2​e−χaug2​(𝐚⋆)/2−12​(𝐚−𝐚⋆)T​Σ⋆−1​(𝐚−𝐚⋆)​pr​(M)pr​(D)absentdifferential-d𝐚1superscript2𝜋𝑘2superscriptΣ12superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆212superscript𝐚superscript𝐚⋆𝑇superscriptsuperscriptΣ⋆1𝐚superscript𝐚⋆pr𝑀pr𝐷displaystyleapproxint da frac1(2pi)k/2(dettildeSigma)1/2e-chirm aug2(astar)/2-frac12(a-astar)TSigmastar-1(a-astar)fracmboxpr(M)mboxpr(D) =pr​(M)pr​(D)​(2​π)−k/2​(detΣ)−1/2​[(2​π)k/2​e−χaug2​(𝐚⋆)/2​(detΣ⋆−1)−1/2]absentpr𝑀pr𝐷superscript2𝜋𝑘2superscriptΣ12delimited-superscript2𝜋𝑘2superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆2superscriptsuperscriptsuperscriptΣ⋆112displaystyle=fracmboxpr(M)mboxpr(D)(2pi)-k/2(dettildeSigma)-1/2left[(2pi)k/2e-chirm aug2(astar)/2(detSigmastar-1)-1/2right] =pr​(M)pr​(D)​(detΣ)−1/2​e−χaug2​(𝐚⋆)/2​(detΣ⋆)1/2. absentpr𝑀pr𝐷superscriptΣ12superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆2superscriptsuperscriptΣ⋆12displaystyle=fracmboxpr(M)mboxpr(D)(dettildeSigma)-1/2e-chirm aug2(astar)/2(detSigmastar)1/2. Thus, neglecting the term pr​(D)pr𝐷mboxpr(D) which is the same for all models, one finds the following form for the log-likelihood −2​log⁡(pr​(M|D))≈−2​log⁡(pr​(M))+χaug2​(𝐚⋆)+log​detΣ−log​detΣ⋆,2prconditional𝑀𝐷2pr𝑀superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆ΣsuperscriptΣ⋆-2log(mboxpr(M|D))approx-2log(mboxpr(M))+chirm aug2(astar)+logdettildeSigma-logdetSigmastar, Including the bias correction term introduced in gives the overall result −2​log⁡(pr​(M|D)BC)=−2​log⁡(pr​(M))+χaug2​(𝐚⋆)+log⁡detΣdetΣ⋆+2​tr​[J−1​(𝐚⋆)​I​(𝐚⋆)].2prsubscriptconditional𝑀𝐷BC2pr𝑀superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆ΣsuperscriptΣ⋆2trdelimited-superscript𝐽1superscript𝐚⋆𝐼superscript𝐚⋆displaystyle-2log(mboxpr(M|D)rm BC)=-2log(mboxpr(M))+chirm aug2(astar)+logfracdettildeSigmadetSigmastar+2 texttr[J-1(astar)I(astar)]. From , we see that the posterior probability pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷mboxpr(M|D) encapsulates the principle of Occam’s Razor: models with large χaug2​(𝐚⋆)superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆chirm aug2(astar) are penalized, but so are models which have a large number of free parameters. (As discussed above, the final bias-correction term reduces to 2​k2𝑘2k asymptotically, where k𝑘k is the number of parameters. ) Unfortunately, in the presence of any empirical priors and/or models with differing dimensions, an effect known as the Jeffreys-Lindley paradox citebib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26 leads to out-sized dependence on the prior widths that can severely distort the overall results. The presence of the paradox is linked closely to the use of “empirical priors” which are not based on true prior information and can be taken to be arbitrarily wide. Note that for estimation of the best-fit parameters 𝐚⋆superscript𝐚⋆astar in a fixed model there is no such distortion, and the use of empirical priors is not problematic. However, when the normalization of the likelihood is important the paradox can lead to nonsensical results if the prior width is taken to be extremely large. For the particular case at hand, instead of attempting to confront the Jeffreys-Lindley paradox head-on, we will instead argue that the effect of the (log​detΣ−log​detΣ⋆)ΣsuperscriptΣ⋆(logdettildeSigma-logdetSigmastar) terms will vanish asymptotically and so can be viewed as a 1/N1𝑁1/N effect in the sample size N𝑁N. For the sake of argument, imagine adopting the following cross-validation procedure: partition our full data set D𝐷D into a small “training set” DTsubscript𝐷𝑇DT, and the remaining data DTcsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑇𝑐DTc. Then imagine first fitting the training set DTsubscript𝐷𝑇DT to determine a set of fit parameters 𝐚Tsubscript𝐚𝑇aT. These results for 𝐚Tsubscript𝐚𝑇aT can then be used to fix priors on 𝐚𝐚a for the fit to the remaining data DTcsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑇𝑐DTc. In practice, the cost of such a procedure is that it would “use up” the data in DTsubscript𝐷𝑇DT to fix priors, reducing the overall statistical precision of the analysis. However, in the asymptotic limit N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty, both partitions DT,DTcsubscript𝐷𝑇superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑇𝑐DT,DTc approach the true asymptotic distribution and will yield exactly the same fit results. Therefore, in this limit Σ→Σ⋆→ΣsuperscriptΣ⋆tildeSigmarightarrowSigmastar and the determinant terms in cancel completely. In other words, this argument establishes that the effect of the determinant terms is subleading in the asymptotic limit, and they may be dropped even without explicit use of a cross-validation procedure. In the same N→∞→𝑁Ntoinfty limit, the bias-correction term reduces to twice the number of parameters (see ), and we recover the well-known Akaike information criterion (AIC) citebib.bib22, bib.bib23: −2​log⁡(pr​(M|D)BC)→N→∞AICM=−2​log⁡(pr​(M))+χaug2​(𝐚⋆)+2​k. →𝑁→2prsubscriptconditional𝑀𝐷BCsubscriptAICM2prMsuperscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆2k-2log(mboxpr(M|D)rm BC)xrightarrowNrightarrowinftyrmAICM=-2log(mboxpr(M))+chirm aug2(astar)+2k. This is our main result for the bias-corrected model weights in the limit N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty. We advocate use of in all cases; without a more complete treatment of finite-sample-size effects, there is no guarantee that inclusion of the determinant terms in will improve estimation of the model weights. From this point forward, we adopt the bias-corrected form as the default choice of pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷mboxpr(M|D) unless explicitly stated otherwise and thus drop the “BC” subscript. More generally, simply dropping the determinant terms from gives the Takeuchi information criterion (TIC)citebib.bib18. However, as discussed in , the TIC form of the bias correction is only necessary in the case of model mis-specification. For models that obviously fail to describe the data, the χaug2superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2chirm aug2 term in either information criterion will dwarf the size of the bias correction, so the distinction between TIC and AIC is most important in cases where little information is available about the true model. In the present context, strong physical motivation often exists for trusting the correctness of the models in use. We therefore advocate the use of the AIC for general model averaging purposes in lattice gauge theory.
content_sections.III Least-squares fitting III.3 Practica: To demonstrate the method and some key features, we begin by considering a simple toy model. We begin by specifying a quadratic “model truth” polynomial function, F​(x)=1.80−0.53​(x16)+0.31​(x16)2. 𝐹𝑥1.800.53𝑥160.31superscript𝑥162F(x)=1.80-0.53left(fracx16right)+0.31left(fracx16right)2. A set of N𝑁N mock data samples are generated for x∈1,2,…,16𝑥12…16xin1,2,. . . ,16 by taking the model truth for each point and adding Gaussian noise η​(x)𝜂𝑥eta(x), uncorrelated in x𝑥x with mean zero and standard deviation σ=1.0𝜎1.0sigma=1.0. The resulting mock data y​(x)=F​(x)+η​(x)𝑦𝑥𝐹𝑥𝜂𝑥y(x)=F(x)+eta(x) are plotted in (top panel) for the choice N=160𝑁160N=160; we will also study the N𝑁N-dependence. 4The code used to generate this practical example, as well as the synthetic correlation function example below, is publicly available at . We take as our space of model functions polynomials labeled by their degree m𝑚m, fm​(x)=∑j=0maj​(x16)j. subscript𝑓𝑚𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑗0𝑚subscript𝑎𝑗superscript𝑥16𝑗fm(x)=sumj=0majleft(fracx16right)j. with 0≤m≤50𝑚50leq mleq 5. We take the flat prior pr​(m)=1/6pr𝑚16mboxpr(m)=1/6, corresponding to minimal prior knowledge about the functional form of the model (except that it is polynomial. ) All parameter priors are taken to be Gaussian with mean 0 and width 10 (the parameters are, essentially, unconstrained. ) The results of the fits to individual models as well as the model-averaging results are shown in (bottom panel) and in . Note that although all models with m>2𝑚2m>2 provide a good description of the data in terms of χ2superscript𝜒2chi2, the bias-corrected model probability estimated through the AIC places relatively more weight on the simpler models, with the maximum probability assigned to the correct choice m=2𝑚2m=2. The model-averaged result is consistent with model truth and has slightly larger uncertainty than the individual fit to the correct model m=2𝑚2m=2. As noted, the results so far use a fixed sample size of N=160𝑁160N=160. We repeat the test as described above with several values of N∈[20,640]𝑁20640Nin[20,640], showing the final estimated result for a0subscript𝑎0a0 using various procedures in . The result of the model averaging procedure using the AIC is seen to be consistent with model truth in all cases, with an error that is uniformly smaller than the more conservative procedure of taking the full variation of the mean over all models with p>0.1𝑝0.1p>0.1 as a systematic error. The AIC model-averaged error is larger than the error on the “quadratic fixed” result using the known true quadratic model; this is to be expected, as using model averaging rather than a fixed model necessarily builds in an additional systematic error due to model uncertainty (see the discussion in Sec. . ) Although in this simple example the true model is known exactly, we emphasize that this situation is rare, and in the absence of such exact knowledge the use of fixed-model fits can result in underestimation of parameter errors. Omitting the bias-correction term and averaging using only the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 results to estimate model probability (the “naive” estimate) also tends to give slightly larger error than model averaging using AIC, but the results remain consistent with the correct answer. In the absence of the bias correction, the likelihood of the models with m>2𝑚2m>2 is overestimated. However, because those higher-order polynomial models include the m=2𝑚2m=2 model within their parameter space, they tend to estimate the correct value of the intercept a0subscript𝑎0a0 on average. As a result, there is no bias introduced into the mean result for a0subscript𝑎0a0 when using biased model probabilities in this particular example. However, the error bar is slightly overestimated when using the naive estimator.
content_sections.IV Data subset selection as a model vari: A routine part of modeling lattice Monte Carlo data is data subset selection, i. e. , choosing a “cut” on the data beyond which the model is not applied. A canonical and simple example is fitting a two-point correlation function C​(t)𝐶𝑡C(t) to extract the ground-state energy. The full model expected to describe this correlation function involves an infinite tower of exponentials, ∑i∞Ai​e−Ei​tsuperscriptsubscript𝑖subscript𝐴𝑖superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑖𝑡sumiinftyAie-Eit. In practice, one truncates the sum after a finite number of terms and then selects a minimum value tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min below which the data are simply ignored. Choosing the precise value of tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min is generally done by hand. Although this process is typically thought of as data selection problem, it can easily be reformulated as a model selection problem. In the previous example, the justification for ignoring data below tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min is partially one of expediency. If we are only interested in the first few states, it suffices to look at times with t≥tmin𝑡subscript𝑡mintgeq trm min where they dominate. Times below tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min will be heavily contaminated by contributions from the higher excited states, and little to no information about the first few states is lost by ignoring them. Based on this observation, we can define a joint model that describes the full data set. First, select a subset of the data and imagine fitting the model of choice M𝑀M to this subset as usual. Second, imagine fitting the remaining data to a “perfect” model with zero degrees of freedom. For example, the “perfect” model could be a polynomial with degree equal to Ncutsubscript𝑁cutNrm cut, but in principle other functional forms will also work. Because the “perfect” model has zero degrees of freedom, there exists a solution for its parameters for which the differences between the model and the sample means vanish exactly. To give an explicit construction, we first define a partition P𝑃P of the data vectors into yi=(yicut,yikeep)subscript𝑦𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖cutsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖keepyi=(yirm cut,yirm keep), where yikeepsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖keepyirm keep are the subset to be modeled and yicutsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖cutyirm cut are the cut data. We then define the corresponding partitioned model gM​(𝐚,P)subscript𝑔𝑀𝐚𝑃gM(a,P) as yi−gM​(𝐚,P)=yi−y¯cut,yi∈yicutyi−fM​(𝐚),yi∈yikeep,subscript𝑦𝑖subscript𝑔𝑀𝐚𝑃casessubscript𝑦𝑖superscript¯𝑦cutsubscript𝑦𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖cutsubscript𝑦𝑖subscript𝑓𝑀𝐚subscript𝑦𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖keepyi-gM(a,P)=begincasesyi-baryrm cut, yiin yirm cut yi-fM(a), yiin yirm keep,endcases where y¯cutsuperscript¯𝑦cutbaryrm cut is the sample average of the yicutsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖cutyirm cut. The partition-dependent log likelihood is then, dropping constant terms that do not change with fixed data set D𝐷D, −2​log⁡pr​(D|𝐚,M)=∑i=1Nχi2​(P)2prconditional𝐷𝐚𝑀superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑁superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑖2𝑃displaystyle-2logmboxpr(D|a,M)=sumi=1Nchii2(P) =∑i=1N(yi−gM​(𝐚,P))T​Σ−1​(yi−gM​(𝐚,P))absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑁superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖subscript𝑔𝑀𝐚𝑃𝑇superscriptΣ1subscript𝑦𝑖subscript𝑔𝑀𝐚𝑃displaystyle=sumi=1N(yi-gM(a,P))TSigma-1(yi-gM(a,P)) =∑i=1N(yikeep−fM​(𝐚))T​ΣP−1​(yikeep−fM​(𝐚))+(const)absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑁superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖keepsubscript𝑓𝑀𝐚𝑇superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖keepsubscript𝑓𝑀𝐚constdisplaystyle=sumi=1N(yirm keep-fM(a))TSigmaP-1(yirm keep-fM(a))+(rmconst) where ΣP−1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1SigmaP-1 is the suatrix of the full inverse data covariance matrix Σ−1superscriptΣ1Sigma-1 which corresponds to the data subset yikeepsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖keepyirm keep. All other terms involving the cut data contain the expression y¯cut−gM​(𝐚,P)superscript¯𝑦cutsubscript𝑔𝑀𝐚𝑃baryrm cut-gM(a,P) at least once and therefore vanish identically by construction, even in the presence of off-diagonal correlations between yikeepsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖keepyirm keep and yicutsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖cutyirm cut. Since matrix inversion does not generally commute with subspace projection, the matrix ΣP−1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1SigmaP-1 typically differs from (ΣP)−1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1(SigmaP)-1, the inverse of the covariance sub-matrix. However, in practical lattice applications (ΣP)−1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1(SigmaP)-1 is often used as an approximation to ΣP−1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1SigmaP-1; the difference between these matrices is given by terms that are suppressed whenever long-range (i. e. , further off-diagonal) correlations are generally smaller than short-range ones. An obstruction to using ΣP−1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1SigmaP-1 directly is that finite-sample estimates of the full covariance matrix Σ−1superscriptΣ1Sigma-1 are typically ill-conditioned. Therefore, in what follows we will use (ΣP)−1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑃1(SigmaP)-1. The result of this construction is that the contribution from the “perfect” model describing the data outside the chosen subset is Δ​χaug2=0Δsuperscriptsubscript𝜒aug20Deltachirm aug2=0. However, there remains a bias-correction term which accounts for the Ncutsubscript𝑁cutNrm cut additional model parameters used to describe the cut data yicutsuperscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖cutyirm cut. The bias correction is still necessary because although the “perfect” model exactly describes the data sample as given, the values of the perfect model parameters will fluctuate as additional data is added. The difference between the perfect model parameters at finite sample size and their asymptotic values leads to a bias correction as described in . We emphasize that the bias correction term itself does not vanish since the individual terms χi2​(𝐚)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑖2𝐚chii2(a) do not vanish identically for the perfect model, only the sum. Thus, the overall model probability for the joint model is easily seen to be obtained from the modified expression AICM,Ncut=−2​log⁡(pr​(M))+χaug2​(𝐚⋆)+2​k+2​Ncut,subscriptAICMsubscriptNcut2prMsuperscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆2k2subscriptNcutrmAICM,Nrm cut=-2log(mboxpr(M))+chirm aug2(astar)+2k+2Nrm cut, where χaug2​(𝐚⋆)superscriptsubscript𝜒aug2superscript𝐚⋆chirm aug2(astar) is evaluated only for the model M𝑀M and for data within our selected subset. The result depends only on quantities that may be estimated from the subset model fit, and on counting factors. As a result, in practice we do not need to construct the “perfect” model at all. We note that a similar penalty term for removal of data points was also proposed in citebib.bib14 in a frequentist context. Although we were motivated by the example of a two-point correlator where the data are cleanly divided into two subsets along a single dimension, the argument above holds for arbitrarily complex subdivisions of the full data set. Whatever subset of the data we choose to fit explicitly to model M𝑀M, the joint model which also describes the remaining data will contribute an additional factor of 2​Ncut2subscript𝑁cut2Nrm cut to the information criterion. We can also consider a set of models M𝑀M and perform ordinary model averaging over the joint space defined by M𝑀M and the parameters that uniquely define a data subset.

To test the data subset selection procedure, we set up another toy-model example resembling a two-point correlation function, following the example description above. The “model truth” in this case is a two-state exponential, F​(t)=2.0​e−0.8​t+10.4​e−1.16​t. 𝐹𝑡2.0superscript𝑒0.8𝑡10.4superscript𝑒1.16𝑡F(t)=2.0e-0.8t+10.4e-1.16t. To generate synthetic data, we add correlated Gaussian noise η​(t)𝜂𝑡eta(t) with mean zero and variance 0.09. The noise is added fractionally to the data, i. e. , the synthetic data are generated according to the formula y​(t)=F​(t)​(1+η​(t))𝑦𝑡𝐹𝑡1𝜂𝑡y(t)=F(t)(1+eta(t)). The correlation matrix of the noise takes the form ρt,t′=ρ|t−t′|subscript𝜌𝑡superscript𝑡′superscript𝜌𝑡superscript𝑡′rhot,tprime=rho|t-tprime|, i. e. equal to 1 on the diagonal and decreasing according to a power law as the temporal separation between points increases, similar to a real lattice QCD correlation function. We fix the numerical correlation coefficient ρ=0.6𝜌0.6rho=0.6. N𝑁N mock data samples are generated for t∈0,1,…,31𝑡01…31tin0,1,. . . ,31. Additional trials in which the above parameters have been varied were also tried, including using uncorrelated Gaussian noise instead of correlated. No qualitative difference in the outcome of the tests was observed with these variations. For this test, we consider a single model which consists of a single exponential term, f​(t)=A0​e−E0​t. 𝑓𝑡subscript𝐴0superscript𝑒subscript𝐸0𝑡f(t)=A0e-E0t. This model is fit to all data in the range [tmin,31]subscript𝑡min31[trm min,31]. We consider all values of tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min from 1 to 28, with the goal of using model averaging with to obtain a combined result for the ground-state energy E0subscript𝐸0E0. The results of four independent trials following the above procedure with N=500𝑁500N=500 are shown in . Excited-state contamination, i. e. the influence of the second exponential state which is not present in our fit model, is clearly visible at low tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min. In each case, excellent agreement of the model-averaged result with model truth is seen. As in the polynomial example, the bias-corrected model probability is seen to weight simpler models more strongly, which in this case means favoring fits that cut away less of the data. In , we repeat the above exercise while varying the sample size N𝑁N, once again over the range N∈[20,640]𝑁20640Nin[20,640]. The results of model averaging using the AIC, i. e. using , show good consistency with the known result. Although the error of the model-averaged result is generally somewhat larger than the error for using a single fixed choice of tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min, the latter has an unaccounted-for systematic error due to model truncation. (Indeed, if we do not adjust tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min as N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty, we expect the result for E0subscript𝐸0E0 to become incompatible with the correct ground-state energy, as the contamination from the second state will eventually be resolved in a large enough sample. ) On the other hand, the error on the model-averaged result is generally much smaller than the more conservative full-width estimate, resulting from taking the full variation of mean results over all models with p>0.1𝑝0.1p>0.1 as a systematic error as in the polynomial example . In contrast to the polynomial example, here omission of the important bias-correction term in the AIC (i. e. dropping the Ncutsubscript𝑁cutNrm cut contribution to model weight) causes a drastic inflation of the error in the averaged result for E0subscript𝐸0E0. Once again, the interpretation is that omitting the bias correction causes overestimation of the likelihood for the more complicated models, in this case models with larger tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min. These models generally give results for E0subscript𝐸0E0 consistent with the correct answer, but with much larger errors, and the model-averaged result is altered accordingly.

We now consider the example of model averaging applied to a pion two-point correlation function from a real lattice QCD calculation. This correlator has been used in published work by the Fermilab Lattice and MILC collaborationscitebib.bib27. The underlying gauge-field ensemble used has a lattice spacing of a≈0.09𝑎0.09aapprox 0.09 fm and a pion mass of about 215 MeV. In this example, the correlation function was constructed using staggered fermions and corresponds to a pion with energy Eπ≈300subscript𝐸𝜋300Epiapprox 300 MeV. The results of our procedure are shown in . The top pane shows the effective mass and the final result of model averaging, which is consistent with by-eye expectations.5 The effective mass of a correlation function C​(t)𝐶𝑡C(t) is defined by meff​(t)=log⁡C​(t)/C​(t+1)subscript𝑚eff𝑡𝐶𝑡𝐶𝑡1mrm eff(t)=log C(t)/C(t+1). The oscillations in the effective mass are a familiar feature of staggered two-point correlation functions with nonzero momentum. The middle pane shows intermediate results for the ground-state energy E0subscript𝐸0E0 from individual fits.6 The ground-state energy E0subscript𝐸0E0 comes from the first term in the spectral decomposition, Eq. . Incidental technical complications related to staggered fermions and oscillating states are discussed in Ref. citebib.bib27, where this data was originally used (cf. their Eq. 2.6). The green band indicates the model-averaged result for the points shown. For clarity of presentation, the results in the middle pane are from fits with (1+1)11(1+1) states only, i. e. , 1 decaying state and 1 oscillating state. We also tried fits including (2+2)22(2+2), (3+3)33(3+3), and (4+4)44(4+4) states. The only qualitative difference from including more states is that good fits are obtained for smaller tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min. The model average is unchanged. The bottom pane shows the model weights for the fits with (1+1)11(1+1) states. As expected, the weights peak in the middle and taper off at both ends. When tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min is small, the fit quality rapidly declines due to contributions from excited states. When tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min is large, disfavors cutting too aggressively. In the intermediate region, the model weights fluctuate visibly with respect to ttminsubscript𝑡tmintrm tmin. This behavior is related to the fact to the that model weights of Eq. exhibit discrete jumps as Ncut∈ℤsubscript𝑁cutℤNrm cutinZ is varied. Although the total χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 is also expected to change by roughly one unit when a degree of freedom is removed, the precise value of course depends on the details of the data. Given the form of Eq. , there is no reason to expect that model weights should be a smooth function of Ncutsubscript𝑁cutNrm cut for a finite data sample. Overall, the model-averaged result agrees with intermediate results that went into the analysis of Ref. citebib.bib27 to better than 1​σ1𝜎1sigma citebib.bib28.Model averaging also shows promising results for extraction of more complicated matrix elements. In this example, we test the extraction of a K→π→𝐾𝜋Ktopi transition matrix ⟨π|J|K⟩quantum-operator-product𝜋𝐽𝐾pi|J|K, again using correlation functions that were a part of published work by the Fermilab Lattice and MILC collaborationscitebib.bib27. In this case, the underlying gauge-field ensemble has a lattice spacing of about 0.120.120.12 fm, with pion and kaon masses of about 220220220 and 515515515 MeV, respectively. Staggered fermions were also used for these correlators. The calculation occurs in the rest frame of the kaon. The pion momentum has been adjusted to be near the point of zero recoil, q2≡(pK−pπ)2≈0superscript𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑝𝐾subscript𝑝𝜋20q2equiv(pK-ppi)2approx 0. The methodology for extracting these matrix elements is complicated but relatively standard within the lattice community. The desired matrix element is the result of a joint correlated fit to two- and three-point functions. In order to visualize the result, it is standard to construct a ratio R​(t,T)𝑅𝑡𝑇R(t,T) of two- and three-point functions whose asymptotic plateau is proportional to the bare lattice matrix element. Here T𝑇T denotes the location of the sink operator which couples to the kaon. In conducting such a fit, the analyst is faced with several choices: the number of states in the pion channel (n+n)𝑛𝑛(n+n), the number of states in the kaon channel (m+m)𝑚𝑚(m+m), and the fit window t∈[tmin,T−tmin]𝑡subscript𝑡min𝑇subscript𝑡mintin[trm min,T-trm min]. We refer the reader to Ref. citebib.bib27 for additional details about fits like these. shows the result of model averaging for the matrix element. We adopt a flat prior model weight pr​(M)=Cpr𝑀𝐶mboxpr(M)=C for all choices of (m+m)𝑚𝑚(m+m) and (n+n)𝑛𝑛(n+n), which drops out of the model average. The top pane shows the ratio R​(t,T)𝑅𝑡𝑇R(t,T) for two different sink locations alongside the result of model averaging. The middle pane shows intermediate fit results and the model average. The particular choices made for each of the fits is displayed along the horizontal axes, with the fit window displayed on top and the number of states on the bottom. For instance, the leftmost point used (n+n)=(3+3)𝑛𝑛33(n+n)=(3+3) states for the pion channel, (m+m)=(3+3)𝑚𝑚33(m+m)=(3+3) states for the kaon channel and a fit range window t∈[3,T−3]𝑡3𝑇3tin[3,T-3]. Finally, the bottom pane gives the model weights. In this case, all the results displayed give consistent results, and the weight of the leftmost fit is essentially unity. This is Occam’s razor, as encoded by , at work. For matching results, the model with the fewest parameters and most data should be preferred. The model-averaged result, once appropriately converted into a form factor, agrees to better than 1​σ1𝜎1sigma with the published result of Ref. citebib.bib27 This example suggests another important application of the framework we are describing. A complete analysis of a lattice matrix element might consider a more general set of fits, e. g. , with different numbers of decaying and oscillating states in each channel or with different tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min cuts for the source and sink. Scanning over all possibilities can easily produce tens or hundreds of individual fit results. Finding an objective selection criterion for choosing a best-fit result can be difficult. The model weights in and offer a potential solution to this problem, particularly when used in conjunction with expert knowledge and the usual careful thinking.
content_sections.V Conclusion: We have presented a Bayesian approach to the problem of model averaging. The statistical methods we describe apply very generally, though our examples have focused on practical problems in lattice gauge theory. The context for regression problems is rather exceptional in many lattice studies, since the models often rest on firm theoretical foundations. For instance, multi-exponential fits to correlators are based on the spectral decomposition, which only requires the existence of a positive-definite transfer matrix. Effective field theory governs extrapolations to the chiral, continuum, or heavy-mass limit. If a model fails to describe the data, the simulation itself is rightfully viewed with additional scrutiny. Hypothesis testing is typically less important than reliably extracting the values of parameters capturing the physics of interest. When predictions from nested models (say, the NLO versus NNLO predictions from effective field theory) differ slightly, it is important to be able to produce a final number with associated statistical and systematic uncertainties. Bayesian model averaging is an attractive approach to problems like these. Two key practical results are the model-averaged mean and variance, and ; the general result allows for averaging of arbitrary expectation values of functions of fit parameters. These formulae rely on the model weights pr​(M|D)prconditional𝑀𝐷mboxpr(M|D). In general, the model weights are defined through complicated integrals. However, analytic results are available in the Gaussian approximation, which is exact for linear least-squares fitting. For nonlinear least-squares fitting, the approximation is expected to become increasingly good for larger data sets. For a fixed dataset with no cuts, is the final expression used to construct the model weights used in the examples. Since this expression is computable just using the familiar augmented χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 and the number of parameters in the model, it is easy to include and test in existing lattice analyses. However, it relies on taking an asymptotic limit in the sample size, and it would be interesting to study improved estimators at finite sample size in future work. A particularly nice application of these ideas is data subset selection, which we recast as a model variation problem. The basic observation was to reinterpret cuts on the data as additional model parameters, leading to a model weight given by . The model averaging approach gives a straightforward way to replace the common practice of tuning such data subset cuts by hand. Broadly speaking, perhaps the most attractive feature of Bayesian model averaging is the natural appearance of Occam’s razor. The model weights appearing in and favor models which use the fewest parameters while describing the most data. Inclusion of an asymptotic bias correction to the estimated likelihood, which yields the AIC as a model-selection criterion in the limit of large sample size, is crucial to the occurrence of this effect. Although this technique allows the data to remove much of the subjectivity from analyses including model variations, this does not extend to the choice of the model prior weights pr​(M)pr𝑀mboxpr(M). In the absence of specific and strong beliefs about particular models, we advocate for the use of a flat prior, i. e. weighting all models equally in the pr​(M)pr𝑀mboxpr(M). In all of the practical examples shown in the text, this is precisely what has been done. In particular, we emphasize that one should not attempt to impose parsimony through the model priors by overweighting models with fewer parameters; this principle (i. e. Occam’s razor) is built-in to the bias-corrected model weights as we have discussed. An interesting direction to explore in future work would be to study improved estimators at finite sample size, rather than relying on the asymptotic result to estimate the model weights. This will necessarily involve careful treatment of the covariance matrix terms in . It would also be interesting to explore direct Monte Carlo evaluation of the integral in , although the bias correction should be studied carefully in the context of whatever specific approach is used. Studying the interplay of model averaging with resampling methods such as jackknife and bootstrap, commonly used in lattice analyses, would likely be a useful extension of this work. It is worthwhile to compare Bayesian model averaging to another commonly-used technique in lattice data analysis, the empirical Bayes method citebib.bib6, bib.bib16, bib.bib15. In this approach, the number of fit parameters is increased until the description of the physics of interest (e. g. the ground state in a two-point correlation function) becomes stable. Empirical (data-driven) priors are used for the additional fit parameters, which are generally treated as nuisance parameters. The use of the empirical Bayes method provides an alternative to model averaging with fixed data as presented in . However, data subset selection can be used in conjunction with empirical Bayes modeling, and may be particularly useful for more complicated analyses where comparison of discrete model choices is required. Within the lattice community, the empirical Bayesian method is most widely developed and applied in the analysis of correlation functions. The usual empirical Bayesian methods also extend gracefully to “effective field theory fits” citebib.bib7, where power-counting arguments furnish firm theoretical motivation for imposing order-unity priors on certain coefficients. In such cases, one can simply add terms until the parameters of interest stabilize. However, for more generic fits (e. g. , when the correct power-counting scheme is not a priori obvious), adding a large number of terms may destabilize the fit and inject undesirable noise into the results. The generality and flexibility of model averaging makes it an appealing tool for analyzing difficult problems like these, particularly in conjunction with existing methods. Overall, we emphasize that the empirical Bayesian and model averaging techniques are complementary.
content_sections.V Conclusion V.1 Practical suggestions a: Model averaging has performed well for us in many test cases. However, as with any statistical tool, the techniques we describe should not be used blindly. In particular, model averaging should not be used as a substitute for plotting data and fits and thinking carefully about the resultscitebib.bib29. A basic assumption underlying this technique is that only statistically correct results are included in the model average. Including results for fits that fail to converge numerically, for example, will likely result in incorrect answers. Incomplete treatment of autocorrelation effects in the data will similarly yield invalid statistical estimates and thus invalid model-averaged results. The model weights of and are useful beyond model averaging à la . For instance, many lattice calculations oblige the analyst to make many choices beyond tminsubscript𝑡mintrm min. In this situation, the model weights can help guide the decision about which, say, dozen fit results (out of potentially hundreds) are most promising for further investigation and scrutiny using more familiar and established techniques. Model selection is equivalent to model averaging in the limit that a single model has very high probability of correctness; this situation can naturally emerge from the data analysis, as in the example shown in . Model averaging may be especially useful in the context of fitting models that contain discrete degrees of freedom that are not amenable to standard numerical minimization procedures. For example, a multi-exponential model ∑i∞Ai​e−Ei​tsuperscriptsubscript𝑖subscript𝐴𝑖superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑖𝑡sumiinftyAie-Eit in which the sign of the amplitudes Aisubscript𝐴𝑖Ai is a priori unknown could be studied with improved numerical stability by fixing the signs of all included amplitudes one by one, and then averaging together the results. In certain cases, we have found that the systematic errors due to model truncation or variation can be significantly overestimated by more conservative methods. Revisiting old lattice analyses which are limited by systematic errors related to model variation may be worthwhile. On the other hand, in our tests we have found excellent agreement between correlator fits with few states and those with many states; the combination of model averaging with few-state fits as a method could reduce problems related to numerical convergence and reduce the computational cost of fitting. Acknowledgements. We thank the members of the Fermilab Lattice and MILC collaborations for generously sharing their correlator data with us for use in numerical tests. We are especially grateful to Elvira Gámiz for providing intermediate results and helping compare against published results. We also thank Jozef Dudek, Andreas Kronfeld, Christoph Lehner, Taku Izubuchi, Tom DeGrand, Jason Chang, and Dan Hackett for helpful discussions and comments in various stages of this work. This work was supported in part by the U. S. Department of Energy (DOE), Office of Science, Office of High Energy Physics, under Award Number DE-SC0010005 (ETN). Fermilab is operated by the Fermi Research Alliance, LLC under contract No. DE-AC02-07CH11359 with the United States Department of Energy.

sorted_papers.title:
Progress report on testing robustness of the Newton method in data
  analysis on 2-point correlation function using a MILC HISQ ensemble
sorted_papers.abstract:
We report recent progress in data analysis on the two point correlation
functions which will be prerequisite to obtain semileptonic form factors for
the $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}\ell\nu$ decays. We use a MILC HISQ ensemble for the
measurement. We use the HISQ action for light quarks, and the Oktay-Kronfeld
(OK) action for the heavy quarks ($b$ and $c$). We used a sequential Bayesian
method for the data analysis. Here we test the new fitting methodology of
Benjamin J.~Choi in a completely independent manner.

sections.4: Amhis et al., Averages of b𝑏b-hadron, c𝑐c-hadron, and τ𝜏\tau-lepton properties as of 2021, 2206.07501.

qualitative: Two-point correlation functions in lattice QCD are essential for extracting semileptonic form factors in B→D(∗)ℓν decays, which probe the CKM matrix element |Vcb|.

The spectral decomposition of 2-point correlation functions includes contributions from both even and odd time-parity states due to the staggered fermion formalism, with odd-parity terms arising from temporal doublers.

The n+m fitting function accounts for n even time-parity states and m odd time-parity states, incorporating exponential decay terms with energy splittings ΔEi = Ei − Ei−2 and relative amplitudes ri = Ai/Ai−2.

The Newton method combined with a scanning method is used to robustly find initial parameter guesses for fitting, leveraging the fast convergence of Newton's method within a narrow range identified by the slower but reliable scanning method.

A multi-dimensional Newton method is applied after the scanning method narrows down the parameter space, ensuring efficient convergence to a global minimum when initial guesses are sufficiently close.
Time slice combinations used in the fitting must include tmin, and have equal numbers of even and odd time slices to maintain symmetry and avoid bias in the fit.
The fitting procedure involves testing multiple time slice combinations, discarding those that fail to converge within a few iterations (7–10), with a failure rate of about 70% for 1+1 fits.
The stopping criterion for the Newton method is max_i |q(ti)| < 10^−12, where q(ti) = (f(ti) − C(ti))/C(ti), ensuring high precision in the fit residuals.
quantitative: The 2-point correlation function is given by: C(t) = ∑_{n=0}^∞ (−1)^n (t+1) |⟨n|𝒪|0⟩|^2 (e^{-E_n t} + e^{-E_n (T−t)}).

The n+m fit function is: fn+m(t) = gn+m(t) + gn+m(T−t), where gn+m(t) = A0 e^{-E0 t} [1 + r2 e^{-ΔE2 t} (1 + ⋯ (1 + r_{2n−2} e^{-ΔE_{2n−2} t})⋯) − (−1)^t r1 e^{-ΔE1 t} (1 + ⋯ (1 + r_{2m−1} e^{-ΔE_{2m−1} t})⋯)].
The parameters are defined as: ri = Ai / Ai−2, ΔEi = Ei − Ei−2 for i ≥ 1, with E_{-1} = E0 and A_{-1} = A0.
For the 1+1 fit, the number of equations is N = 2(n+m) = 4.
The scanning method uses initial ranges: r1 ∈ [0, 1.5], ΔE1 ∈ [0, 1.0].
The Newton method typically converges in 7–10 iterations when successful.
The failure rate for the 1+1 fit is approximately 70%.
The stopping condition is: max_{i=1,…,N} |q(ti)| < 10^{-12}, where q(ti) = (f(ti) − C(ti)) / C(ti).

content_sections.1 Introduction: Semileptonic form factors for the B→D(∗)​l​ν→𝐵superscript𝐷𝑙𝜈Bto D(*)lnu decays can probe the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix element |Vc​b|subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏left|Vcbright| citebib.bib1. This requires the precise data analysis on 2-point correlation functions citebib.bib2. Here, we present our recent progress in data analysis on 2-point correlation functions. This work provides an independent cross-checking of the methodology developed by Benjamin Choi in Ref. citebib.bib3. We use the MILC HISQ ensembles for the numerical study. In Table , we summarize details on the MILC lattice a12m310. For bottom and charm quarks, we use the Oktay-Kronfeld (OK) action citebib.bib4. In Table , we summarize details on hopping parameters for bottom quarks, and light quark masses.

The n+m𝑛𝑚n+m fit implies that we include the n𝑛n even time-parity states and m𝑚m odd time-parity states in the fitting function.
content_sections.3 Newton method: When we do the least χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 fitting, we use the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) algorithm citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9 to minimize χ2superscript𝜒2chi2. This algorithm belongs to a category of the quasi-Newton method in an optimization problem citebib.bib10. The quasi-Newton method requires an initial guess as input to the fitting by construction. If a initial guess is good, the quasi-Newton method finds the minimum efficiently. If the initial guess is poor (out of radius of convergence), the number of iterations increases and the chance to find the local minima instead of the global minimum grows up. Therefore, it is essential to find a good initial guess close to the true solution (the global minimum), if we want to save computing resources. For this purpose, it is best to obtain initial guess directly from the data, as long as the computing time is negligibly small compared with that of the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 minimizer. It is the multi-dimensional Newton method combined with a scanning method citebib.bib3 that satisfies these conditions. The (1-dimensional) Newton method converges quadratically with respect to the distance from the true solution. Hence, it find a root in a few iterations, if the initial guess is sufficiently close to the true solution. However, if the initial guess is sufficiently far away from the true solution, it loses its merit completely. The scanning method converges slowly, but it can narrow down a range to find roots in a few iterations. If we combine the Newton method with the scanning method, it is possible to keep only the merits, while discarding the disadvantages. In other words, the scanning method finds a narrow range to find roots in a few iterations, and then the Newton method can find a root in a few iterations within the narrow range. Let us consider the 1+1111+1 fit as an example. We feed results for the 1+0101+0 fit as an initial guess for the A0,E0subscript𝐴0subscript𝐸0A0,E0 part of the 1+1111+1 fit parameters. In order to obtain initial guess for the remaining parameters r1subscript𝑟1r1 and Δ​E1Δsubscript𝐸1Delta E1, we use the 4-dimensional Newton method combined with the scanning method. The initial range is set to r1∈[0,1.5]subscript𝑟101.5r1in[0,1.5] and Δ​E1∈[0,1.0]Δsubscript𝐸101.0Delta E1in[0,1.0] for the scanning method. After a few iterations (1∼2similar-to121sim 2), the scanning method finds such a narrow range for r1subscript𝑟1r1 and Δ​E1Δsubscript𝐸1Delta E1 that we may use the Newton method citebib.bib3 to find an exact solution for Eqs. in a few iterations (7∼10similar-to7107sim 10). The Newton method uses the same time slice combination (e. g. t1=tmin,t2,t3,t4subscript𝑡1subscript𝑡subscript𝑡2subscript𝑡3subscript𝑡4t1=tmin,t2,t3,t4 for the 1+1111+1 fit) as the scanning method. First, we collect all the possible time slice combinations within the fit range ( tmin≤t≤tmaxsubscript𝑡𝑡subscript𝑡tminleq tleq tmax ; e. g. tmin=12subscript𝑡12tmin=12 and tmax=26subscript𝑡26tmax=26 for the 1+1111+1 fit), before using the scanning and Newton methods. A possible time slice combination should satisfy the following two conditions: 1) it must contains tminsubscript𝑡tmin, and 2) the number of even time slices must be equal to that of odd time slices. For each time slice combination, we run the Newton method until we consume all the time slice combinations. If the Newton method find a good solution in a few iterations (7∼10similar-to7107sim 10), then we keep it, and if it fails, we discard that time slice combination. The failure rate is about 70%percent7070% for the 1+1 fit. The Newton method finds a solution to satisfy the N𝑁N equations: q​(ti)=0𝑞subscript𝑡𝑖0q(ti)=0 with ti∈tmin,t2,t3,…,tNsubscript𝑡𝑖subscript𝑡subscript𝑡2subscript𝑡3…subscript𝑡𝑁tiintmin,t2,t3,ldots,tN, with N=2​(n+m)𝑁2𝑛𝑚N=2(n+m) for the n+m𝑛𝑚n+m fit (e. g. N=4𝑁4N=4 for the 1+1111+1 fit). q​(ti)=f​(ti)−C​(ti)C​(ti)=0. 𝑞subscript𝑡𝑖𝑓subscript𝑡𝑖𝐶subscript𝑡𝑖𝐶subscript𝑡𝑖0q(ti)=fracf(ti)-C(ti)C(ti)=0,. Here, C​(ti)𝐶subscript𝑡𝑖C(ti) is the 2pt correlator data coming from our numerical measurements, and f​(t)𝑓𝑡f(t) is the fitting function in Eq. . The stopping condition is maxi=1,…,N⁡|q​(ti)|<10−12. subscript𝑖1…𝑁𝑞subscript𝑡𝑖superscript1012maxi=1,dots,Nleft|q(ti)right|<10-12,.
content_sections.4 Fitting results: Here we describe our sequential Bayesian fitting procedure such as 1+0 fit →→to 1+1 fit →→to 2+1 fit →→to 2+2 fit in detail. We also present results for the n+m𝑛𝑚n+m fit in each stage of the sequential Bayesian method (SBM). We also explain how to perform stability tests at each stage of the SBM.

content_sections.4 Fitting results 4.2 1+1 Fit: We use results of the 1+0 fit to determine Bayesian priors for the 1+1 fit. We set the Bayesian prior widths (BPW) to the maximum fluctuation (σpm​fsuperscriptsubscript𝜎𝑝𝑚𝑓sigmapmf) or the signal cut (σps​csuperscriptsubscript𝜎𝑝𝑠𝑐sigmapsc) of the A0subscript𝐴0A0 and E0subscript𝐸0E0 parameters.2The signal cut means that the error (= noise) is the same as the average (= signal), when the parameters should be positive thanks to physical reasons. We choose min⁡(σpm​f,σps​c)superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑝𝑚𝑓superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑝𝑠𝑐min(sigmapmf,sigmapsc) for the BPW. Starting from tmin1+1=tmin1+0−2superscriptsubscript𝑡11superscriptsubscript𝑡102tmin1+1=tmin1+0-2, we run tmin1+1superscriptsubscript𝑡11tmin1+1 over the lower values until χ2/dofsuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/textdof overflows the appropriate criterion. In Fig. we present χ2/dofsuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/textdof as a function of tmin1+1superscriptsubscript𝑡11tmin1+1. Here we find that the optimal fit range is tmin1+1=12superscriptsubscript𝑡1112tmin1+1=12. In Fig. we present the residual r​(t)𝑟𝑡r(t) with the fit range 12≤t≤2812𝑡2812leq tleq 28. The 1+1 fit results are summarized in Table .
content_sections.4 Fitting results 4.3 2+1 Fit: First, we use the results of 1+1111+1 to set the BPW for 2+1212+1 fit. Starting from tmin2+1=tmin1+1−2superscriptsubscript𝑡21superscriptsubscript𝑡112tmin2+1=tmin1+1-2, we run tmin2+1superscriptsubscript𝑡21tmin2+1 over the lower values until χ2/dofsuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/textdof overflows the criterion. In Fig. we present χ2/dofsuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/textdof as a function of tmin2+1superscriptsubscript𝑡21tmin2+1. Here we find that tmin2+1=6superscriptsubscript𝑡216tmin2+1=6 is the optimal fit range for the 2+1 fit. In Fig. we present the residual r​(t)𝑟𝑡r(t) with the fit range 6≤t≤286𝑡286leq tleq 28. Here we adopt the same notation and convention as in Ref. citebib.bib12. sorted_papers.title: $\Xi_c-\Xi_c^{\prime}$ mixing From Lattice QCD
sorted_papers.abstract:
In heavy quark limit, the lowest-lying charmed baryons with two light quarks
can form an SU(3) triplet and sextet. The $\Xi_c$ in the SU(3) triplet and
$\Xi_c'$ in the sextet have the same $J^{PC}$ quantum number and can mix due to
the finite charm quark mass and the fact the strange quark is heavier than the
up/down quark. We explore the $\Xi_c$-$\Xi_c'$ mixing by calculating the
two-point correlation functions of the $\Xi_c$ and $\Xi_c'$ baryons from
lattice QCD. Based on the lattice data, we adopt two independent methods to
determine the mixing angle between $\Xi_c$ and $\Xi_c'$. After making the
chiral and continuum extrapolation, it is found that the mixing angle $\theta$
is $1.2^{\circ}\pm0.1^{\circ}$, which seems insufficient to account for the
large SU(3) symmetry breaking effects found in weak decays of charmed baryons.

qualitative: In the heavy quark limit, charmed baryons with two light quarks form an SU(3) triplet (Ξ_c) and sextet (Ξ_c'), which can mix due to finite charm quark mass and SU(3) flavor symmetry breaking from the heavier strange quark.
The Ξ_c–Ξ_c' mixing is studied using lattice QCD through two-point correlation functions of interpolating operators for the triplet and sextet states.
Two independent methods are employed to determine the mixing angle: direct fitting of energy levels and solving the generalized eigenvalue problem (lattice spectroscopy method).
Excited-state effects are addressed by using single- and two-state fits to the correlation function matrix, allowing for reliable extraction of the ground state energy and mixing parameters.
Chiral and continuum extrapolations are performed to obtain the physical mixing angle in the limit of zero pion mass and vanishing lattice spacing.

The mixing angle is found to be small (on the order of 1°), suggesting that Ξ_c–Ξ_c' mixing alone is insufficient to explain the large SU(3) symmetry breaking observed in weak decays of charmed baryons.
quantitative: The mixing angle is determined to be $\theta = 1.2^\circ \pm 0.1^\circ$ after chiral and continuum extrapolation.

The two-point correlation function matrix is defined as $\langle 0 | O_{\Xi_c}(\bar{3},6)(t) \bar{O}_{\Xi_c}(\bar{3},6) | 0 \rangle$, where $O_{\Xi_c}(\bar{3})$ and $O_{\Xi_c}(6)$ are interpolating operators for the anti-triplet and sextet baryons.

The results from different theoretical approaches show significant discrepancies: $\theta = 24.66^\circ \pm 0.90^\circ$ (from experiment), $\theta = 5.5^\circ \pm 1.8^\circ$ (QCD sum rules), $\theta = 8.12^\circ \pm 0.80^\circ$ (HQET), and $\theta \sim 1^\circ$ (this lattice QCD study).
The lattice simulations use recently generated configurations with multiple lattice spacings and pion masses to perform chiral and continuum extrapolations.

content_sections.I Introduction: Heavy baryons with a bottom or charm quark provide an ideal place to study the underlying theory for strong interactions. In heavy quark limit, charmed baryons can be elegantly classified according to heavy quark spin symmetrycitebib.bib1, bib.bib2. Ground states of baryons with one heavy quark can be categorized into two sets: if the light quark system is spinless, three baryons form an SU triplet; if the spin of the light quark system is one, then six charmed baryons form a sextet. However in reality, since the charm quark mass is finite and the strange quark is heavier than the up/down quark, charm baryons in the triplet and sextet are likely to mix with each other. An explicit realization is the Ξc−Ξc′subscriptΞ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′Xic-Xicprime mixing, which is a direct consequence of the effective heavy quark and flavor SU symmetry breaking. Weak decays of charmed baryons also contribute to the understanding of the electroweak sector of the standard model. In particular semileptonic decays of charmed baryons are valuable to extract the CKM matrix element |Vc​s|subscript𝑉𝑐𝑠|Vcs|(for some recent experimental measurements and lattice QCD calculations , please see Refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7 and many references therein). It is interesting to notice that based on the experimental measurements the flavor SU symmetry, which is a very useful tool widely applied in the analysis of weak decays of heavy mesons, is found to be sizably brokencitebib.bib8. Various mechanisms to explain this observation were explored in Ref. citebib.bib8 and a competitive explanation is through the Ξc−Ξc′subscriptΞ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′Xic-Xicprime mixingcitebib.bib9. Very interestingly, several recent works are devoted to determine the mixing angle from various methods, but the results are controversial. Based on experimental data on weak decays of ΞcsubscriptΞ𝑐Xic and Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc, it is found that a large mixing angle is called for: θ=24.66∘±0.90∘𝜃plus-or-minussuperscript24.66superscript0.90theta=24.66circpm 0.90circ in Ref. citebib.bib9, bib.bib10 and 16.27∘±2.30∘plus-or-minussuperscript16.27superscript2.3016.27circpm 2.30circ in Ref. citebib.bib11. A direct calculation in QCD sum rule gives θ=5.5∘±1.8∘𝜃plus-or-minussuperscript5.5superscript1.8theta=5.5circpm 1.8circ citebib.bib12, while in heavy-quark effective theory, it is found θ=8.12∘±0.80∘𝜃plus-or-minussuperscript8.12superscript0.80theta=8.12circpm 0.80circ citebib.bib13. Besides, an earlier lattice QCD exploration indicates a small mixing angle citebib.bib14. This paper presents an update-to-date exploration of the Ξc−Ξc′subscriptΞ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′Xic-Xicprime mixing from lattice QCD. Using the recently generated lattice configurations, we calculate the matrix elements ⟨0|OΞc(3¯,6)​(t)​O¯Ξc(3¯,6)​|0⟩quantum-operator-product0subscript𝑂superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐¯36𝑡subscript¯𝑂superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐¯3600 0|OXic(bar3,6)(t)barOXic(bar3,6)|0, where OΞc3¯subscript𝑂superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐¯3OXicbar3 and OΞc6subscript𝑂superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐6OXic6 denote the interpolators for the anti-triplet and sextet baryon respectively. With the results for the correlation function matrix, we use the direct fitting method to fit the energy levels and the mixing angle. As a comparison, the lattice spectroscopy method will also be used. After extracting the mixing angles with different lattice spacings and pion masses, we extrapolate the result to the chiral and continuum limit. We finally find that the mixing angle is at the order of 1∘superscript11circ, which is likely to indicate the necessity to include other SU symmetry-breaking effects in ΞcsubscriptΞ𝑐Xic decays. The rest of this paper is organized as follows. The theoretical framework for the Ξc−Ξc′subscriptΞ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′Xic-Xicprime mixing is collected in Sec. II. We present our lattice simulations of the two-point correlation functions in Sec. III and a determination of the mixing angle from a direct fit in Sec. IV. Sec. V gives an analysis of the mixing angle through solving the generalized eigenvalue problem. The conclusion of this work is given in Sec. VI.

In heavy quark limit, heavy baryons with one charm quark can be well classified according to the angular momentum J𝐽J of light quark system. If the spin wave function of the light quark system is antisymmetric, i. e. Jq​q=0subscript𝐽𝑞𝑞0Jqq=0, the Fermi statistics and antisymmetric feature in color space require that the flavor wave function is also antisymmetric in S​U​F𝑆𝑈subscript3𝐹SUF. This corresponds the decomposition (3×3)antisymmetric=3¯subscript33antisymmetric¯3(3times 3)antisymmetric=bar3. As a result, one can obtain the baryonic currents of S​U​F𝑆𝑈subscript3𝐹SUF eigenstate ascitebib.bib15 O3¯=ϵa​b​c​(ℓT​a​C​γ5​sb)​P+​cc,superscript𝑂¯3superscriptitalic-ϵ𝑎𝑏𝑐superscriptℓ𝑇𝑎𝐶subscript𝛾5superscript𝑠𝑏subscript𝑃superscript𝑐𝑐displaystyle Obar3=epsilonabcleft(ellTaCgamma5sbright)P+tildecc, O6=ϵa​b​c​(ℓT​a​C​γ→​sb)⋅γ→​γ5​P+​cc,superscript𝑂6⋅superscriptitalic-ϵ𝑎𝑏𝑐superscriptℓ𝑇𝑎𝐶→𝛾superscript𝑠𝑏→𝛾subscript𝛾5subscript𝑃superscript𝑐𝑐displaystyle O6=epsilonabcleft(ellTaCvecgammasbright)cdotvecgammagamma5P+tildecc, with ℓ=(u,d)ℓ𝑢𝑑ell=(u,d).
