The operators to are a generalization of the operators to from the case with mass-degenerate u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks to the unequal-mass case of u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks. Specifically, the operators , and are the counterparts of the operators , and . The main difference is due to the isospin symmetry of the u𝑢u and the d𝑑d quark, where for I=0𝐼0I=0 only the antisymmetric combination u​d−d​u𝑢𝑑𝑑𝑢ud-du is allowed. In contrast, for the u𝑢u and the s𝑠s quark there is no such restriction, leading to two possibilities when combining a pseudoscalar and a vector meson: the operators and correspond to operator and the operators and correspond to operator . In terms of meson pairs, the two B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast operators in the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud case correspond to the two B​Bs∗𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗BBsast and the two B∗​Bssuperscript𝐵∗subscript𝐵𝑠BastBs operators in the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus case.To enhance the overlaps with the low-lying states of interest, we apply standard smearing techniques in the interpolating operators defined in and . The u𝑢u, d𝑑d, s𝑠s, and b𝑏b quark fields are Gaussian smeared (see, e. g. , Eq. in Ref. citebib.bib40) with parameters listed in Table. Here, σGausssubscript𝜎GausssigmatextGauss represents the Gaussian width of the smeared quark field in units of the lattice spacing. For the light and strange quarks, these values were chosen such that the smearing width in physical units, σGauss​asubscript𝜎Gauss𝑎sigmatextGaussa, is essentially independent of the lattice spacing (σGauss​a≈0.55​fmsubscript𝜎Gauss𝑎0.55fmsigmatextGaussaapprox 0.55,textfm for the u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks, σGauss​a≈0.45​fmsubscript𝜎Gauss𝑎0.45fmsigmatextGaussaapprox 0.45,textfm for the s𝑠s quark). The gauge links needed for Gaussian smearing are APE smeared (see, e. g. , Eq. in Ref. citebib.bib67) with parameters NAPE=50subscript𝑁APE50NtextrmAPE=50 and αAPE=0.5subscript𝛼APE0.5alphatextrmAPE=0.5.As discussed in the introduction, a major technical advance of this work compared to previous lattice QCD investigations of b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquarks is that we use the two types of operators, local operators and scattering operators, both at the source and at the sink of the corresponding correlation functions. Thus, we compute square correlation matrices Cj​k​(t)=⟨𝒪j​(t)​𝒪k†​⟩subscript𝐶𝑗𝑘𝑡delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝒪𝑗𝑡superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑘†0Cjk(t)=BigOj(t)OkdaggerBig with operators to for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system and to for the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus system, i. e. , 5×5555times 5 or 7×7777times 7 correlation matrices, respectively. This is a major improvement compared to our previous papers citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, where scattering operators were only used at the sink, and to similar work from other groups citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib41, bib.bib43, where only local operators were employed. We consider the use of scattering operators to be important, even though here we are mainly interested in the ground states of the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus sectors, which correspond to bound states in the infinite-volume limit. The reason why scattering operators might be helpful is that the ground state and the low-lying scattering states in the finite volume are expected to have close-by energies and also similar structures. The latter is also reflected in the operator definitions. For example, 𝒪[B​B∗]​subscript𝒪delimited-𝐵superscript𝐵∗0O[BBast] is contained in 𝒪B​​B∗​subscript𝒪𝐵0superscript𝐵∗0OBBast (considering only terms with x=yxyx=y on the right-hand side of Eq. corresponds to the right-hand side of Eq. ). When extracting energy levels without using both local and scattering operators one might obtain an incorrect estimate of the lowest energy somewhere between the true ground state and one of the low-lying scattering states, resulting in underestimated tetraquark binding energies. In our previous works citebib.bib40, bib.bib42 we presented numerical evidence that the ground-state energies extracted from multi-exponential matrix fits are too high when using only local operators (see, in particular, Fig.3 in Ref. citebib.bib42). More importantly, if one is interested in reliably determining the excited-state finite-volume energy levels, the inclusion of scattering operators at both source and sink becomes essential. The excited-state energy levels can be used to determine the energy dependence of the meson-meson scattering amplitudes using Lüscher’s method citebib.bib52, bib.bib53 and rigorously search for shallow bound states or resonances, as we have done recently for the b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud systems with I​(JP)=0​(0+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript0I(JP)=0(0+) and I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) citebib.bib49. Another interesting system to which these techniques could be applied is b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud with I​(JP)=0​(1−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1-), as discussed in Section.We computed the correlation matrix elements with local operators at the source using Gaussian smeared point-to-all propagators, and the correlation matrix elements with scattering operators at the source using Gaussian smeared stochastic timeslice-to-all propagators (see e. g. Ref. citebib.bib58 for a comprehensive discussion of these techniques in the context of four-quark states). For the latter, we use spin and color dilution and employ the one-end-trick to reduce the statistical noise. Because the correlation matrix is symmetric, the matrix elements that combine a local and a scattering operator can be computed either way, with point-to-all propagators (placing the local operator at the source) or stochastic timeslice-to-all propagators (placing the scattering operator at the source). We did both and found statistical uncertainties of similar magnitude. To increase the statistical precision we averaged both results on each gauge-link configuration. To generate the necessary sources for the point-to-all-propagators, we selected 30 points randomly on each gauge link configuration (an exception is ensemble a15m310 with a rather small number of lattice sites, where we selected only 10 points). Moreover, for the stochastic timeslice-to-all propagators we use 4 equally separated timeslices with a randomly chosen global offset in t𝑡t direction (an exception is ensemble a15m310, where we use only 1 timeslice). On each timeslice we generate 5 independent random ℤ2×ℤ2subscriptℤ2subscriptℤ2Z2timesZ2 sources. When using stochastic timeslice-to-all propagators, i. e. two times the one-end-trick, it is essential that the two stochastic sources are independent. Since we have used 5 sources per timeslice, there are 5×4=2054205times 4=20 possible combinations. We consider and average over all these combinations to further reduce the statistical noise. To determine statistical uncertainties, we use the jackknife method throughout this work.

To determine the binding energies of the QCD-stable b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquarks, we need to compare the ground-state energies of these four-quark systems to the respective lowest meson-meson thresholds. To this end, we also computed the energies of the pseudoscalar and vector B𝐵B and Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs mesons using exactly the same lattice parameters. The corresponding interpolating operators are 𝒪B​(p)=∑xb¯​(x)​γ5​u​(x)​ei​x⋅p,subscript𝒪𝐵psubscriptx¯𝑏xsubscript𝛾5𝑢xsuperscripte⋅𝑖xpdisplaystyleOB(p)=sumxbarb(x)gamma5u(x)textrmeixcdotp, 𝒪B∗​(p)=∑xb¯​(x)​γj​u​(x)​ei​x⋅p,subscript𝒪superscript𝐵∗psubscriptx¯𝑏xsubscript𝛾𝑗𝑢xsuperscripte⋅𝑖xpdisplaystyleOBast(p)=sumxbarb(x)gammaju(x)textrmeixcdotp, 𝒪Bs​(p)=∑xb¯​(x)​γ5​s​(x)​ei​x⋅p,subscript𝒪subscript𝐵𝑠psubscriptx¯𝑏xsubscript𝛾5𝑠xsuperscripte⋅𝑖xpdisplaystyleOBs(p)=sumxbarb(x)gamma5s(x)textrmeixcdotp, 𝒪Bs∗​(p)=∑xb¯​(x)​γj​s​(x)​ei​x⋅p. subscript𝒪superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗psubscriptx¯𝑏xsubscript𝛾𝑗𝑠xsuperscripte⋅𝑖xpdisplaystyleOBsast(p)=sumxbarb(x)gammajs(x)textrmeixcdotp. We allow for non-vanishing momenta p=2​π​𝐧/Lp2𝜋𝐧𝐿p=2pin/L with L=Ns​a𝐿subscript𝑁𝑠𝑎L=Nsa, 𝐧∈ℤ3𝐧superscriptℤ3ninZ3, which are necessary to compute the kinetic masses of the B𝐵B and the Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs mesons. These kinetic masses are required for scattering analyses, which we carried out to estimate finite-volume effects on binding energies as outlined in . For the computation of the correlation functions we use the same Gaussian smeared point-to-all propagators discussed in .
content_sections.IV Energies and kinetic masses of pseudo: We determined the ground-state energies of pseudoscalar and vector B𝐵B and Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs mesons via correlated χ2superscript𝜒2chi2-minimizing single-exponential fits to the correlation functions of the interpolating operators to with p=0p0p=0. We consider a number of fit ranges tmin≤t≤tmaxsubscript𝑡min𝑡subscript𝑡maxttextminleq tleq ttextmax by varying 7≤tmin/a≤97subscript𝑡min𝑎97leq ttextmin/aleq 9 and 17≤tmax/a≤2017subscript𝑡max𝑎2017leq ttextmax/aleq 20. The lower bound tmin=7​asubscript𝑡min7𝑎ttextmin=7a corresponds to the temporal separation for which the corresponding effective energies start to be consistent with a plateau. We generate final results by weighted averaging following a method used by the FLAG Collaboration citebib.bib68 (a brief summary of the method can also be found in AppendixB of Ref. citebib.bib42). The results for the B𝐵B, B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast, Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs and Bs∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗Bsast meson energy levels for each of our seven ensembles are listed in . To exemplify the quality of our numerical data, we show in Fig. plots of effective energies a​Eeff​(t)=ln⁡(C​(t)/C​(t+a))𝑎subscript𝐸eff𝑡𝐶𝑡𝐶𝑡𝑎aEtexteff(t)=ln(C(t)/C(t+a)) for ensemble a12m220L together with the corresponding final energies from Table. Note that, due to the use of NRQCD, the energies listed in Table and elsewhere contain overall negative shifts proportional to nbsubscript𝑛𝑏nb, the number of b𝑏b quarks present in the corresponding states. At tree-level, this shift amounts to −nb​mbsubscript𝑛𝑏subscript𝑚𝑏-nbmb, where mbsubscript𝑚𝑏mb is the b𝑏b-quark mass. When considering energy differences between four-quark states and meson-meson thresholds with the same number of b𝑏b quarks, as done, for example, in , these energy shifts cancel. Further quantities that can directly be compared to their experimental counterparts are the energy differences between the vector and pseudoscalar B𝐵B and Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs meson energies. Experimentally, these differences are EB∗−EB=45.21​​MeVsubscript𝐸superscript𝐵∗subscript𝐸𝐵45.2121MeVEBast-EB=45.21,textMeV and EBs∗−EBs=48.5−1.5+1.8​MeVsubscript𝐸superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗subscript𝐸subscript𝐵𝑠subscriptsuperscript48.51.81.5MeVEBsast-EBs=48.5+1.8-1.5,textMeV citebib.bib69. Our corresponding lattice results are listed in and are found to be approximately 10% smaller. This discrepancy is likely due to the missing one-loop corrections to the matching coefficient c4subscript𝑐4c4 of the term −g/(2​mb)​ψ†​𝝈⋅𝐁​ψ⋅𝑔2subscript𝑚𝑏superscript𝜓†𝝈𝐁𝜓-g/(2mb),psidaggersigmacdotBpsi in the NRQCD action. We also determined the kinetic meson masses mM,kinsubscript𝑚𝑀kinmM,,rm kin for M∈B,B∗,Bs,Bs∗𝑀𝐵superscript𝐵∗subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗MinB,Bast,Bs,Bsast, in which the energy shifts are not present, and which reflect the energy-momentum dispersion relation. The kinetic meson masses are important for scattering analyses, as discussed in . To determine them, we additionally computed the meson energies for several non-vanishing momenta, using the interpolating operators to with p=2​π​𝐧/Lp2𝜋𝐧𝐿p=2pin/L with 𝐧∈(1,0,0),(1,1,0),(1,1,1),(2,0,0)𝐧100110111200nin(1,0,0),(1,1,0),(1,1,1),(2,0,0). Then we extracted mM,kinsubscript𝑚𝑀kinmM,,rm kin by fitting the right-hand side of EM​(p)−EM​=mM,kin2+p2−mM,kinsubscript𝐸𝑀psubscript𝐸𝑀0superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑀kin2superscriptp2subscript𝑚𝑀kindisplaystyle EM(p)-EM=sqrtmM,,rm kin2+p2-mM,,rm kin for each M∈B,B∗,Bs,Bs∗𝑀𝐵superscript𝐵∗subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗MinB,Bast,Bs,Bsast and each ensemble to the corresponding lattice results for the four energy differences EM​(p)−EM​subscript𝐸𝑀psubscript𝐸𝑀0EM(p)-EM. An exception are the three ensembles a12m220S, a12m220 and a12m220L, which only differ in the volume, and where we determined a common kinetic mass by performing a single fit for each meson to the corresponding twelve energy differences. The results for the kinetic masses are collected in Table. Moreover, in Fig. we present an example plot demonstrating that the energy-momentum relation is fully consistent with the data.
content_sections.V Energies of antiheavy-antiheavy-light-: To determine the low-lying energy levels for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus four-quark systems from the 5×5555times 5 and 7×7777times 7 correlation matrices Cj​k​(t)subscript𝐶𝑗𝑘𝑡Cjk(t) discussed in Section, we solve the standard generalized eigenvalue problems (GEVPs) ∑kCj​k​(t)​vk,n​(t,t0)=λn​(t,t0)​∑kCj​k​(t0)​vk,n​(t,t0),subscript𝑘subscript𝐶𝑗𝑘𝑡subscript𝑣𝑘𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝑘subscript𝐶𝑗𝑘subscript𝑡0subscript𝑣𝑘𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0displaystylesumkCjk(t)vk,n(t,t0)=lambdan(t,t0)sumkCjk(t0)vk,n(t,t0), where the indices j𝑗j and k𝑘k are operator indices and the indices n𝑛n enumerate the resulting eigenvalues and eigenvectors and are, thus, related to the indices of the low-lying energy eigenstates. The choice of t0subscript𝑡0t0 is made individually for each system and each ensemble. Our strategy was to choose the largest value of t0subscript𝑡0t0 for which statistical uncertainties on the resulting eigenvalues and eigenvectors did not significantly increase (see Table). The energy levels Ensubscript𝐸𝑛En are then determined by carrying out a correlated χ2superscript𝜒2chi2-minimizing fit of an exponential function to each resulting eigenvalue λn​(t,t0)subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0lambdan(t,t0). Below, we also discuss the components vj,n​(t,t0)subscript𝑣𝑗𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0vj,n(t,t0), where we always normalize the eigenvectors according to ∑j|vj,n​(t,t0)|2=1subscript𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡021sumj|vj,n(t,t0)|2=1.

As done in our determination of B𝐵B and Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs meson energies in , we again consider multiple fit ranges tmin≤t≤tmaxsubscript𝑡min𝑡subscript𝑡maxttextminleq tleq ttextmax by varying tminsubscript𝑡minttextmin and tmaxsubscript𝑡maxttextmax. For ensemble a12m220L, the fits are summarized in a graphical way in Fig. , including the corresponding fit ranges and resulting energy levels. Analogous plots for the other ensembles are collected in Fig. in the appendix. As before, the final results are then generated by weighted averaging over all fits according to the FLAG method and are shown in Table and Fig. , where Δ​En=En−EB​−EB∗​Δsubscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐸𝐵0subscript𝐸superscript𝐵∗0Delta En=En-EB-EBast. The ground-state energy is found to be significantly below the B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast threshold on all ensembles, Δ​E0≈(−105​…​-70)Δsubscript𝐸0105…-70Delta E0approx(-105ldotsmbox-70) MeV. An energy level in that region is expected from and consistent with previous lattice QCD investigations of this b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib43, bib.bib44. It signals the existence of a QCD-stable b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark. The energies of the first and second excitations are within statistical uncertainties consistent with the B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast and B∗​B∗superscript𝐵∗superscript𝐵∗BastBast thresholds. This indicates that, with our basis of interpolating operators, we are able to not only resolve the tetraquark state, but also these two low-lying scattering states. As expected, with the operator basis used here [Eqs. to ], we are not able to resolve scattering states with non-vanishing relative momenta. In particular, this is the case for B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast states in which the two mesons have opposite minimal non-vanishing momenta pmin=(2​π)/Lsubscript𝑝min2𝜋𝐿ptextmin=(2pi)/L, where L=Ns​a𝐿subscript𝑁𝑠𝑎L=Nsa is the ensemble-dependent spatial extent of the lattice. The estimated energy of such states with respect to the B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast threshold is Δ​EB​B∗,p=pmin=(mB2+pmin2)1/2+(mB∗2+pmin2)1/2−mB−mB∗,Δsubscript𝐸𝐵superscript𝐵∗𝑝subscript𝑝minsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑚𝐵2superscriptsubscript𝑝min212superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑚superscript𝐵∗2superscriptsubscript𝑝min212subscript𝑚𝐵subscript𝑚superscript𝐵∗displaystyleDelta EBBast,p=ptextmin=Big(mB2+ptextmin2Big)1/2+Big(mBast2+ptextmin2Big)1/2-mB-mBast, where mBsubscript𝑚𝐵mB and mB∗subscript𝑚superscript𝐵∗mBast are the full meson masses, which can be taken to be the kinetic masses from Table. For example, for ensembles a12m220 and a12m220L, Δ​EB​B∗,p=pmin≈20​MeVΔsubscript𝐸𝐵superscript𝐵∗𝑝subscript𝑝min20MeVDelta EBBast,p=ptextminapprox 20,textMeV and Δ​EB​B∗,p=pmin≈13​MeVΔsubscript𝐸𝐵superscript𝐵∗𝑝subscript𝑝min13MeVDelta EBBast,p=ptextminapprox 13,textMeV, respectively. With exception of ensemble a15m310, where Δ​EB​B∗,p=pmin≈50​MeVΔsubscript𝐸𝐵superscript𝐵∗𝑝subscript𝑝min50MeVDelta EBBast,p=ptextminapprox 50,textMeV, we have Δ​EB​B∗,p=pmin<mB∗−mB≈45​MeVΔsubscript𝐸𝐵superscript𝐵∗𝑝subscript𝑝minsubscript𝑚superscript𝐵∗subscript𝑚𝐵45MeVDelta EBBast,p=ptextmin<mBast-mBapprox 45,textMeV. Thus, the true second excited state is expected significantly below the B∗​B∗superscript𝐵∗superscript𝐵∗BastBast threshold, which is not reflected by the numerically obtained energy differences Δ​E2Δsubscript𝐸2Delta E2 (see Table). The overlaps of our operators with this state are likely very small. To resolve this state and higher states of this type, we would need to include scattering operators with non-vanishing relative momenta. To illustrate the importance of scattering operators, we compare in Fig. effective energies obtained by solving GEVPs for a 3×3333times 3 correlation matrix with only local operators [Eqs. to ] (left plot) and the full 5×5555times 5 correlation matrix, which also contains the scattering operators [Eqs. and ] (right plot). While the effective masses Eeff,1​(t)subscript𝐸eff1𝑡Etexteff,1(t) and Eeff,2​(t)subscript𝐸eff2𝑡Etexteff,2(t), defined as Eeff,n​(t)=(1/a)​ln⁡(λn​(t,t0)/λn​(t+a,t0))subscript𝐸eff𝑛𝑡1𝑎subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡𝑎subscript𝑡0Etexteff,n(t)=(1/a)ln(lambdan(t,t0)/lambdan(t+a,t0)), quickly approach the B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast and B∗​B∗superscript𝐵∗superscript𝐵∗BastBast thresholds for the full 5×5555times 5 matrix, this is not the case for the 3×3333times 3 matrix. Thus, it is practically impossible to extract physically meaningful energy levels Ensubscript𝐸𝑛En for n≥1𝑛1ngeq 1 from the 3×3333times 3 matrix. The conclusion is that it is imperative to include scattering operators if energy levels of scattering states are needed, e. g. , for a finite-volume scattering analysis as discussed in . However, the results for the effective energy Eeff,0​(t)subscript𝐸eff0𝑡Etexteff,0(t) are essentially the same with and without scattering operators. Thus, if one is only interested in determining the mass of the QCD-stable b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark in a finite volume, local operators may be sufficient. This finding is surprising, given our experience in Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, where we compared multi-exponential matrix fits with and without scattering operators at the sink and found some impact on the fit results for E0subscript𝐸0E0. In Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, we were unable to use the GEVP for the non-square correlation matrices with scattering operators included at the sink only. The eigenvectors vj,n​(t,t0)subscript𝑣𝑗𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0vj,n(t,t0) obtained by solving GEVPs are fairly independent of t𝑡t for larger t𝑡t and provide information about the composition of the low-lying energy eigenstates associated with the extracted energy levels Ensubscript𝐸𝑛En. In Fig. we show the signed squared eigenvector components, sign​(vj,n)​|vj,n|2signsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑛2textsign(tildevj,n)|tildevj,n|2, for the full 5×5555times 5 correlation matrix from ensemble a12m220L, where the quantities vj,nsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑛tildevj,n are obtained by fitting constants to vj,n​(t,t0)subscript𝑣𝑗𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0vj,n(t,t0) in the range tmin≤t≤tmaxsubscript𝑡min𝑡subscript𝑡maxttextminleq tleq ttextmax (we use multiple fits with different ranges, 6≤tmin/a≤96subscript𝑡min𝑎96leq ttextmin/aleq 9 and 12≤tmax/a≤1412subscript𝑡max𝑎1412leq ttextmax/aleq 14 in this case, and present the FLAG-method averages over these fits). When ignoring the signs, these quantities add up to 1 and can be interpreted as the relative importance of each interpolating operator 𝒪jsubscript𝒪𝑗Oj when approximating the energy eigenstate |n⟩ket𝑛|n as a sum over trial states 𝒪j​|n⟩subscript𝒪𝑗ket𝑛Oj|n 2Such an interpretation requires a similar normalization of all trial states 𝒪j​|n⟩subscript𝒪𝑗ket𝑛Oj|n. One possibility to implement this is to include appropriate volume factors, as present in the definitions of our interpolating operators. A common alternative is to replace Cj​k​(t)subscript𝐶𝑗𝑘𝑡Cjk(t) by Cj​k​(t)/Cj​j​(t=a)​Ck​k​(t=a)subscript𝐶𝑗𝑘𝑡subscript𝐶𝑗𝑗𝑡𝑎subscript𝐶𝑘𝑘𝑡𝑎Cjk(t)/sqrtCjj(t=a)Ckk(t=a), which corresponds to a normalization of the trial states at t=a𝑡𝑎t=a, i. e. ⟨Ω|𝒪j​(t=a)​𝒪k†​(t=0)|Ω⟩=1quantum-operator-productΩsubscript𝒪𝑗𝑡𝑎superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑘†𝑡0Ω1Omega|Oj(t=a)Okdagger(t=0)|Omega=1. Throughout this paper we follow this latter strategy. Note that the eigenvalues λn​(t,t0)subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0lambdan(t,t0) and, consequently, the extracted energy levels Ensubscript𝐸𝑛En are unaffected by such a normalization. . The signs are of particular interest in the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus case (see ), where they expose the approximate light flavor symmetry of u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks. The results support our above conclusions based on the extracted energy levels. The ground state |0⟩ket0|0, which is a QCD-stable tetraquark, is excited almost exclusively by the three local operators [Eqs. to ], while the scattering operators [Eqs. and ] are essentially irrelevant. The relative contributions of the local operators to the QCD-stable tetraquark are, moreover, consistent with previous lattice QCD investigations using static potentials and the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. In particular, the B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast component is slightly larger than, but of similar magnitude as the B∗​B∗superscript𝐵∗superscript𝐵∗BastBast component, which is in agreement with Fig.3 of Ref. citebib.bib37, and the meson-meson components are of similar importance as the diquark-antidiquark component, which is in line with the main result from Ref. citebib.bib66. The first and second excitation |1⟩ket1|1 and |2⟩ket2|2, on the other hand, are clearly B​B∗𝐵superscript𝐵∗BBast and B∗​B∗superscript𝐵∗superscript𝐵∗BastBast scattering states, as already indicated by the consistency of their energy levels with the corresponding thresholds.

For the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus system we proceed in the same way as for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system in . For ensemble a12m220L, the fits are summarized in Fig. ; the plots for the other ensembles are collected in Fig. in the appendix. The final results for Δ​En=En−EB​−EBs∗​Δsubscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐸𝐵0subscript𝐸superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗0Delta En=En-EB-EBsast from the FLAG-method weighted averages are shown in Table and Fig. . The ground-state energy is found to be significantly below the B​Bs∗𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗BBsast threshold, Δ​E0≈(−35​…−30)​MeVΔsubscript𝐸035…30MeVDelta E0approx(-35ldots-30)textMeV, indicating the existence of a QCD-stable b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark, consistent with previous lattice-QCD investigations of this system citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib42, bib.bib43. We obtain precise results also for three additional states with energies consistent with the B​Bs∗𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗BBsast, Bs​B∗subscript𝐵𝑠superscript𝐵∗BsBast and B∗​Bs∗superscript𝐵∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗BastBsast thresholds. However, as already noted for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud case, we are unable to resolve scattering states with non-vanishing back-to-back momenta; for example, on ensembles a12m220 and a12m220L one has Δ​EB​Bs∗,p=pmin≈20​MeVΔsubscript𝐸𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗𝑝subscript𝑝min20MeVDelta EBBsast,p=ptextminapprox 20,textMeV and Δ​EB​Bs∗,p=pmin≈13​MeVΔsubscript𝐸𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗𝑝subscript𝑝min13MeVDelta EBBsast,p=ptextminapprox 13,textMeV, respectively. Scattering operators with non-vanishing back-to-back momenta would be needed to resolve these states. Again, we illustrate the importance of the scattering operators by comparing in Fig. effective energies obtained by solving GEVPs for a 4×4444times 4 correlation matrix with only local operators [Eqs. to ] (left plot) and the full 7×7777times 7 correlation matrix, which also contains the scattering operators [Eqs. to ] (right plot). The overall picture and conclusions are the same as for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system. To determine the mass of the QCD-stable b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark, local operators appear to be sufficient, but it is imperative to include scattering operators if energy levels of scattering states are needed. The fact that the results for Eeff,0​(t)subscript𝐸eff0𝑡Etexteff,0(t) are essentially the same with and without scattering operators is even more surprising here than for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system, given the weaker binding. In Fig. we show the signed squared eigenvector components sign​(vj,n)​|vj,n|2signsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑛2textsign(tildevj,n)|tildevj,n|2 for the full 7×7777times 7 correlation matrix from ensemble a12m220L (here, we use the FLAG-method averages over fits with 5≤tmin/a≤95subscript𝑡min𝑎95leq ttextmin/aleq 9 and 11≤tmax/a≤1411subscript𝑡max𝑎1411leq ttextmax/aleq 14). The eigenvector components support and complement our above conclusions based on the extracted energy levels, and exhibit the approximate SU flavor symmetry. The ground state |0⟩ket0|0, which is a QCD-stable tetraquark, is excited mostly by the four local operators. It is u​s𝑢𝑠us flavor antisymmetric, which shows that the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark has a flavor structure analogous the I=0𝐼0I=0 b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark discussed in the previous section. The first and second excitations are mixtures of B​Bs∗𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗BBsast and Bs​B∗subscript𝐵𝑠superscript𝐵∗BsBast scattering states, where |1⟩ket1|1 is mostly u​s𝑢𝑠us flavor symmetric, i. e. similar to I=1𝐼1I=1, while |2⟩ket2|2 is mostly u​s𝑢𝑠us flavor antisymmetric, i. e. similar to I=0𝐼0I=0. The third excitation |3⟩ket3|3 is clearly a B∗​Bs∗superscript𝐵∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗BastBsast scattering state, as already suggested by its energy.
content_sections.VI Finite-volume effects: Some of the previous lattice-QCD studies of b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and c¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑐¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barcbarcud systems citebib.bib40, bib.bib70, bib.bib71 have employed Lüscher’s method citebib.bib52, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, bib.bib53 to relate the finite-volume energy levels with infinite-volume B𝐵B-B∗superscript𝐵B* and D𝐷D-D∗superscript𝐷D* scattering amplitudes. The masses of the infinite-volume bound states or virtual bound states were then obtained as the pole locations in these amplitudes. Such an analysis has not yet been performed for the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus system, which is more challenging due to the coupling of the Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs-B∗superscript𝐵B* and B𝐵B-Bs∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠Bs* channels with nearly identical thresholds citebib.bib42. The improvements made in the present work in extracting the finite-volume spectra (compared to Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42) in principle enable more advanced scattering-amplitude analyses, including an analysis of coupled-channel Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs-B∗superscript𝐵B*, B𝐵B-Bs∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠Bs* scattering. The studies presented in Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib70, bib.bib71 used Lüscher’s method both above and below threshold, and used effective-range expansions (EREs) to parametrize the energy dependence of the scattering amplitude. It was recently pointed out that the D𝐷D-D∗superscript𝐷D* scattering amplitude has a left-hand cut starting close to threshold due to pion exchange citebib.bib80, which determines the radius of convergence of the ERE to be smaller than the regions studied in the aforementioned references. The same problem is also present in B𝐵B-B∗superscript𝐵B* scattering and, due to kaon exchange, in Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs-B∗superscript𝐵B*, B𝐵B-Bs∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠Bs* scattering. Moreover, the two-body Lüscher quantization condition itself becomes invalid on the left-hand cut citebib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87. For B𝐵B-B∗superscript𝐵B* scattering, the left-hand cut due to single-pion exchange starts at the invariant-mass-squared scut=sth−mπ2+(mB∗−mB)2,subscript𝑠cutsubscript𝑠thsuperscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝑚superscript𝐵subscript𝑚𝐵2srm cut=srm th-mpi2+(mB*-mB)2, where sth=(mB∗+mB)2subscript𝑠thsuperscriptsubscript𝑚superscript𝐵subscript𝑚𝐵2srm th=(mB*+mB)2. For physical pion mass, one finds scut−sth≈−1​MeVsubscript𝑠cutsubscript𝑠th1MeVsqrtsrm cut-sqrtsrm thapprox-1>rm MeV, while at mπ=300​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜋300MeVmpi=300>rm MeV, scut−sth≈−4​MeVsubscript𝑠cutsubscript𝑠th4MeVsqrtsrm cut-sqrtsrm thapprox-4>rm MeV. Similarly, for Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs-B∗superscript𝐵B*, B𝐵B-Bs∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠Bs* scattering at physical kaon mass, the cut due to single-kaon exchange starts at scut−sth≈−11​MeVsubscript𝑠cutsubscript𝑠th11MeVsqrtsrm cut-sqrtsrm thapprox-11>rm MeV. The finite-volume ground-state energies we obtained in this work (Section) and in Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42 for b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus are well below these values. New approaches to describing the finite-volume energy levels in the region effected by the left-hand cut were recently proposed in Refs. citebib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87; these involve modifications to the two-body Lüscher formalism, the use of the three-body Lüscher formalism, or chiral effective theory. However, the implementation of these approaches is beyond the scope of the present work. In the remainder of this paper, we will focus on the ground-state energies and assume that finite-volume effects are negligible compared to our statistical uncertainties. Before we became aware of the left-hand cut problem, we did in fact perform extractions of S𝑆S-wave scattering amplitudes from the energy levels presented here using the standard two-body Lüscher method; we can use these results to get at least some idea of the possible size of finite-volume effects. For the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system, we used the lowest two energy levels on each ensemble and fitted a two-parameter model of the K𝐾K matrix citebib.bib88, similar to the effective-range expansion used in citebib.bib40. From these fits, we found that the bound-state pole locations differ from the finite-volume ground-state energies by ≲0.1less-than-or-similar-toabsent0.1lesssim 0.1 MeV, consistent with the expectation from Ref. citebib.bib40. For b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus, we attempted coupled-channel fits to the lowest three energy levels with various parametrizations of the 2×2222times 2 K𝐾K matrix, including fits that combine the data from the a12m220S, a12m220, and a12m220L ensembles that differ only in the volume. However, most of these fits did not converge properly, and the only model that gave stable results was a diagonal form of the K𝐾K matrix with two s𝑠s-independent parameters on the diagonal. For those ensembles for which we achieved a reasonable χ2/d. o. fformulae-sequencesuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/rm d. o. f, this model predicted shifts between the finite-volume ground-state energies and the bound-state pole locations of ≲1less-than-or-similar-toabsent1lesssim 1 MeV. Another way of estimating the finite-volume effects is directly comparing the finite-volume energies of the a12m220S, a12m220, and a12m220L ensembles listed in Table, which suggests that the effects for b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus are not larger than about 5 MeV for the smallest volume.
content_sections.VII Chiral and continuum extrapolations: To obtain estimates of the tetraquark binding energies at the physical point, we consider two different fits to the ground-state energies from the different ensembles: (i) neglecting lattice-spacing dependence but allowing for a linear dependence on mπ2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2mpi2, as in Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, Δ​E0​(mπ)=Δ​E0​(mπ,phys)+c​(mπ2−mπ,phys2),Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋phys𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋phys2displaystyleDelta E0(mpi)=Delta E0(mpi,textphys)+c(mpi2-mpi,textphys2), and (ii), allowing for linear dependencies on both mπ2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2mpi2 and a2superscript𝑎2a2, Δ​E0​(mπ,a)=Δ​E0​(mπ,phys,0)+c​(mπ2−mπ,phys2)+d​a2. Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋𝑎Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋phys0𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋phys2𝑑superscript𝑎2displaystyleDelta E0(mpi,a)=Delta E0(mpi,textphys,0)+c(mpi2-mpi,textphys2)+d,a2. Here, we use mπ,phys=135​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜋phys135MeVmpi,textphys=135,textrmMeV for the physical pion mass in the isospin-symmetric limit. These models are expected to be approximate only. For the actions used here, gluon and sea-quark discretization errors are expected to start at O​(αs2​a2)𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2superscript𝑎2O(alphas2a2) and O​(αs​a2)𝑂subscript𝛼𝑠superscript𝑎2O(alphasa2), respectively, but the Wilson-clover valence action is only tree-level-O​(a)𝑂𝑎O(a) improved and thus will have some remaining O​(αs​a)𝑂subscript𝛼𝑠𝑎O(alphasa) errors. With the particular level of Symanzik improvement used here, the NRQCD action has both O​(αs2​a)𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2𝑎O(alphas2a) and O​(αs2​a2)𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2superscript𝑎2O(alphas2a2) errors, plus other systematic errors from missing higher-order relativistic and radiative corrections that we will estimate separately below. Our fits using Eqs. or are illustrated in Figs. and , and the results for the fit parameters are given in Table . The coefficients d𝑑d that describe the lattice-spacing dependence turn out to be consistent with zero. For the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system, there is a slight indication that the binding energy increases in magnitude as the pion mass is lowered, which becomes more statistically significant when not including the a2superscript𝑎2a2 term. On the other hand, the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus binding energy is essentially independent of the pion mass in the range considered here. Some of the fits have χ2/d. o. f. >1superscript𝜒2d. o. f.1chi2/textrmd. o. f. >1 due to a slight tension between data points with similar lattice parameters; in these cases, we scaled the uncertainties of the fit results by χ2/d. o. f. superscript𝜒2d. o. f. sqrtchi2/textrmd. o. f. . We find that the results for Δ​E0​(mπ,phys)Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋physDelta E0(mpi,textphys) and Δ​E0​(mπ,phys,0)Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋phys0Delta E0(mpi,textphys,0) are compatible within statistical uncertainties. We take the latter as our final estimates of the tetraquark binding energies. The extrapolations do not remove the systematic errors from missing higher-order relativistic and radiative corrections in the lattice NRQCD action. To estimate these sources of systematic uncertainty, we follow the prescription of Ref. citebib.bib40 with adjustments for the different choices of NRQCD matching coefficients used here. The values of Δ​E0Δsubscript𝐸0Delta E0 depend both on the heavy-light meson energies and on the energies of the four-quark systems. For the heavy-light mesons, the most significant systematic NRQCD uncertainties are expected to be as follows: • Four-quark operators are missing in the action. They appear only at order αs2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2alphas2 in the QCD matching, but according to Ref. citebib.bib89 their effect is of order 3​MeV3MeV3,rm MeV. • One-loop corrections to the matching coefficient c4subscript𝑐4c4 of the operator −g/(2​mb)​ψ†​𝝈⋅𝐁​ψ⋅𝑔2subscript𝑚𝑏superscript𝜓†𝝈𝐁𝜓-g/(2mb),psidaggersigmacdotBpsi are not included here. The associated uncertainties can be estimated as αs​ΛQCD2/mb≈6​MeV,subscript𝛼𝑠superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2subscript𝑚𝑏6MeValphasLambdatextrmQCD2/mbapprox 6,rm MeV, where we use αs≈0.3subscript𝛼𝑠0.3alphasapprox 0.3, ΛQCD≈300subscriptΛQCD300LambdatextrmQCDapprox 300 MeV, and mb≈4.5subscript𝑚𝑏4.5mbapprox 4.5 GeV. • We also use only tree-level values for the coefficients c2subscript𝑐2c2 and c3subscript𝑐3c3 of the operators of order (ΛQCD2/mb2)superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑏2(LambdatextrmQCD2/mb2). The uncertainties from missing higher-order contributions to these coefficients are given by αs​ΛQCD3/mb2≈0.4​MeV. subscript𝛼𝑠superscriptsubscriptΛQCD3superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑏20.4MeValphasLambdatextrmQCD3/mb2approx 0.4,rm MeV. As the matching coefficients c1subscript𝑐1c1, c5subscript𝑐5c5, and c6subscript𝑐6c6 include order-αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas corrections, systematic uncertainties arising from the related operators are negligible. For the four-quark system, the power counting is more complicated due to the presence of two bottom quarks. A reasonable estimate for the systematic uncertainties can be obtained by replacing the QCD scale ΛQCDsubscriptΛQCDLambdatextrmQCD by the binding momentum |kBS|subscript𝑘BS|ktextrmBS| (if larger than ΛQCDsubscriptΛQCDLambdatextrmQCD) citebib.bib40. Our results for the binding energies correspond to |kBS|≈730subscript𝑘BS730|ktextrmBS|approx 730 MeV for b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and |kBS|≈400subscript𝑘BS400|ktextrmBS|approx 400 MeV for b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus, which leads to estimated systematic uncertainties of ≈36absent36approx 36 MeV for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud energy and ≈11absent11approx 11 MeV for the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus energy. It is reasonable to expect some partial cancellation of systematic errors between the tetraquark and heavy-light meson energies, so these numbers could be taken as the total systematic uncertainties of Δ​E0Δsubscript𝐸0Delta E0 citebib.bib40. However, note that our previous calculations citebib.bib40, bib.bib42 predicted larger binding momenta of |kBS|≈800subscript𝑘BS800|ktextrmBS|approx 800 MeV for b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and |kBS|≈680subscript𝑘BS680|ktextrmBS|approx 680 MeV for b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus, and the smaller values obtained here may, in principle, be due to the very systematic errors we are trying to estimate. Therefore, to be conservative, we will report asymmetric uncertainties in which we use the larger values of |kBS|subscript𝑘BS|ktextrmBS| from Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42 to obtain the size of the error bars in the direction of increased binding. This prescription then yields our final results Δ​E0​(mπ,phys,0)​[b¯​b¯​u​d]Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋phys0delimited-¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑displaystyleDelta E0(mpi,textphys,0)[barbbarbud] =displaystyle= −100±10−43+36​MeV,plus-or-minus100subscriptsuperscript103643MeVdisplaystyle-100pm 10>+36-43>>rm MeV, Δ​E0​(mπ,phys,0)​[b¯​b¯​u​s]Δsubscript𝐸0subscript𝑚𝜋phys0delimited-¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠displaystyleDelta E0(mpi,textphys,0)[barbbarbus] =displaystyle= −30±3−31+11​MeV. plus-or-minus30subscriptsuperscript31131MeVdisplaystyle-30pm 3>+11-31>>rm MeV.
content_sections.VIII Conclusions: In this work, we have confirmed the existence and computed the binding energies of both the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) and the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark with quantum numbers JP=1+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript1JP=1+ using lattice QCD. We have improved on existing similar lattice-QCD computations citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44 by implementing, for the first time, a combination of local and scattering interpolating operators both at the source and at the sink of symmetric correlation matrices. We have demonstrated that this enables high-precision determinations of the energies of low-lying scattering states, and that it is practically impossible to determine these energies without scattering operators. Reliably extracting the complete low-lying finite-volume spectra is essential when using the Lüscher method to determine energy-dependent scattering amplitudes, as we have done for the b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud systems with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(0+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript0I(JP)=0(0+) and I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) in Ref. citebib.bib49, based on the methods developed here. On the other hand, we found that the inclusion of scattering operators had essentially no impact on the ground-state results obtained from the GEVP for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus systems. This finding was somewhat unexpected, given our experience from Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, in which we compared the results of multi-exponential matrix fits with and without scattering operators at the sink and found some impact on the fit results for E0subscript𝐸0E0. When using the GEVP, local operators alone appear to be sufficient to determine the binding energies of the deeply bound b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquarks, but the inclusion of scattering operators provides reassurance and may be necessary to reliably extract the ground-state energies of other, less deeply bound systems. Using chiral and continuum extrapolations of the results from several ensembles, we obtained binding energies of −100±10−43+36​MeVplus-or-minus100subscriptsuperscript103643MeV-100pm 10>+36-43>>rm MeV for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) and −30±3−31+11​MeVplus-or-minus30subscriptsuperscript31131MeV-30pm 3>+11-31>>rm MeV for the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark with quantum numbers JP=1+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript1JP=1+. These values are reasonably consistent with results of other recent lattice-QCD studies of these systems citebib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, but are in slight tension with earlier works citebib.bib38, bib.bib41. We have updated the summary plots for both tetraquark systems from Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib42 and show them in Fig. . For the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system, also calculations citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib37 based on static potentials from lattice QCD and the Born-Oppenheimer approximation are shown; these gave a somewhat smaller binding energy. The predictions from other approaches such as potential models, effective field theories, and QCD sum rules were taken from Refs. citebib.bib1, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib3, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. The main systematic uncertainties in our present results are associated with the lattice NRQCD action citebib.bib64, in particular with the use of the tree-level value of the coefficient c4subscript𝑐4c4 and the missing four-quark operators. The radiative corrections to c2subscript𝑐2c2 and c4subscript𝑐4c4, and the coefficients of the four-quark operators with four heavy quarks have in fact been computed to one loop in lattice perturbation theory in Refs. citebib.bib90, bib.bib91. The systematic uncertainties could have been reduced by including these corrections, although four-quark operators with both heavy and light quarks would still be missing. A computation of the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark energies using a relativistic action for the b¯¯𝑏barb quarks is also a worthwhile future direction to complement and cross-check the results from this work and from Refs. citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, which all used NRQCD. Finally, we note that the calculations presented here only used scattering interpolating operators with zero relative momenta, leading to missing states in the energy range considered, especially for the larger volumes. A natural next step is to include also scattering operators with non-vanishing relative momenta. Their implementation is straightforward within our computational setup, and we have already included such operators in our recent investigation of the b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud systems citebib.bib49.

sorted_papers.title: Precise Omega baryons from lattice QCD
sorted_papers.abstract:
In this paper we determine the masses of $I(J^P)=0\left(3/2^+\right)$ and
$0\left(3/2^-\right)$ $\Omega$-baryon ground states using lattice QCD. We
utilise Wilson-clover ensembles with $2+1$ dynamical quark flavours generated
by the CLS consortium along a trajectory with a constant trace of the
quark-mass matrix. We show that N$^3$LO $\text{SU}(3)_f$ chiral perturbation
theory expressions describe the ground-state masses with positive-parity well,
and we use them to set the lattice scale. Methodologically, our combination of
gauge-fixed wall sources and the generalized Pencil of Functions allows for
high-precision determinations of the lattice spacing at a relative error of
around $0.3\%$ with controlled excited-state contamination. The fit we perform
allows for the continuum value of $t_0$ to vary, thereby determining this
quantity with a comparable level of precision to that of the lattice scale.
Using the resulting scales our measurement of the negative-parity
$\Omega^{3/2^{-}}$ state is found to be consistent with the recently-discovered
$\Omega(2012)^-$, which can therefore be assigned the quantum numbers
$I(J^P)=0\left(3/2^-\right)$.

qualitative: Lattice QCD two-point correlators are used to extract hadron masses by analyzing the exponential decay of correlation functions in Euclidean time.
Excited-state contamination in two-point correlators can bias mass determinations if not properly accounted for, especially at short time separations.
The generalized Pencil of Functions method is employed to control excited-state contamination by allowing flexible parametrizations of the correlator that include multiple exponential contributions.
Gauge-fixed wall sources are used to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio and improve the precision of two-point correlator measurements.
Single-state fits are insufficient for high-precision results when excited states are not negligible; two-state fits are necessary to reliably isolate the ground-state mass.
The use of dimensionless ratios involving the Ω-baryon mass and t₀ allows for robust scale setting with reduced systematic uncertainties.
The N³LO SU(3)₈ chiral perturbation theory expressions are used to describe the dependence of the Ω-baryon ground-state mass on the quark mass, enabling extrapolation to the physical point.
quantitative: The lattice spacing is determined with a relative error of approximately 0.3%.
The continuum value of t₀ is determined with a precision comparable to that of the lattice scale.
The negative-parity Ω(3/2⁻) state mass is consistent with the experimentally observed Ω(2012)⁻, supporting the assignment of quantum numbers I(Jᴾ) = 0(3/2⁻).
The Ω-baryon mass is used in combination with dimensionless quantities (light meson masses and t₀) to set the lattice scale.
The CLS ensembles used are generated with 2+1 dynamical quark flavors along a trajectory of constant trace of the quark-mass matrix.
The N³LO SU(3)₈ chiral perturbation theory expressions describe the positive-parity Ω-baryon ground-state mass with high accuracy.

content_sections.I Introduction: Lattice quantum chromodynamics (lattice QCD) is now firmly established as the method of choice for calculations of the strong interaction in the non-perturbative regime, and the overall statistical resolution of lattice-determined quantities of interest is continuously improving. An example of such a quantity is the leading order hadronic contribution to the magnetic moment of the muon ((g−2)μ)subscript𝑔2𝜇((g-2)mu) citebib.bib1, where uncertainties of QCD determinations are falling below 1%percent11% citebib.bib2. In this “precision era” of Lattice QCD, sub-0.5%percent0.50.5% determinations of the scale in lattice simulations are of paramount importance to high-precision measurements, as they are vital to make sure the scale-setting isn’t the dominant source of uncertainty in the calculation citebib.bib3. In a minimal sense, for an nf=2+1subscript𝑛𝑓21nf=2+1-flavor simulation, 3 physical quantities need to be used to determine the “physical point”. These are thereby sacrificed as quantities that can be predicted by the theory. Typically, the iso-symmetric pion and kaon masses are used, plus a third quantity that sets the overall physical scale. In short, this procedure is referred to as “setting the scale”. Typically one chooses a particularly simple-to-measure physical quantity, which, together with the pion and kaon masses, then defines a “renormalisation trajectory” that sets the approach to the continuum. Of course any such choice has its own discretisation effects, so lattice spacing determinations from different quantities must differ. Historically, popular options have been the pion/kaon decay constants citebib.bib4, bib.bib5 or masses of light or light-strange baryons e. g. the Nucleon citebib.bib6, the Cascade citebib.bib7, or the fully-strange Omega baryon mass MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega citebib.bib8, bib.bib9. Even the splitting between the Υ′superscriptΥ′Upsilonprime and ΥΥUpsilon in lattice NRQCD has been used in the past citebib.bib10. Nowadays, an intermediate scale such as r0subscript𝑟0r0 citebib.bib11 or r1subscript𝑟1r1 citebib.bib12, t0subscript𝑡0t0 citebib.bib13, or w0subscript𝑤0w0 citebib.bib14 is often used, although its value in the continuum must still be inferred from another experimentally-measurable quantity. All of the determinations of the lattice scale have their own challenges – both in measurement, and regarding their associated systematic uncertainties. It is also possible to use suitable sets of physical quantities instead of a single observable. For instance, in citebib.bib15 the baryon octet and decuplet baryons masses were used simultaneously to set the scale. Whatever states, or combinations thereof, are used in the determination of the scale no longer are predictions of the theory. This makes quantities like the kaon decay constant fKsubscript𝑓𝐾fK, for which there is a phenomenological interest for tests of CKM-unitarity, undesirable as an observable for scale-setting. In this paper we will demonstrate that the I​(JP)=0​(3/2)+𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript32I(JP)=0(3/2)+ ΩΩOmega-baryon ground-state mass MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega is relatively straightforward to obtain with good precision. Subsequently the measured MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega can be used in combination with dimensionless quantities based on the light meson masses and t0subscript𝑡0t0 to set the scale. The ΩΩOmega-baryon provides a very enticing state for setting the scale as strange quark propagators are relatively cheap to compute in comparison to those of the light flavors. The noise to signal ratio is not large, no complicated improved currents and renormalisation is needed, and finite-volume effects are expected to be small. The ΩΩOmega in pure QCD is stable and a determination of the mass in lattice QCD is therefore unambiguous. As the ΩΩOmega has isospin 0, strong isospin-breaking effects appear in the sea rather than in the valence sector and are expected to be negligible; on top of this pure-QED effects due to the charged nature of the strange quarks are present but expected to be small citebib.bib16. The ΩΩOmega is very precisely known experimentally too, making it an ideal candidate for scale-setting. With this in mind, we will present a highly-accurate lattice-spacing and t0subscript𝑡0sqrtt0 determination using MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega determined on the Coordinated Lattice Simulations (CLS) ensembles citebib.bib17, bib.bib18. In the next section we will introduce our methodology, followed by a discussion of flavor-SU​SU3textSU (SU​fSUsubscript3𝑓textSUf) chiral fits in Section. We then proceed to present our ΩΩOmega-baryon masses in Section and the resulting determination of the lattice scale in Section. We also compare our determination to previous ones using some of the same ensembles along different renormalisation trajectories citebib.bib5, bib.bib7, bib.bib15. In addition, we use our determined lattice scales to compute the physical mass of the negative parity MΩ(3/2)−superscriptsubscript𝑀Ωsuperscript32MOmega(3/2)--baryon and present the results of this in Section. The calculated mass is consistent with the mass of the recently-discovered Ω​−Ωsuperscript2012Omega- citebib.bib19.
content_sections.II Methodology: We use the simple local operator for the ΩΩOmega (lower color, upper spin indices) with open spin index κ𝜅kappa, Ωiκ​(x)=ϵa​b​c​(saT​C​γi​sb)​scκ​(x),subscriptsuperscriptΩ𝜅𝑖𝑥subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑎𝑏𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑠𝑎𝑇𝐶subscript𝛾𝑖subscript𝑠𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑠𝜅𝑐𝑥Omegakappai(x)=epsilonabc(saTCgammaisb)skappac(x), focusing only on the polarisation-diagonal components citebib.bib8 to make the contraction particularly cheap. The parity of choice of choice is projected-out in the usual manner, CΩ±​(t−t′)=12​(1±γt)κ​κ′​13​∑i=x,y,z∑xL3Ωiκ​(x,t)​(Ωiκ′​(0,t′))†. superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡superscript𝑡′12superscriptplus-or-minus1subscript𝛾𝑡𝜅superscript𝜅′13subscript𝑖𝑥𝑦𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑥superscript𝐿3subscriptsuperscriptΩ𝜅𝑖𝑥𝑡superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptΩsuperscript𝜅′𝑖0superscript𝑡′†COmegapm(t-tprime)=frac12(1pmgammat)kappakappaprimefrac13sumi=x,y,zsumxL3Omegakappai(x,t)(Omegakappaprimei(0,tprime))dagger,. We perform an average over the forward and backward propagating states 1With particular care being taken when going through the temporal boundaries as anti-periodic boundary conditions are applied on the fermions in the solve. , with the appropriate parity projections to obtain a correlator with the correct JP=3/2±superscript𝐽𝑃3superscript2plus-or-minusJP=3/2pm quantum numbers. These forward and backward states are measured to be statistically quite independent and come only at the cost of more contractions (at least for the periodic gauge field ensembles). To ameliorate the cost of the creation of the deflated subspace used in our solver citebib.bib21 we will do as many solves as beneficial on each individual configuration. Here we invoke the Truncated Solver method citebib.bib22: For periodic ensembles in time we use translational-invariance in time and perform low-precision (sloppy) inversions on every timeslice (t′)superscript𝑡′(tprime), and one exact solve randomly chosen in the bulk. For the open boundaries we start sufficiently far from the boundary and average every forward-propagating correlator on every timeslice from there until a cutoff below Lt/2subscript𝐿𝑡2Lt/2 and likewise the backward propagating opposite-parity projected correlator from Lt−t′subscript𝐿𝑡superscript𝑡′Lt-tprime decreasing the source position. This also makes use of translational-invariance in time but restricted to the bulk away from the boundaries. We measure the negative-parity, JP=3/2−superscript𝐽𝑃3superscript2JP=3/2- state in a similar fashion, as it is only a trivial parity-projection of the basic contracted correlator. The one-end-trick (e. g. citebib.bib23) is not applicable to baryon correlators and simple generalizations suffer from a signal to noise problem as explained in citebib.bib24. Popular alternative choices are some form of point sources (with or without smearing) or Coulomb gauge-fixed wall sources citebib.bib25, bib.bib26. We will apply the latter option, which obviously requires us to fix the gauge, but yields a far superior volume-average. These have the limitation that the quarks carry a definite momentum but as everything we will consider will be at rest, thereby being no limitation for this work. Fixing to Coulomb gauge to high precision can be a numerically-cheap procedure if done using the Fourier-accelerated non-linear conjugate gradient algorithm of citebib.bib27, where an average gauge-fixing accuracy of Θ=10−14Θsuperscript1014Theta=10-14 can be achieved with negligible resources even for the largest volumes considered here (963×192superscript963192963times 192). The main mass-trajectory CLS uses keeps the trace of the quark-mass matrix constant. As the pion mass decreases the strange quark is made heavier, simultaneously the volume is enlarged to ensure mπ​L⪆4greater-than-or-approximately-equalssubscript𝑚𝜋𝐿4mpiLgtrapprox 4. Hence, at the physical pion-mass point, the ensembles are expected to provide the most precise data and to most strongly constrain our extrapolations. As gauge-fixed wall sources approach the plateau region from below for the ΩΩOmega and many other states beside pions (they are maximally asymmetric at source and sink), some care is needed in controlling excited-state contamination so that masses are not found to be lower than their asymptotic value. Empirically, we have found the best way to do this is to form a simple 2×2222times 2 generalised Pencil of Functions (PoF) citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30 matrix of the Wall-Point correlator CΩ±​(t)superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡COmegapm(t) MΩ±​(t)=(CΩ±​(t)CΩ±​(t+1)CΩ±​(t+1)CΩ±​(t+2)). superscriptsubscript𝑀Ωplus-or-minus𝑡matrixsuperscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡1superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡1superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡2MOmegapm(t)=beginpmatrixCOmegapm(t)COmegapm(t+1) COmegapm(t+1)COmegapm(t+2) endpmatrix. We solve this as a symmetric Generalised Eigenvalue Problem (GEVP) citebib.bib31, bib.bib32 at a fixed time τ0subscript𝜏0tau0 and use the eigenvectors at some later time τ𝜏tau to approximately diagonalise the resulting correlator matrix. We then take the diagonal components as the principal correlators. A simple, fully-correlated, single-exponential fit to the lowest-lying principal correlator is sufficient to determine the ground state ΩΩOmega-baryon mass precisely. Some attention is needed in changing the GEVP parameters τ0subscript𝜏0tau0 and τ𝜏tau, and increasing these for finer lattice spacings is necessary. (Similar results could also be obtained by increasing the spacing between elements creating the matrix. ) The PoF method yields a statistically-noisier result but removes a large amount of the excited-state contamination, which would usually cause the Wall-point correlator to approach from below. The application of the PoF is a trade-off as it allows for fits at short times albeit with data which is noisier than usual. The PoF also removes a lot of correlation within neighbouring points of the data making correlated fits easier. It also agrees well with a correlated two-exponential fit, with much less ambiguity on the ground-state’s mass as compared to multi-exponential fits which often show some lower fit-bound dependence. A comparison of these two approaches can be seen in Fig. for one of our ensembles; note that there is no reduction in statistical uncertainty but the PoF is less sensitive to the choice of fit range. Our final approach is quite similar to the scale-setting used in citebib.bib2, but in that work the authors used smeared point sources, missing out on the volume average from the wall source. The PoF is not ubiquitously effective, for instance it does not often work well for mesons; in citebib.bib33 we found it was effective for only one of our tetraquark operators, the one that looked like a compact diquark-antidiquark. This suggests that the method could have something to do with the spin-color nature of the operator and the compact nature of the state considered.
content_sections.III A flavor-SU​(3)SU3\\text{SU}(3) chir: We apply the chiral Lagrangian with three light flavours to compute the quark-mass dependence of the ΩΩOmega mass. Expressions for all baryon octet and decuplet masses have been documented in a series of previous works citebib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib15 where particular emphasis was put on the use of on-shell hadron masses in the one-loop contributions. In previous works citebib.bib38, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 such a framework lead to significantly-improved convergence properties of the chiral expansion. Moreover, with the use of on-shell masses a much-improved description of finite-box effects stemming from such loop terms was seen citebib.bib35. In particular, the well-known Lüscher type terms are contained in our expressions, as is important for most of the baryon decuplet states. Given such a framework in the isospin-limit, the set of baryon masses on a specified lattice ensemble would have to be determined as a solution of 8 coupled and non-linear equations. In this work we focus on the positive-parity ground-state ΩΩOmega-mass on CLS ensembles, where we take the lattice values of all hadron masses that enter the loop expressions, i. e. the pion and kaon masses as well as J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2 ΞΞXi masses. Instead of solving the aforementioned non-linear system we insert lattice values into the loop expressions and extract from there specific combinations of Low-Energy-Constants (LECs), and ultimately the lattice scale parameters. Our starting point is an expression for the ΩΩOmega-mass valid in a finite box at the NLO accuracy level. As will be illustrated below in Sec. an attempt fitting NLO does not suffice to arrive at the precision required for a faithful reproduction of the lattice data. We note that an extrapolation attempt using the chiral Lagrangian with two light flavours only citebib.bib39 is futile also in our case. In the following we denote with mπsubscript𝑚𝜋mpi, mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK and mηsubscript𝑚𝜂meta the masses of the Goldstone bosons at a given lattice scale “a” of a considered ensemble. Correspondingly, MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega, MΞsubscript𝑀ΞMXi and MΞ∗subscript𝑀superscriptΞMXi* are the baryon masses on that ensemble. Later on we will also consider such masses as expressed in units of 8​t08subscript𝑡08,t0, a popular choice in the lattice community. We fit our data in terms of the dimensionless quantities on each lattice ensemble. Labeling these for a particular state “Q” as, φQ=8​t0​mQ2. subscript𝜑𝑄8subscript𝑡0superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑄2varphiQ=8t0mQ2;. Our starting point will involve specific combinations of LECs used previously in citebib.bib15. Any application of the chiral Lagrangian requires the knowledge of the quark masses, mu,md,subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑚𝑑mu,md, and mssubscript𝑚𝑠ms, that characterize a given lattice ensemble. In principle, the π𝜋pi, K𝐾K, and η𝜂eta masses follow in terms of a set of relevant LECs. If the loop contributions to the meson masses are formulated in terms of on-shell masses, already the determination of the the π𝜋pi, K𝐾K, and η𝜂eta go in terms of the solution of a set of three coupled and nonlinear equations. Conversely, if π𝜋pi and K𝐾K masses are known on a given ensemble, we may directly compute the quark masses citebib.bib36, bib.bib15.
