posterior reconstruction of a given contribution to the correlation function (or g̊Asubscript̊𝑔𝐴mathringgA) and the “data” superscript denotes the numerical data. The full correlation between the numerical data and posteriors are used in these reconstruction. The 2pt + sc is the sum of the first two terms and the 3pt is the sum of all excited states. The FH(tsep,τc=1)A3/g̊AA3(trm sep,tauc=1)/mathringgA keys correspond to the same breakdown of various excited state contributions after they are passed through Eq. . From these plots, there are several observations and conclusions one can make: 1. There is a significant cancellation between the scattering excited states and two-point excited states; 2. The transition excited states are the dominant source of excited states and they are relatively suppressed in FHA3​(tsep,τc)subscriptFHsubscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sepsubscript𝜏𝑐rm FHA3(trm sep,tauc) as compared to RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2); 3. The total excited state contamination at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm is ≈8%absentpercent8approx 8% for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2) and ≈1%absentpercent1approx 1% for FHA3​(tsep,τc)subscriptFHsubscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sepsubscript𝜏𝑐rm FHA3(trm sep,tauc). In Eq. we see, from the proportionality of the nthsuperscript𝑛thnrm th excited state to gn​nΓ−g00Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔00ΓgnnGamma-g00Gamma, an explicit cancellation between the scattering (3pt sc) and two-point excited state (2pt es) contamination. For this cancellation to be significant, as observed in the posterior determination of the two classes of excited states, one explanation is that gn​nΓ≈g00Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔00ΓgnnGammaapprox g00Gamma for all n𝑛n. For the vector current, we know that in infinite volume, the vector operator measures the iso-vector charge of the system, which in the isospin limit, is exactly equal to g00V4=1superscriptsubscript𝑔00subscript𝑉41g00V4=1 (up to renormalization). It has further been demonstrated that in the forward scattering limit, the finite volume interaction amplitudes conspire with the finite volume Lellouch-Lüscher factors to ensure that gn​nV4=g00V4superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛subscript𝑉4superscriptsubscript𝑔00subscript𝑉4gnnV4=g00V4 for two-particle statescitebib.bib103. This result, while not surprising, demonstrates charge conservation in finite volume by allowing one to relate n𝑛n-point Greens functions to (n−1)𝑛1(n-1)-point Greens functions through the Ward-Takahashi equations. In the case of the axial-vector matrix element, it is plausible that in the forward scattering limit, the corrections to such a relation arising from the partially-conserved nature of the axial-vector current would also vanish, such that in this limit, the Lellouch-Lüscher factors are similarly removed. For example, for the nucleon, we know the induced pseudo-scalar form factor, which would provide a correction to the Ward-Takahashi equations, vanishes in the forward limit. We leave a detailed study of this question to future work. In the left panel of Fig. we see that the scattering excited state contribution (the upper (red) band/data) and the contribution from the excited states coming from the two-point function (the middle (purple) band/data) are roughly equal and opposite in sign. The hatched curve, which is mostly white with gray hash lines, is the sum of these two contributions and it lies between them. We observe that the sum of these two sources of excited state contributions decay to a 1% correction (the horizontal dashed (red) lines) at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm. In contrast, the transition (3pt tr) excited states depicted by the lowest (blue) band/data do not decay to the 1% level until tsep≈2.2subscript𝑡sep2.2trm sepapprox 2.2fm. In the right panel of Fig. we show the same excited state breakdown for the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function. The sign of the scattering and two-point excited state contributions both change compared to RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2). This sign change can be understood from Eq. . It is also interesting to note that the magnitude of the transition excited state contributions at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm go from ≈7.5%absentpercent7.5approx 7.5% for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2) to ≈2.5%absentpercent2.5approx 2.5% for FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1). Finally, for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2), the sum of the scattering and two-point excited state contributions is the same sign as the sum of transition excited states, while for FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1) it is the opposite sign at intermediate and large tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep. Thus, the total excited state contamination decays to the 1% level for the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm while this does not happen until tsep>2subscript𝑡sep2trm sep>2fm for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2). There is no proof that this fortunate cancellation must happen. However, an examination of our results in Ref. citebib.bib31 shows a consistent picture that the excited state contamination of the FH(tsep,τc=none)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=rm none) correlation function decays to the 1% level by roughly 1 fm over a broad range of pion masses, 130≲mπ≲400less-than-or-similar-to130subscript𝑚𝜋less-than-or-similar-to400130lesssim mpilesssim 400MeV.
content_sections.III Lattice Calculation III.4 Comparis: In this section, we perform the correlator analysis on late time separation data, tsep=[10,12,14]subscript𝑡sep101214trm sep=[10,12,14], which is directly comparable to the results in Refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib34 that were performed on the same ensembles, and otherwise mimicking the more common strategy used for example in Refs. citebib.bib18, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib37. The results are obtained with a simultaneous fit to the two- and three-point functions using the same priors as our main fit, given in App. . We also demonstrate the sensitivity of the posterior distributions of the ground state parameters to changes in the input priors as in App. . Because we have only three values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep, we use a minimal number of excited states: with one excited state, there is only one degree of freedom in describing the tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep dependence of RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau), after which, the analysis relies upon the priors for the excited state matrix elements. With the quark smearing we have used we find that we must apply a relatively aggressive truncation on the current insertion time in order for the model to describe the numerical data, restricting the analysis to the “center” of the current insertion time τ𝜏tau. We illustrate the region of τ𝜏tau used for each tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep in Fig. and depict the stability of the ground state axial matrix element in Fig. . The optimal result is chosen as the fit with the largest amount of numerical data while maintaining a good fit quality (cfr. the lower panel of Fig. ). We refer to the optimal choice of data included for this 2-state model fit as τinc=τincoptsubscript𝜏incsuperscriptsubscript𝜏incopttaurm inc=taurm incrm opt, but we also show results for the ground state posteriors when one more (τincopt+1superscriptsubscript𝜏incopt1taurm incrm opt+1) or one less (τincopt−1superscriptsubscript𝜏incopt1taurm incrm opt-1) value of τ𝜏tau is included in the fit. While not depicted, the ground state posteriors are also stable under variation of the choice of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintrm seprm min in the two-point function. We observe that for this specific dataset, the late-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep result is in ≈2​σabsent2𝜎approx 2sigma tension with the result from the full analysis that includes more values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep, and is clearly stable over a variety of data truncations and model variations. This is indicative that late-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep data can be subject to correlated fluctuations which are difficult to identify without having results at many values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep. The same systematic effect can be seen in a simpler case using a fit to the two-point correlation function. In Fig. , we show the result of the ground state energy extracted from the two-point correlation function under varying tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintrm seprm min. We highlight the best fit ground-state energy, which is supported by a robust plateau in the dimension of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintrm seprm min with high Q𝑄Q-value. However, we see that at late time beyond 1fm, a second plateau develops approximately two standard deviations above our best fit. This second plateau cannot be physical and simply reached later in time due to latent excited state contamination, as the 2-pt correlator is positive definite and therefore required to approach the ground state from above. This second “stable” plateau is also the logical result for a one-state fit. Analogous to the three-point analysis, if the model fails to describe excited-state contaminations, the model must extract ground-state parameters at late time. These values are then sensitive to uncontrolled statistical fluctuations. Also note that we observe the fluctuation in the effective mass occurs approximately 0.2fm later than the matrix elements, see the top panel of Fig. in App. . Similar to the three-point analysis, in the absence of a more holistic view as granted by analyzing more source-sink separation data, there is no measure on the size of how large the potential underestimation of errors are. Without using more than three values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep, it is not possible to identify if one is susceptible to such a fluctuation, and therefore, it is not possible to fully quantify the uncertainty on the posterior distribution of the ground state parameters.
content_sections.III Lattice Calculation III.5 Compariso: In this final section, we compare our result with that from Ref. citebib.bib31 on the same a09m310 ensemble. Those results were obtained using the Feynman-Hellmann method as described in Ref. citebib.bib48, which is the same as Eq. with the sum over current insertion time running over the full time extent, including contributions from contact operators at τ=[0,tsep]𝜏0subscript𝑡septau=[0,trm sep] and from out-of-time region, τtsep𝜏subscript𝑡septau>trm sep. We applied a two-state model and a frequentist analysis on the data from the two-point and Feynman-Hellmann correlation functions. In Fig. we show the extracted value of g̊Asubscript̊𝑔𝐴mathringgA with three different fully-correlated analysis strategies: 23s : Fit to C2​(tsep)subscript𝐶2subscript𝑡sepC2(trm sep), RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau) and FH(tsep,τc=1)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=1); 2s : Fit to C2​(tsep)subscript𝐶2subscript𝑡sepC2(trm sep) and FH(tsep,τc=1)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=1); 23 : Fit to C2​(tsep)subscript𝐶2subscript𝑡sepC2(trm sep) and RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau). The horizontal gray band is the result from Ref. citebib.bib31. These results show the consistency between the four computational and analysis strategies. Even though the FH(tsep,τc)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc) correlation functions are constructed from the RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau) correlation functions, they are subject to a different pattern of excited state contamination (cfr. Sec. ). Therefore it is useful to include them in the fully-correlated analysis. The combined fit yields a robust and consistent fitting strategy and precise extractions of hadronic matrix elements at zero-momentum transfer. In Fig. , we present the posterior correlation function on top of the numerical data for both the results in Ref. citebib.bib31 as well as from the FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1) dataset in the present work. Using the bootstrap results, the correlated ratio of gAsubscript𝑔𝐴gA determined previously and now is gA​Changetal. gA​[present​work]=1.009​,subscript𝑔𝐴Changetal. subscript𝑔𝐴delimited-presentwork1.00931fracgAtextcite[cite]@@bibrefAuthors Phrase1YearPhrase2Chang:2018uxx@@citephrase(@@citephrase)gA[rm presentwork]=1.009,, showing the statistical consistency of the ground state matrix element extracted from these two different methods on the same ensemble. The FH(tsep,τc=none)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=rm none) dataset from Ref. citebib.bib31 is observed to have significantly less excited state contamination at early tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep as compared to FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1). This is what enabled a two-state analysis in Ref. citebib.bib31. The combined fit (23s) in the present work enables a determination of significantly more excited state parameters through the precise early-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep data. Moreover, as shown in this section, a confident extraction of the ground state posterior distributions is attained: as depicted in the figure, both strategies yield precise and consistent extractions of the large-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep extrapolation of the results. The former result is more economical for obtaining a precise value of g̊Asubscript̊𝑔𝐴mathringgA, while the method in this work also enables a determination of non-zero momentum transfer results which can be used to determine the form factors.
content_sections.IV Observations and Conclusions: In this work, we have computed the three-point correlation functions that are used to determine the nucleon axial charge for 13 values of the source-sink separation time in the range tsep≈0.17−1.22subscript𝑡sep0.171.22trm sepapprox 0.17-1.22fm on an ensemble with a≈0.09𝑎0.09aapprox 0.09fm and mπ≈310subscript𝑚𝜋310mpiapprox 310MeV. This large numerical dataset (Fig. ) has enabled us to robustly determine the ground state mass and matrix elements with a fully-quantified systematic uncertainty arising from excited states. Important to this study is the use of a fully-correlated global minimization of the two-point and respective three-point correlations functions. We were further able to quantify the excited state contribution to the correlation function with greater detail than has been previously achieved in the literature. We were able to demonstrate that the ground state parameters are stable against the model of excited states (Eq. and Fig. ), the number of excited states used in the analysis, and truncations of either small tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep or large tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep results as well as through the use of only even or odd values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep results (Fig. ). A re-writing of the spectral decomposition of the three-point correlation functions revealed a prospective cancellation between two classes of excited states arising from the n𝑛n-to-n𝑛n scattering states and those arising from the two-point correlation function used to construct RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau), Eq. (there is an exact cancellation for the vector matrix element for which gn​nV4=g00V4=1superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛subscript𝑉4superscriptsubscript𝑔00subscript𝑉41gnnV4=g00V4=1 up to renormalization). For our calculation, this cancellation seems to materialize for gAsubscript𝑔𝐴gA (Fig. ) leaving the dominant excited state contributions to be the n𝑛n-to-m𝑚m transition terms. In the standard fixed source-sink separation method, the excited state contributions do not decay down to the 1% contamination level until tsep≳2greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑡sep2trm sepgtrsim 2fm while in the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function, Eq. , they decay to the 1% level at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm. We further observed that the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function constructed with a sum over the entire time extent of the latticecitebib.bib48 (FH(tsep,τc=none)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=rm none)), rather than just between the source and the sink (FH(tsep,τc=1)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=1)), leads to an even further suppression of excited state contamination (Fig. ). This stronger cancellation of excited states enabled us to compute gAsubscript𝑔𝐴gA with a ≈1%absentpercent1approx 1% uncertainty by utilizing early-time datacitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, which demonstrated this higher suppression of excited states over a broad range of pion masses, 130≲mπ≲400less-than-or-similar-to130subscript𝑚𝜋less-than-or-similar-to400130lesssim mpilesssim 400MeV. By utilizing a large number of early to mid-time data, one is able to detect if the late-time data is subject to a correlated fluctuation which might otherwise bias the determination of the ground state matrix elements (Figs. and ). With only three values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep at late time, it is not possible to perform such a data truncation study that could identify this issue. We have found that including the Feynman-Hellmann correlation functions in a global fully correlated analysis, even though they are constructed from the three-point functions as in Eq. and are highly correlated with RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau), improves the stability and precision of the extracted ground state parameters (Fig. ). This improvement arises because the excited states present themselves differently in the two sets of matrix-element correlation functions. Even though the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function has greater noise than the standard three-point correlator at equal tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep and statistics (compare, for example, tsep=1subscript𝑡sep1trm sep=1fm. in Fig. ), the more rapid decay of excited states demonstrated in Sec. means that this strategy mitigates the exponential degradation of the signal-to-noise of the nucleon’s two- and three-point correlation functions as tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep is increased, which requires exponentially more computational resources to control the stochastic precision. The strategy we present in this work offers a more economical method of obtaining the ground state matrix elements than that which is more commonly advocated for in the literature, which is to use high-statistics calculations at tsep≈1−2subscript𝑡sep12trm sepapprox 1-2fm or larger. In future work, we will investigate the same strategy for non-zero momentum transfer correlation functions which are used to determine the nucleon form factors. Data availability: The computations were performed utilizing LaLiBecitebib.bib104 which utilizes the Chroma software suitecitebib.bib92 with QUDA solverscitebib.bib105, bib.bib106 and HDF5citebib.bib107 for I/Ocitebib.bib108. They were efficiently managed with METAQcitebib.bib109, bib.bib110 and status of tasks logged with EspressoDBcitebib.bib111. The final extrapolation analysis utilized gvarcitebib.bib112 and lsqfitcitebib.bib113. The analysis and data for this work can be found at . Acknowledgements. We thank O. Bär, R. Briceño, R. Gupta, M. Hansen and A. Jackura for enlightening conversations and correspondence. We thank B. Hörz for help cross-checking the three-point code in LaLiBe and generating some of the results. We thank the MILC Collaboration for use of their HISQ gauge ensembles. Computing time for this work was provided through the Innovative and Novel Computational Impact on Theory and Experiment (INCITE) program and the LLNL Multiprogrammatic and Institutional Computing program for Grand Challenge allocations on the LLNL supercomputers. This research utilized the NVIDIA GPU-accelerated Summit supercomputer at Oak Ridge Leadership Computing Facility at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory, which is supported by the Office of Science of the U. Department of Energy under Contract No. DE-AC05-00OR22725 as well as the Lassen supercomputer at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, which is operated by the National Nuclear Security Administration of the U. S. Department of Energy under Contract No. DE-AC52-07NA27344. This work was supported in part by the Berkeley Physics International Education Program (JH), the Berkeley Laboratory Undergraduate Research Program (IC); the NVIDIA Corporation (MAC), the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation through a Feodor Lynen Research Fellowship (CK), the U. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Nuclear Physics under Award Numbers DE-AC02-05CH11231 (CCC, CK, AWL), DE-AC52-07NA27344 (DAB, DH, ASG, PV), DE-FG02-93ER-40762 (EB), DE-AC05-06OR23177 (CJM); DE-SC00046548 (ASM); the DOE Early Career Award Program (AWL), and the U. all_papers.title:
$\bar b \bar b u d$ and $\bar b \bar b u s$ tetraquarks from lattice QCD
  using symmetric correlation matrices with both local and scattering
  interpolating operators
all_papers.abstract:
We study the $\bar b \bar b u d$ tetraquark with quantum numbers $I(J^P) =
0(1^+)$ as well as the $\bar b \bar b u s$ tetraquark with quantum numbers $J^P
= 1^+$ using lattice QCD. We improve on existing work by including both local
and scattering interpolating operators on both sides of the correlation
functions and use symmetric correlation matrices. This allows not only a
reliable determination of the energies of QCD-stable tetraquark ground states,
but also of low-lying excited states, which are meson-meson scattering states.
The latter is particularly important for future finite-volume scattering
analyses. Here, we perform chiral and continuum extrapolations of just the
ground-state energies, for which finite-volume effects are expected to be
small. Our resulting tetraquark binding energies, $-100 \pm
10\:^{+36}_{-51}\:\:{\rm MeV}$ for $\bar b \bar b u d$ and $-30 \pm
3\:^{+11}_{-31}\:\:{\rm MeV}$ for $\bar b \bar b u s$, are consistent with
other recent lattice-QCD predictions.

qualitative: The study employs symmetric correlation matrices constructed from both local and scattering interpolating operators to improve the determination of tetraquark energy levels in lattice QCD.

Including both local and scattering operators on both sides of the correlation functions enables a reliable extraction of not only the ground state but also low-lying excited states, which are identified as meson-meson scattering states.
The use of symmetric correlation matrices enhances the signal-to-noise ratio and allows for a more robust separation of energy levels, particularly important for finite-volume scattering analyses.
Excited-state contributions are captured through multi-state fits to the two-point correlation functions, with single- and two-state fits used to extract energy levels.
Finite-volume effects are expected to be small for ground-state energies, justifying the use of chiral and continuum extrapolations on these states alone.
The presence of scattering states in the spectrum is crucial for future studies of scattering amplitudes and resonance properties in the tetraquark sector.
quantitative: Ground-state binding energy for $\bar b \bar b u d$: $-100 \pm 10\:^{+36}_{-51}\ \text{MeV}$
Ground-state binding energy for $\bar b \bar b u s$: $-30 \pm 3\:^{+11}_{-31}\ \text{MeV}$
The analysis includes chiral and continuum extrapolations of ground-state energies, with finite-volume effects considered negligible for these states.
The correlation matrices are symmetric and constructed using both local and scattering interpolating operators on both sides of the two-point functions.
The study performs single- and two-state fits to the two-point correlation functions to extract energy levels, including both ground and excited states.

Constantia Alexandrou, Jacob Finkenrath, Theodoros Leontiou, Stefan
  Meinel, Martin Pflaumer, Marc Wagner

content_sections.I Introduction: Mesons, which are hadrons with integer spin, are typically composed of one valence quark and one valence antiquark. However, they can as well contain two valence quarks and two valence antiquarks. Such exotic states are called tetraquarks1In the literature, the term “tetraquark” is somewhat ambiguous. In certain papers it exclusively refers to a diquark-antidiquark structure, while in other papers it is used more generally for arbitrary bound states and resonances with a strong four-quark component, including, e. g. , mesonic molecules. Throughout this paper we follow the latter convention. . In this work we use lattice QCD to study two particular antiheavy-antiheavy-light-light four-quark systems, the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) and the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark with quantum numbers JP=1+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript1JP=1+. These tetraquarks are expected to be QCD-stable (i. e. , have a mass lower than the sum of the masses of the lightest possible strong-decay products, a pseudoscalar and a vector heavy-light meson in this case) for sufficiently large heavy-quark mass citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3. Accurately predicting the binding energies at the physical b𝑏b-quark mass is quite challenging. Approaches using potential models, effective field theories, and QCD sum rules citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib3, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32 gave a wide spread of results for the binding energy of the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system, and did not all predict the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus mass to be below threshold (see, e. g. , the summary plots in Fig. in the conclusions section of this paper). On the other hand, studies using lattice QCD consistently find both the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquarks to be QCD-stable. While early investigations based on static potentials from lattice QCD and the Born-Oppenheimer approximation slightly underestimated the binding energy of the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, more recent rigorous full lattice-QCD simulations citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44 obtained binding energies of 𝒪​(100​MeV)𝒪100MeVO(100,textMeV) for the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud tetraquark with I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) and 𝒪​(50​MeV)𝒪50MeVO(50,textMeV) for the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus tetraquark with JP=1+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript1JP=1+, with good agreement within statistical and systematic uncertainties among the more advanced calculations. There are further antiheavy-antiheavy-light-light systems that are promising with respect to the existence of tetraquark bound states or resonances, but for whom the situation is less clear. Two such candidates are the b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud systems with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(0+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript0I(JP)=0(0+) and I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+). Independent groups carrying out full lattice-QCD simulations have arrived at different conclusions. The existence of a strong-interaction-stable b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud tetraquark with I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) was initially reported in Ref. citebib.bib45 but later revoked in Ref. citebib.bib46. In Ref. citebib.bib42, we did not find evidence for QCD-stable b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud bound states, but could not rule out shallow bound states. Other authors found an indication for a b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud bound state with I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) below threshold citebib.bib47, bib.bib48. The conclusions of these studies were based only on the finite-volume ground-state energy. In a recent project carried out in parallel to this work we revisited both b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud systems taking into account also finite-volume energies of low-lying excitations. We found strong indication for very shallow bound states citebib.bib49. Another interesting system is b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(1−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1-). This system has not yet been investigated in full lattice QCD, but has been explored in the Born-Oppenheimer approximation using lattice-QCD static potentials. While, at first, a tetraquark resonance was predicted in a crude way (completely neglecting effects of the heavy-quark spins) citebib.bib50, a refined study that is still ongoing suggests that neither a bound state nor a resonance exist in the region near the B​B𝐵𝐵BB threshold citebib.bib51. Given that the Born-Oppenheimer approximation with lattice-QCD static potentials seems to underestimate the binding energy of QCD-stable antiheavy-antiheavy-light-light systems, the existence of a I​(JP)=0​(1−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1-), b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud resonance is not yet excluded and should be investigated in full lattice QCD. To rigorously search for tetraquark resonances or shallow bound states in full lattice QCD, determinations of the meson-meson scattering amplitudes both below and above the relevant thresholds are mandatory. Such computations are typically based on Lüscher’s method citebib.bib52 and its generalizations (see the review in Ref. citebib.bib53), and require a precise computation of all relevant low-lying finite-volume energy levels. To be able to resolve all of these energy levels, one needs a sufficiently large basis of interpolating operators that must be able to capture the spatial structures of all relevant states. This requires the use of both local operators, in which the four quarks are placed at or near the same spatial point and jointly projected to the desired total momentum, and scattering operators, which are constructed from products of two individually momentum-projected meson operators. Previous full lattice-QCD studies of antiheavy-antiheavy-light-light systems containing anti-bottom quarks used either exclusively local operators citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib48 or a combination of local and scattering operators with the latter employed only at the sink of the correlation functions citebib.bib40, bib.bib42. In this paper (and in our very recent work citebib.bib49 on the b¯​c¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑐𝑢𝑑barbbarcud system, which we carried out in parallel) we implemented and used, for the first time in the bottom sector, local and scattering interpolating operators both at the source and at the sink of correlation functions, leading to symmetric correlation matrices containing both types of operators. Here we focus again on the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) and the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus system with quantum numbers JP=1+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript1JP=1+. We demonstrate that we are able to extract the energy levels of several meson-meson scattering states in addition to the ground-state energy below the lowest meson-meson threshold (however, in contrast to Ref. citebib.bib49, here we only included scattering operators with vanishing relative momenta and were therefore only able to resolve the subset of scattering states of that type). We use a lattice setup quite different from our previous lattice-QCD studies citebib.bib40, bib.bib42 of antiheavy-antiheavy-light-light systems. The reason is that in our previous work we could reuse existing domain-wall point-to-all propagators previously generated for other projects citebib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57. However, the computation of symmetric correlation matrices with both local and scattering operators is not possible with only point-to-all propagators. One additionally needs stochastic timeslice-to-all propagators for the correlation matrix elements with scattering operators at the source. Technical details can be found, for example, in Ref. citebib.bib58, which includes a discussion of the one-end-trick that is essential for computing matrix elements with stochastic propagators. Since we had to recompute light and strange quark propagators, we decided to use a computationally cheaper mixed-action setup previously employed by the PNDME collaboration citebib.bib59, bib.bib60: clover-improved Wilson (with HYP link smearing) valence u𝑢u, d𝑑d, and s𝑠s quarks on the (2+1+1)211(2+1+1)-flavor gauge-link ensembles generated by the MILC collaboration using the highly improved staggered quark (HISQ) action citebib.bib61. As before, we use lattice NRQCD for the heavy b¯¯𝑏barb quarks. While it is certainly interesting to revisit the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud system with quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+) and the b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus system with quantum numbers JP=1+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript1JP=1+, now for the first time with a combination of local and scattering operators and symmetric correlation matrices, the main motivation of this work is rather to explore and prepare computational methods suited to study antiheavy-antiheavy-light-light tetraquark resonances. As discussed above, this requires a precise determination of energy levels of low-lying scattering states. We demonstrate that this works well with our setup, and also present evidence that it is not feasible without scattering operators. This article is organized in the following way. In we briefly summarize our lattice setup. In we discuss the interpolating operators for the two systems we investigate and the corresponding correlation functions. In we provide our lattice results for the energies of the pseudoscalar and vector B𝐵B and Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs mesons and determine their kinetic masses from the momentum dependence of these energies. In we present the numerical results for the low-lying finite-volume energy levels of the b¯​b¯​u​d¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑑barbbarbud and b¯​b¯​u​s¯𝑏¯𝑏𝑢𝑠barbbarbus four-quark systems. We also explore the importance of each of our interpolating operators, which provides certain insights concerning the structure of the low-lying states. In we comment on finite-volume effects and the left-hand cut issue. Our extrapolations of the binding energies to the physical pion mass and continuum limit are discussed in . Finally, we summarize the main points of our work in and give a brief outlook. Note that results obtained at an early stage of this project were presented at the Lattice 2022 conference citebib.bib62.
content_sections.II Lattice setup: We use (2+1+1)211(2+1+1)-flavor gauge-link ensembles generated by the MILC collaboration using the highly improved staggered quark (HISQ) action and a one-loop Symanzik improved gauge action (see Ref. citebib.bib61 for details). The main properties of the seven ensembles, which differ in the lattice spacing, the spatial volume, and the pion mass, are collected in Table. The relative lattice spacings were determined using r1/asubscript𝑟1𝑎r1/a, which is related to the static quark-antiquark force F​(r)𝐹𝑟F(r) and defined via r12​F​(r1)=1superscriptsubscript𝑟12𝐹subscript𝑟11r12F(r1)=1; the physical value r1=0.3106​​​​fmsubscript𝑟10.31068144fmr1=0.3106,textfm was obtained through an analysis of pseudoscalar decay constants citebib.bib61, bib.bib63. We note that the ensembles a12m220S, a12m220 and a12m220L have the same gauge coupling and bare light quark mass and only differ in the number of lattice sites in the spatial directions. Thus, they are particularly suited to investigate the volume dependence of energy eigenvalues. For the valence u𝑢u, d𝑑d, and s𝑠s quarks, we use the Wilson-clover action with HYP-smeared gauge links to suppress exceptional configurations that can appear in a mixed-action setup (see the detailed discussion in SectionII. A in Ref. citebib.bib59). This mixed-action setup was tested and successfully used by the PNDME collaboration in the context of computations of isovector and isoscalar tensor charges of the nucleon citebib.bib59, bib.bib60. We use the values for the bare quark masses and the Sheikholeslami-Wohlert coefficient tuned and provided by the PNDME collaboration (see Table as well as TableII of Ref. citebib.bib59 and TableI and TableII of Ref. citebib.bib60). The corresponding valence-quark pion masses are listed in Table and quite similar to their counterparts computed within the unitary setup. A similar statement holds for the kaon masses, where the s𝑠s-quark mass is close to the physical s𝑠s-quark mass for all seven ensembles. The b𝑏b quarks appear only as valence quarks and are implemented using lattice NRQCD citebib.bib65. We use the same order-v4superscript𝑣4v4 action as in Ref. citebib.bib64. In contrast to the light and strange valence-quark action, the gauge links in the NRQCD action are not smeared. The matching coefficients of the kinetic terms, c1subscript𝑐1c1, c5subscript𝑐5c5, and c6subscript𝑐6c6, include one-loop radiative corrections, while the other matching coefficients are set to their tree-level values. All parameters were taken from TablesII and III in Ref. citebib.bib64 and are also reproduced here in Table.
